segments.s657-g414: کنام پلنگان و شیران شود continues the result clause: کنام (literally 'bosom/seat') here means 'abode/haunt' or 'the place of', followed by the plural animates پلنگان و شیران ('leopards and lions'). شود repeats the subjunctive/copula 'become', so the whole = 'become the haunt of leopards and lions.'
segments.s657-g415: بپرهیز is an imperative 'refrain / beware'. ازین جنگ = 'from this war' (از 'from' + ین as an orthographic variant of این). The coordinate و + آویختن links a second verbal noun/infinitive 'and clinging' (آویختن used in the sense 'to cling to/attach oneself to'), so the speaker orders: avoid the war and the act of clinging.
segments.s657-g416: به بیداد بر خیره خون ریختن strings together prepositional and verbal nouns: به بیداد = 'to/into injustice' (meaning 'attachment to tyranny'), بر خیره = 'upon the astonished/stunned' (بر 'upon' + خیره 'astonished'), and خون ریختن is the compound verbal noun 'to spill/shed blood'. Grammatically these infinitival/nominal verbs form the objects of بپرهیز (i.e. things to avoid).
segments.s657-g273: که begins a relative/connecting clause ('that' / 'who'). خواهید is 2nd-person plural present/future (here expressing desire/intention: 'you wish' or 'you will'), and برخویشتن is a prepositional phrase (بر + خویشتن) meaning 'upon/for yourselves'; پادشا is the archaic spelling of 'king' (پادشاه) and serves as the complement of the verb. Word order is verb → (prepositional) complement, typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s657-g274: که here continues/introduces the subordinate explanatory clause ('as' / 'which'). دانید is 2nd-person plural present 'you know' (used parenthetically: 'as you know'). زین is an archaic/contractional preposition (≈ از این 'from these') introducing the source, and دوجوان پارسا is a noun phrase 'two pious youths' with the adjective پارسا following the head (typical adjective-after-noun order).
segments.s657-g275: فروماندند is past tense, 3rd-person plural 'they remained / stayed behind'; اندر ان ('اندران') is a locative compound meaning 'among them' or 'among those'; موبدان is the plural of موبد (mōbed), 'the priests', and is the noun governed by the locative phrase. The verb precedes the locative phrase, a common poetic order.
segments.s657-g276: بزرگان is a plural noun 'the great ones' (appositive/coordination). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. بیدار دل is a compound epithet meaning 'awake/alert of heart' (بیدار modifying دل); بخردان is plural/collective meaning 'the wise' (here in apposition with بزرگان). The adjectives and epithets follow the nouns in this poetic sequence.
segments.s657-g277: نشسته is a past-participial/predicative form 'seated' or 'sitting'; همی is the habitual/continuous particle used in older Persian (here giving a progressive sense: 'were sitting'). دوجوان is the subject 'the two youths', and بر دو تخت is a prepositional phrase 'upon two thrones' (preposition بر + numeral + تخت).
segments.s657-g278: بگفت is the narrative/archaic past form 'said' (often used in epic diction). دو فرزانه نیکبخت is the nominative phrase 'two wise, fortunate ones' (فرزانه = 'wise/sage', نیکبخت = 'fortunate/blessed') and names the speakers; adjectives follow the nouns in this order.
segments.s657-g279: بدانست is past tense 3rd-person singular 'he/one knew it' (the prefix بـ is the emphatic/narrative form). شهری is a relational adjective/noun 'of the city' or 'the city', و هم is the conjunction plus 'also' (هم adds 'also/together'), and لشکری is a noun/adjective referring to the army or soldiery—together these form the objects/fields the subject knew.
segments.s657-g280: کزان is a relative prepositional compound ('from which' / 'whence'). کارجنگ is a compound noun literally 'the affair/work of war' (کـار + جنگ). آید is the present/future verb 'comes/will come' (here used in a prophetic/gnomic sense), and و داوری links 'and judgment' as a parallel noun—so the clause says that from those (city and army) will arise war and adjudication.
segments.s657-g673: برآنی is an archaic/adverbial compound (بر + آنی) meaning 'in view of that / since that'; it introduces a reason. که is the relativizing/connecting particle 'that/which' linking the following clause. از من شدی is a past, 2nd-person construction ('you became/are') with the preposition از marking source/agent ('by me' / 'from me'), and بی‌گزند is a predicative adjective 'unharmed' describing the subject.
segments.s657-g674: دلت را is 'your heart' with the possessive enclitic -ت on دل and the object marker را. به زنار افسون is a prepositional phrase 'to/with the girdle of enchantment' (به + noun + noun modifier). مبند is a negative imperative (2nd person singular) meaning 'do not bind' (مـ-prefix + بند). The clause orders not to fasten the heart metaphorically.
segments.s657-g675: به آتش + شدن uses به with a noun to indicate being in/into the fire; شوی is 2nd-person singular of شدن ('you become' / 'you will be') used here as a simple present/future sense. ناگهان is an adverb 'suddenly' and سوخته is a past-participle/adjectival complement 'burnt' describing the resultant state.
segments.s657-g676: روان ('soul, spirit') + آژده ('startled') is a noun + adjective phrase 'the startled soul'. چشمها دوخته is a verblike adjectival construction: چشمها (the eyes) + دوخته (lit. 'sewn', here 'fixed/fastened') — i.e. 'the eyes were fixed' or 'fixed their gaze'. The subject is understood from the preceding clause.
segments.s657-g677: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the simple past 'he heard'. گو here functions as a poetic/verbal modifier (literally 'said'/'so-called') attached to the following noun phrase and links the reported speech; آن پیام درشت = 'that harsh/rude message'. Together: 'When he heard that harsh message...'.
segments.s657-g678: دلش is the subject 'his heart' (دل + possessive -ش). راز مهر برادر is a genitive construction 'the secret of the brother's love' (راز = object/theme; مهر برادر = 'love/affection of (a) brother'). بشست is the simple past 'washed' (he/it washed away). Grammatically, دلش performs the action on راز مهر برادر.
segments.s657-g679: دلش is 'his heart'. زان is an archaic contraction of از آن ('from that' / 'because of that') and here takes سخن ('speech/remark') as its complement: 'from that speech'. گشت is the past of گشتن/'became' and اندوهگین is the resultant predicate adjective 'sorrowful' ('became sorrowful').
segments.s657-g680: به فرزانه گفت is 'he said to the sage' (به + indirect object + گفت). این شگفتی ببین is direct speech/imperative: این 'this', شگفتی 'wonder/marvel' (object), and ببین is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'see/behold'. Note: the listed sense entries for گفت did not include 'he said' exactly, so sense index is marked -1.
segments.s657-g105: که introduces the interrogative clause here (classical/poetic usage: 'which...'); ازماکدامست is a fused/contracted interrogative phrase (از ما کدام است) meaning 'which of us is', with the copula -است implied in the fused form. شایسته‌تر is the comparative adjective ('more worthy/ deserving') agreeing with that implied subject and completes the question.
segments.s657-g106: به دل places the evaluation 'in respect/of the heart' (به = 'in/to/regard to' + دل = 'heart, mind'); برتر and بایسته‌تر are comparative adjectives ('more superior/better' and 'more fitting/proper') joined by و نیز ('and also'), so the segment lists two comparative qualities that answer the question 'which of us...'.
segments.s657-g107: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (poetic word order places the verb before the subject); مادر is the subject 'the mother'; به هر دو پسر is an indirect-object phrase 'to both sons' (به + هر دو پسر).
segments.s657-g108: که تا introduces a purposive/subordinate clause ('that/so that'); تا carries the sense 'so that/in order that'. از شما باکه یابم هنر literally 'from you, by whom I may find/obtain skill' — باکه is the interrogative 'by/with whom?', یابم is 1st-person singular subjunctive/present used in purpose ('that I may find'), and هنر is the object 'skill/art'. The whole clause expresses the mother's aim to discover which son possesses skill.
segments.s657-g109: This is an asyndetic list of virtues. خردمندی = 'wisdom/prudence'; ورای here should be read as و + رای ('and counsel/opinion') — a poetic fusion common in classical Persian — followed by و پرهیز و دین ('and forbearance and religion/piety'). The nouns are listed in parallel to enumerate desirable qualities.
segments.s657-g110: زبان چرب is a compound meaning literally 'a greasy tongue' = 'a flattering/smooth speech'; گوینده is 'speaker/orator' (here part of the qualities: eloquent speech or speaker); بآفرین (با آفرین) means 'with praise' or 'praiseworthy' (often used to indicate applause or commendation). The sequence continues the catalogue of desirable traits.
segments.s657-g111: چودارید is a fused/classical construction (چو + دارید) meaning 'since/as you have' or 'because you are'; هر دو = 'both'; ز شاهی نژاد = 'of royal birth/lineage' (ز = poetic 'from/of', شاهی نژاد = 'royal descent'). The phrase gives the reason for the requirements that follow.
segments.s657-g112: خرد باید is 'wisdom is necessary' or 'you must have wisdom' — باید is the modal verb 'must/ought' expressing obligation; و شرم و پرهیز و داد continues the list 'and modesty (shame), and forbearance, and داد' — داد here reads as the noun 'justice' or 'giving/charity' (classical lists often use داد = 'justice'/'generosity'). The whole segment states the virtues expected of royal-born sons.
segments.s657-g1017: پیاده (adverb/adjective) precedes the verb to describe manner: 'on foot'. برفتی is a past second-person singular verb 'you went'. The two short prepositional phrases ز پیش و ز پس use the poetic z (ز) meaning 'from', repeating it for parallelism: 'from the front and from the back/behind.'
segments.s657-g1018: کجا is an interrogative 'where' starting the question. بود is the past tense of بودن ('was') and here functions as the main verb. در جنگ is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the battle', and فریادرس is a predicate noun 'a rescuer/helper' (i.e. 'where was a rescuer in battle?').
segments.s657-g1019: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when/whenever'. بگذاشتی is a past second-person singular verb that here means 'you allowed/let (it be) / you left'. تا functions as 'until' marking the limit; سر آوردگاه is a genitive compound 'head of the battlefield' (سر + آوردگاه).
segments.s657-g1020: نشستی is the past form 'you sat' (note: the provided lemma/senses list was for a different root; here the form means 'sat'). چو here is a comparative 'like/as', linking to فرزانه 'a sage' (simile). بر دست شاه is a locative/idiomatic phrase 'on the king's hand' meaning 'in the king's power/favor' with دست meaning 'power/control' in this context.
segments.s657-g1021: همان نیز is a demonstrative plus also/emphatic 'that same, too'. فرزانه is the noun 'wise (man)'. یک خانه بیش is a quantitative phrase 'one house more' meaning 'no more than one house' or 'not beyond one dwelling', with بیش 'more' indicating limit/extent.
segments.s657-g1022: نرفتی is the past second-person singular 'you did not go' (here functioning as a negated or limited movement in context). نبودی is past of بودن 'you were not/was not'. از + ین (ازین = 'from this') introduces the referent 'this king', and پیش 'in the presence of / before' completes the locative phrase 'before this king.' The two verbs form a coordinated comment about absence/motion.
segments.s657-g1023: سه خانه is a numeral + noun 'three houses/steps' functioning as a measure of distance or progress; it precedes the verb برفتی 'you went', so the structure is 'you went three houses'. سرافراز is an adjective 'upright/triumphant/honored' modifying پیل 'elephant'.
segments.s657-g1024: بدیدی is a past second-person singular 'you saw'. همه رزم گه is a noun phrase 'the whole battlefield' (همه = all/whole, رزم گه = battlefield/scene of battle). از دو میل is a measure phrase 'from two mils' (mil = a distance unit); the preposition از introduces the span/source. Note: the provided senses for میل do not include the distance sense, so that specific sense is indicated below as unknown.
segments.s657-g305: زبانها is the subject 'tongues/languages' (plural). ز گفتارشان is a prepositional/case phrase 'from/because of their speech' with the possessive suffix -شان on گفتار. شد ستوه is a compound predicate (شدن + adjective) meaning 'became weary' — poetic word order puts the predicate after the cause phrase.
segments.s657-g306: نگشتند is the past negative of گشتن used as 'did not become/turn / did not come to be' (subject is omitted, understood from previous phrase). همرای و با هم‌گروه are adverbial/comitative phrases 'together/along with and with the same group' describing the lack of union; the coordinating و links the two accompanying phrases.
segments.s657-g307: پراگنده گشت is a predicate 'became scattered/dispersed' (پراگنده adjective + گشتن 'to become'); آن بزرگ انجمن is the determinate subject 'that great assembly' placed after the predicate in poetic inversion.
segments.s657-g308: سپاهی وشهری lists two groups 'soldiers and townspeople' (و as simple 'and'). همه تن به تن is an emphatic distributive phrase 'all, person by person / each and every one' (تن used as 'person'); به here has the sense 'to' in the fixed collocation تن به تن ('one to one' / 'each one').
segments.s657-g309: یکی is an indefinite 'someone / a certain one' as subject; سوی followed by a name/term (طلخند) marks the recipient 'to Talakhnd' (proper-name usage). پیغام کرد is a simple past clause 'sent/caused a message' (کرد as the main past verb).
segments.s657-g310: زبان را (زبان + object marker را) is the direct object 'the tongue / speech' (here meaning 'his words'). زگو is an adverbial/relational 'from that / from it' pointing back to the prior exchange; پر ز دشنام کرد is 'filled (it) with insults/curses' (پر + ز + noun = 'full of X' with کرد as the verb).
segments.s657-g311: دگر سوی 'to the other/another direction' with دگر = 'another/other'; گو here functions as a reference to the addressee or 'that speaker' (usage in the text is elliptical/poetic) and is treated as the object of سوی. رفت is past 'went'; با + instrument nouns 'گرز و تیغ' = 'with club/mace and sword' (instruments accompanying the action).
segments.s657-g312: که introduces a content clause 'that/which' giving the substance of what was said. از شاه functions as a prepositional phrase 'with regard to / for the king' (beneficiary/concern), جان را is the definite object 'the life' (را marks the object), ندارم is first-person present 'I have not / I do not possess', and دریغ is the predicate noun 'reluctance / hesitation' — together: 'that (I) do not have reluctance regarding my life for the king.'
segments.s657-g825: سوار آمد = “a rider came” (سوار subject + آمد past 3sg). و connects the two actions. سر به سر بنگرید is an adverbial phrase: سر به سر (literally “head to head”) here means “all around” or “everywhere,” and بنگرید is the classical past 3sg form of نگریدن meaning “he looked/seen.” The whole segment thus says that the rider arrived and scanned everywhere.
segments.s657-g826: درفش سرنامداران ندید = direct clause with object first: درفش (the banner/standard) + سرنامداران (of the leading men) as its qualifier, followed by ندید, past negative 'did not see.' In classical Persian the negated past is often written as a single word (ندید).
segments.s657-g827: همه قلب گه دید پر گفت و گوی is structured as object/phrase before the verb: همه قلب = “every heart,” گه = “at times/sometimes,” دید = past 3sg ‘he saw.’ پر گفت و گوی = “full of words/talk” (پر governing a following noun + گفت و گوی = گفت‌وگو 'conversation' split poetically). Together: he perceived every heart sometimes full of talk.
segments.s657-g828: سواران کشور = the horsemen of the realm (noun followed by possessor), همه = 'all,' and شاه جوی is a compound meaning 'seeking the king' (جوی here = search). Word order is straightforward: subject phrase + quantifier + predicate nominal 'king-seeking.'
segments.s657-g829: فرستاده برگشت و آمد چو باد = فرستاده (the messenger) + برگشت (returned) + و آمد (and came), with چو باد 'like the wind' as a simile qualifying the speed of his coming. برگشت and آمد are past actions; چو = 'as/like' and باد = 'wind.'
segments.s657-g830: سخنها همه پیش او کرد یاد = سخنها (the words/reports) + همه (all) + پیش او ('before him,' i.e. in his presence) + کرد (did/made) + یاد (memory/recall). یاد کردن is an idiom meaning 'to mention/recall,' so کرد یاد = 'he recalled/related' — he told him all that had been said.
segments.s657-g831: سپهبد فرود آمد از پشت پیل = سپهبد (the spahbed/general) + فرود آمد (alighted/dismounted; classical past) + از پشت پیل (from the elephant's back). از پشت = 'from the back of' and پیل = 'elephant' — so he got down from the elephant.
segments.s657-g832: پیاده همی‌رفت گریان دو میل = پیاده 'on foot' (adverbial/adjunct), همی‌رفت is the classical past-continuous/habitual 'was going,' گریان modifies the subject 'weeping/tearful,' and دو میل gives a distance 'two miles' (an adverbial measure). Word order places adverbial and modifiers before/after the verb in the customary epic style.
segments.s657-g257: همی‌خواست is an imperfective/prospective marker here meaning “was about to” (an older narrative phrasing). فرزانه is the noun 'the sage' (agent). The two occurrences of گو are different: the first (فرزانه گو) is an attributive/participial use ('the speaking sage' or 'the sage who speaks'), and the second (just before بود in the next clause) functions as the quotative/verb 'to say' introducing reported content. که is the relative/quotative conjunction 'that' linking the speaker to what follows.
segments.s657-g258: بود is the past copula 'was'. شاه is the subject 'the king'. درسندلی is a frozen prepositional expression 'on the seat / in the chair' (i.e. on the throne). پیشرو is an adjective/adverb 'foremost, in front' modifying the place of the throne; the whole clause is a simple past-state clause completing the quotation introduced by گو که.
segments.s657-g259: هم at the start is an additive/emphatic 'also'. آنکس که introduces a relative clause 'that person who...'. استاد is used in the sense 'master/expert' and is followed by the noun طلخند ('bitter laugh' or 'wry smile') in a possessive/occupational phrase 'master of طلخند'. بود is the past copula 'was' completing the relative clause.
segments.s657-g260: به فرزانگی is an adverbial phrase 'in/with wisdom' (به + abstract noun yields an ablative/instrumental sense: 'by/with wisdom'). هم before خردمند repeats/strengthens the additive sense 'also/too'. خردمند is a predicative adjective 'wise' and بود is again the past copula 'was'. The clause therefore states a parallel quality (both master of طلخند and wise).
segments.s657-g261: همی here is the narrative progressive particle 'kept/was continually' marking iterative action. این and بران (an older fused form equivalent to بر آن) mark the two contrasting referents 'this one' and 'that one'; the verb زد 'struck' follows the object-marking/prepositional strings (typical older word order: demonstrative + locative particle + verb). وآن برین repeats the mirror image for the other party (بر + این written as برین).
segments.s657-g262: چنین is a resumptive adverb 'thus/so' linking to the previous line. تا here functions temporally/continuative 'until' (introducing the end state). دو مهتر is the object 'the two chiefs/elders'. گرفتند is the past transitive verb 'they seized/took' and کین is the direct object 'vengeance/hatred' (i.e. they took vengeance).
segments.s657-g263: نهاده is a past participle/verb 'placed/put' and forms a periphrastic past with بدند here to indicate a completed action ('they placed'). بدند is the older plural past marker used with نهاده. اندر is the preposition 'in/within' and ایوان is the locative 'hall/portico'. دو تخت is the direct object 'two thrones' (word order: verb phrase before locative and object is typical in this elevated style).
segments.s657-g264: نشسته is a predicate adjective/participle 'seated'. به + تخت here gives the place 'on the throne' (به marking location). آن دو is the demonstrative 'those two' referring back to the previously mentioned thrones. پیروز بخت is a binomial epithet 'victorious of fortune' or 'fortunate victors', with پیروز qualifying and بخت indicating 'fortune/fate' — together they describe the occupants of the thrones.
segments.s657-g897: چوازراه is a fused adverbial phrase (چو از راه) meaning 'when/from the road' (temporal/circumstantial); برخاست is a past verb 'rose/rose up/advanced' placed after its adverbial; گرد سپاه is a postposed prepositional/circumstantial phrase 'around the host' (گرد = 'around/about', followed by its object سپاه). The clause order is adverbial → verb → circumstantial modifier, typical of classical Persian narration.
segments.s657-g898: نگه کرد is a light verb construction: نگه (a noun 'gaze/watch') + کرد ('made/did') together mean 'he looked/peered' or 'kept watch.' بینادل is an uncertain/rare lexical item here (left untranslated as an unusual reading in the manuscript). از دیده‌گاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the viewpoint/lookout' (از = 'from', دیده‌گاه = 'field of sight/vantage'). Verb+object/order is straightforward: action (looked) then origin of gaze.
segments.s657-g899: همی is an adverb here indicating continued/ongoing action ('kept on'/'still'); دیده‌بان is the subject 'the sentry/watchman'; بنگرید is a present-subjunctive/imperative form used narratively as a pastive 'looked' or 'kept looking' (classical poetic usage often employs present/subjunctive forms for past narrative). از دو میل is an origin/position phrase 'from the two masts/pillars' (از = 'from', دو میل = 'two masts/markers').
segments.s657-g900: که introduces a subordinate clause 'so that / to see that' (here: 'to see whether'); بیند is subjunctive/present of 'to see' used for potential ('he might see'); مگر here functions as 'except' / 'other than' (classical usage); تاج طلخند و پیل lists the things he can make out — تاج 'crown', طلخند appears as a proper/name-like word in the text (here treated as a lexical item attached to تاج), and پیل 'elephant'. The clause thus expresses expectation/limitation: 'to see if he could see anything except ...'.
segments.s657-g901: ز بالا is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from on high / above' (ز is the archaic/poetic form of از); درفش (banner/standard) is the subject; گو is a suffix-like qualifier here (literally 'speaking' or part of a compound name such as 'darfash‑e‑go'); آمد پدید is a verb phrase 'came into view/appeared' with آمد 'came' + پدید 'appeared/manifested'. Word order: source → subject → verb phrase, typical in epic diction.
segments.s657-g902: همه روی کشور is a locative expression 'over the whole face/surface of the country' (همه = 'all/entire', روی کشور = 'the face of the land' with روی used spatially 'upon'); سپه is the subject 'the host/army'; گسترید is a past tense verb 'spread' (3rd person), so the clause says the army spread across the land. Classical Persian often places the locative phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s657-g903: نیامد پدید is a negated verbal phrase: نیامد ('did not come') + پدید ('into view/appear'), so 'did not appear.' از میان سپاه is the source phrase 'from among the host' (از میان = 'from the midst of', سپاه = 'army'). Word order here is negative verb phrase followed by its source phrase, standard for a negative statement.
segments.s657-g904: سواری is the noun 'a rider/horseman' (subject); برافگند is a classical/perfective-form verb meaning here roughly 'flung forth / sprang up / was cast into view' (a poetic verb often used for sudden appearance or action); از دیده‌گاه is the origin 'from the lookout/vantage.' The clause thus describes a rider suddenly coming into view from the vantage point. Note the strong poetic verb choice and the compact syntax (subject → verb → source).
segments.s657-g425: وگر is the conditional conjunction 'if / but if' introducing a concessive clause. چند here means 'several / some' and qualifies the following noun. بیداد جویی is a compound: بیداد (injustice) + جویی (seeking/pursuit), a verbal-noun phrase meaning 'pursuit of injustice' (i.e., 'seek injustice'). همه following that phrase emphasizes totality ('all/everyone') and links to the following verbal predicate.
segments.s657-g426: پراگندن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to scatter' used here as the action; گرد 'around' gives the direction/extent ('about, around'), and کرده is the perfect/participle form of کردن used to form a compound verbal idea ('have/made ...'); رمه 'flock' is the accusative/theme placed at the end (typical SOV order). Together the phrase means 'have scattered the flock about.'
segments.s657-g427: بدین گیتی is a fixed poetic locution 'in this world' (بدین = 'by/with this' or 'in this manner' + گیتی 'world'). اندر نکوهش بود is a simple past copular clause: اندر 'in/within', نکوهش 'reproach/censure', and بود 'was' (past). The whole segment states a past-state: 'he was in reproach in this world.'
segments.s657-g428: همین 'this very' is a demonstrative identifying what follows. رابدان is an archaic verbal form (from رابد/رابَدن) meaning 'to seize' or 'seized' and here functions as the core of a purposive/causal phrase. سر 'head' is the object/goal word following رابدان, and پژوهش 'investigation/search' with بود 'was' completes the clause as a past-state: 'this very (thing) was (the) seizing-of-the-head's pursuit' — i.e. 'this very thing caused/was the motive of his quest for the head.' (The construction is idiomatic and archaic.)
segments.s657-g429: مکن‌ای is the negative imperative 'do not' (مکن) combined with the vocative particle -ای addressing someone; برادر is a vocative 'O brother'. The phrase به بیداد رای uses به + noun + رای to mean 'give/assign your counsel/opinion to X' — literally 'have a counsel toward/for injustice' (i.e. favour injustice).
segments.s657-g430: که here introduces the reason ('for' / 'because'). بیداد را is the topicalized/definite object ('injustice' marked by را). نیست is the negative copula 'is not / does not exist'. با + داد means 'with justice' or 'alongside justice', and پای (lit. 'foot') is used figuratively 'foot/standing' — so the clause states that injustice has no standing alongside justice.
segments.s657-g431: چون here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when/as'. فرستاده is the subject 'the messenger', پیش طلخند means 'in the presence of / before Talakhund' (پیش = before/in the presence of), and شد is the past verb 'became/arrived' (classical use of شد to mark occurrence).
segments.s657-g432: به پیغام شاه uses به instrumentally ('by/with the king's message'); پیغام شاه = 'the king's message'. از در پند شد literally 'from the door admonition happened' — از در = 'at/from the gate/door', پند شد = 'he was given admonition' (پند as admonition, شد as passive/occurrence).
segments.s657-g217: 'که' here introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('that/which'). 'آن را که' functions as 'that which' (آن + object marker را + relative که). 'خواهد دهد' is a third-person present/future-form of خواستن/دادن meaning 'will/does give' or 'wishes to give' — in epic narration it presents a general truth. 'نیکوی' is an adjective qualifying the giving ('goodly/beneficent') placed after the verb for poetic emphasis.
segments.s657-g218: 'نگر' is an imperative/exhortation 'look/consider'. 'جز به یزدان' = 'except to God' (جز = 'except', به = 'to/for', یزدان = 'God'). The phrase that follows ('به کس نگروی') uses گرویدن in a negative exhortative sense: do not take/adhere to anyone else; word order is inverted for poetic effect ('except to God, to no one should you turn').
segments.s657-g219: Subject 'من' ('I') + past verb 'انداختم' (I put/placed/laid down — here metaphorically 'I have written/compiled'). The clause 'هرچ آمد ز پند' is a free relative 'whatever came from advice' (هرچ = 'whatever', آمد = 'came', ز = 'from', پند = 'advice'), acting as the object of 'انداختم'.
segments.s657-g220: 'اگر' introduces a conditional ('if'). 'نیست' is the existential/predicative 'is not' (there is no). The verbless verblike sequence 'پند منت سودمند' should be read as a nominal clause 'advice, a favour, [is] not beneficial' — i.e. if advice is lacking, mere favour/thanks is not useful; word order is compact/elliptical, typical of poetry.
segments.s657-g221: 'نگر' again is an imperative 'consider/see'. 'تاچه بهتر' = 'how much better / how best' (تاچه = 'how', بهتر = 'better'). 'ز کارآن' = 'with/regarding that affair' (ز = 'of/from/about', کارآن = 'that matter'). 'کنید' is a second-person plural/polite imperative/subjunctive 'do / you should do' — together: 'Consider how best you should handle that affair.'
segments.s657-g222: 'وزین' = 'and/from this' or adjectival 'weighty' here introducing the counsel ('وزین پند' = 'this weighty advice'). 'پند من' = 'my advice'. 'توشه جان' is a genitive compound 'provisions of the soul / provision for life'. 'کنید' (same form as above) is the verb 'make/take' (imperative/plural): 'make my counsel a provision for your life.'
segments.s657-g223: 'وزان پس' = 'and/from that then' (sequence marker). 'همه بخردان را' = 'all the wise (people)' with را marking them as definite object/topic. 'بخواند' is a narrative third-person verb here meaning 'he called/summoned' (خواندن has the senses 'read/recite' but here 'call/summon' is intended). The verb form is classical/poetic narrative.
segments.s657-g224: 'همه پندها' = 'all the counsels' (plural). 'پیش ایشان' = 'before them/in their presence' (پیش = 'before/in presence of', ایشان = 'them'). 'براند' (from راندن) here means 'he set forth/placed/presented' — used in narrative, third-person; the verb brings the counsels forward before the assembled wise men.
segments.s657-g393: درفشی درفشان is a nominal phrase: درفشی (a banner/standard) followed by the adjectival/participial درفشان ‘shining, glittering’; Persian often places the modifier after the noun. به سر بر به پای is a compact locative: به + سر (on the head) then بر (on/upon) + به + پای (at the feet), a poetic stacking that means “from head to foot / on head and on foot.”
segments.s657-g394: یکی introduces a contrasted individual (‘one [of them]’). پیکرش is پیکر + ـش (his/its body) and the copula ‘is’ is omitted (typical in Persian descriptive lines), so پیکرش ببر = ‘his body (is) a tiger.’ و دیگر همای parallels that: ‘and the other (is) a Humay.’
segments.s657-g395: پیاده here names the infantry/foot soldiers (noun). به پیش is a prepositional phrase ‘in front/forward,’ and اندرون places them ‘within’ or ‘among the inner ranks.’ نیزه‌دار is a compound noun ‘spear‑bearer’ describing those inside.
segments.s657-g396: سپردار is a noun ‘commander’ or ‘one with arms.’ The conjunction و links to شایسته کار زار: شایسته is an adjective ‘worthy,’ followed by کار (deed/work) and زار ‘wretched/pitiable,’ so the phrase qualifies the commander as ‘worthy in station but wretched in his deeds’ (زار modifies کار).
segments.s657-g397: نگه کرد is a past tense verb phrase ‘he looked/turned his gaze’ (کرد here is the past auxiliary). گو is a quotative/introductory particle ‘said’ (used to introduce what follows). اندراند (اندران) + دشت جنگ is a locative phrase ‘there in the plain of battle’ (دشت + جنگ = battlefield).
segments.s657-g398: هوا here means ‘the air/mood/spirit’ (of the battle) and دید is the past ‘he saw/perceived.’ چون is a simile particle ‘like/as,’ and پشت جنگی پلنگ is the image compared to — پشت ‘back’ + جنگی ‘warlike’ (adjective) + پلنگ ‘leopard’ — i.e. ‘like the back of a war‑leopard.’
segments.s657-g399: همه کام خاک is an elliptic clause: همه ‘all’ + کام ‘desire’ + خاک ‘earth’ → ‘all’s desire was the earth’ (a poetic way to say they fell/pressed to the ground). و همه دشت خون parallels it: ‘and the whole plain (was) blood,’ where دشت is the plain and خون denotes the blood/bloodshed covering it.
segments.s657-g400: بگرد is an imperative of گشتن, here ‘search/turn about’; اندرون specifies the location ‘within/among them.’ نیزه is the noun ‘spear,’ and بد rهنمون is a small adjectival phrase ‘bad guide’ (بد modifying رهنمون), so the clause reads like an injunction to search within because ‘the spearman is a poor guide.’
segments.s657-g745: اگر introduces a conditional. بود here is an archaic auxiliary meaning roughly 'be/would be' placed before the main verb; خواهید is 2nd-person (plural or honorific) present meaning 'you wish/want'. با من یکی = 'one with me' (literally 'one with me', i.e. united with me). Word order is somewhat inverted (auxiliary before main verb) as in older Persian.
segments.s657-g746: نپیچید is a negative imperative (2nd-person plural/honorific) 'do not turn / do not twist'. سر را is the definite object with the object marker را. ز is the poetic preposition 'from / away from'. داد here means 'justice' (not the verb 'gave'). اندکی 'a little' modifies the degree ('not even a little'). The clause literally: 'Do not turn the head a little from justice.'
segments.s657-g747: اگر introduces the conditional. جنگ جویم = 'I seek/seek battle' with جویم a 1st-person verb form (verb follows its object, typical Persian order). چه ... چه is the correlative 'whether ... or' linking دریا (sea) and کوه (mountain) as alternatives.
segments.s657-g748: چو here = 'when' (introduces a temporal clause). در جنگ = 'in battle'; لشکر is the subject 'the host/army'. بود is past 'was'. هم‌گروه is a predicative adjective 'of the same group / united as one band'. The clause means 'when the army was in battle it was of one company / united.'
segments.s657-g749: اگر starts the condition. یار باشید = 'if you are companions/allies' with باشید as 2nd-person plural subjunctive/ present subjunctive ('be' in the protasis). با من به جنگ = 'with me into battle' (با من 'with me', به جنگ 'to/in battle').
segments.s657-g750: از indicates source/cause 'from / by'. آواز روبه = 'the cry/voice of the fox' (روبه here = fox). نترسد is 3rd-person singular present negative 'does not fear / will not be frightened'. پلنگ (placed at the end for emphasis) is the subject 'the leopard'. Literal order: 'From the fox's cry the leopard will not be afraid.'
segments.s657-g751: هر آنکس که = 'whoever / every person who' (هر + آنکس + relative که). جویند is a 3rd-person plural verb used with an indefinite subject (a classical Persian feature) meaning 'seeks' or 'pursues' here. نام بزرگ = 'great name' i.e. great fame or renown.
segments.s657-g752: ز گیتی = 'from the world' (ز = from, poetic). بیابند is 3rd-person plural subjunctive/indicative form meaning 'they find / they attain' (used here with the indefinite construction to state the result). کام بزرگ = 'great desire/fulfillment' (کام = satisfaction/fulfillment). The clause states the outcome: 'they will obtain great fulfillment from the world.'
segments.s657-g681: بدوگفت is a past-tense verbal phrase meaning 'said to him' (verb before indirect object in this poetic inversion). فرزانه names the speaker (the sage) and functions as the subject/agent. کای is a poetic vocative particle 'O', and شهریار is the addressee 'sovereign/king' — together they form the direct address the sage utters.
segments.s657-g682: تویی is the emphatic copular form 'thou art' (subject + copula). از here indicates source/ancestry ('from/of'), پدر means 'father/ancestor', and تخت را یادگار is a predicate noun phrase 'a memento/legacy of the throne' with را marking تخت as definite/emphatic; the whole string says 'you are, from your father, the throne's legacy' (i.e. heir).
segments.s657-g683: ز دانش پژوهان introduces the standard of comparison with ز ('than/of') followed by the noun phrase 'seekers of knowledge' (دانش = knowledge, پژوهان = seekers). تو is the subject 'you' and داناتری is the comparative adjective 'more learned' (the -تری comparative). The prepositional comparative phrase precedes the copular clause due to poetic word order.
segments.s657-g684: هم 'also/even' adds emphasis that the following comparison holds as well; از تاجداران again introduces the standard of comparison ('than the crown-bearers'). تواناتری is the comparative 'more able/stronger'. The subject 'تو' is omitted here (understood from the previous clause).
segments.s657-g685: مرا is the experiencer/ethical dative 'to me' (here indicating 'in my view'), followed by این درستست 'this is right/true' (درستست = it is correct). و گفتم is the simple past 'and I said', and بشاه is a vocative 'O king' addressed to the sovereign — either 'I said (it), O king' or 'and I told the king', depending on vocative/indirect object reading.
segments.s657-g686: ز is used instrumentally/poetically as 'by' or 'from', introducing the celestial witnesses 'گردنده خورشید' (the revolving/turning sun) and 'تابنده ماه' (the shining moon). گردنده and تابنده are present participles used attributively before the nouns, giving vivid image and serving as the cause/witness invoked for the speaker's claim.
segments.s657-g687: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/for that', followed by این نامور 'this famed/renowned one' as the subject. تا + نگردد هلاک uses تا 'until' with نگردد (negative of the verb 'گردیدن' in a subjunctive/optative sense) and هلاک 'destruction' — poetically expressing 'until he comes to destruction' (the negative subjunctive construction here marks the temporal boundary before ruin).
segments.s657-g688: بگردد is a present/future-oriented form of گشتن 'to turn/become' meaning 'will turn/writhe'. چو مار is a simile 'like a snake'. اندرین تیره خاک is a locative phrase 'in this dark earth' (اندرین = in these/this, تیره = dark/ominous, خاک = earth/soil). The verb at the start gives a vivid action followed by the simile and location.
segments.s657-g729: ز introduces the subordinate condition/cause (classical/poetic use akin to 'since/if'); ماهرک is a diminutive adjective/noun meaning a 'somewhat' or 'small' expert; پیروز گرد/د is a three-person singular verbal phrase meaning 'becomes victorious' (گردد = 'become', here used as the finite verb, classical present/subjunctive/optative shape used for a conditional sense). به جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'in/into battle' modifying پیروز گردد.
segments.s657-g730: بریزیم is a 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive/optative form ('let us pour' / 'we shall spill') functioning as the main verb of the clause; خون is the direct object ('blood' / here 'bloodshed'); اندرین جای تنگ is a locative phrase ('in/at this narrow place') with اندرین ('in this') + جای ('place') + تنگ ('narrow, confined'). Word order places the verb before its object for emphasis, typical in poetic style.
segments.s657-g731: سپه را uses the object marker را to mark 'the army' as definite object; همه modifies سپه ('the whole army' / 'everyone of the host'). دستگیر here is an adjective/participial noun meaning 'taken/captured' or 'seized', and آوریم (1st person plural) is the verb 'we will bring/make' — together دستگیر آوریم = 'we will seize/carry off as prisoners.' The verb is placed at the end as usual.
segments.s657-g732: مبادا که is a classical warning/negative optative 'lest' / 'may it not be that...' introducing a prohibition or warning. شمشیر و تیر آوریم uses the verb آوریم (1st pl.) 'we bring/carry' with the coordinated objects 'sword and arrow' — grammatically this reads as a caution 'let us not bring sword and arrow' or 'lest we bring swords and arrows.'
segments.s657-g733: فرستاده برگشت و آمد is a compound past narrative: فرستاده 'the messenger' + برگشت 'returned' + و آمد 'and came' (both verbs in past). چو باد is a simile 'like the wind' (چو = 'as/like', باد = 'wind') modifying آمد to indicate speed.
segments.s657-g734: بروبر is an adverb meaning 'directly' / 'face-to-face' (here 'straightaway'); سخنهای گو is a noun phrase 'the spoken words' where گو is a postpositive/participial element meaning 'speaking' or 'said'; کرد یاد is a light-verb construction where کرد ('did/said') takes یاد ('remembrance/recollection') to mean 'he related/recalled' — so together: 'he directly related the spoken words from memory.'
segments.s657-g735: چوطلخند is a poetic adverb/phrase 'with a bitter/sardonic laugh' (چو + طلخند fused here); بشنید is the past tense 'he heard.' گفتار گو follows as 'he spoke words' (گفتار = 'speech', گو = 'speaking'), so the clause gives: 'When he heard the sardonic laugh, he spoke (these) words.'
segments.s657-g736: ز لشکر is a prepositional/partitive phrase 'from the army' (ز 'from' used in poetic register + لشکر 'host, army'). هرآنکس که introduces a free relative 'whoever / anyone who' with که marking the relative clause; بد پیشرو is a nominal phrase 'a bad/ill-suited vanguard' (بد = 'bad', پیشرو = 'vanguard/forerunner'). Together: 'from the army, whoever is a bad vanguard...' (the remainder of the thought continues afterwards).
segments.s657-g817: زقلب سپه is a poetic contraction of از قلب سپه (“from the heart/middle of the host”); قلب (heart, centre) follows the noun سپه (host) in the genitival phrase. چون نگه کرد is a past-tense clause (‘when he looked’), where نگه (gaze) plus کرد (do/make) forms the verb ‘looked’. گو here is the classical particle meaning “he said / it is reported,” introducing reported observation that follows.
segments.s657-g818: ندید is simple past negative ‘did not see’. آن points to a specific banner already in the narrative. درفش is ‘standard/banner’, سپهدار is the commander/title attached to the banner, and نو is the adjective ‘new’ following the noun (typical adjective–noun order in classical Persian can vary).
segments.s657-g819: سواری فرستاد is a straightforward past action ‘he sent a rider/horseman’. تا introduces a purposive clause here (‘so that / in order that’). پشت پیل means ‘behind the elephant’ (پشت = rear/behind, پیل = elephant).
segments.s657-g820: بگردد and بجوید are present-subjunctive/optative forms (third-person singular with بـ-) governed by the preceding تا: they express the rider's intended actions (‘to go about / to circle’ and ‘to search / to seek’). همه میل میل repeats میل for poetic emphasis: همه ‘all/every’, میل here means ‘mast / pole’ (i.e. banner‑staff), and the duplication intensifies comprehensiveness.
segments.s657-g821: ببیند is another present-subjunctive (3rd person) tied to the earlier تا: ‘to see / so that he may see’. که introduces the subordinate content clause. آن لعل رخشان درفش is a noun phrase with adjectives: آن ‘that’, لعل ‘ruby / deep red’, رخشان ‘shining/radiant’, درفش ‘standard/banner’ — adjective chain modifies درفش.
segments.s657-g822: کزو is a relative adverb ‘from which / from whom’ referring back to the banner. بود is past-tense ‘was’. روی here means ‘face’ or ‘appearance’; سواران is the plural ‘horsemen/riders’. بنفش (‘purple’) predicates the colour of their faces — the order is characteristic of classical Persian (relative → copula → predicate phrase).
segments.s657-g823: کجاشد is an interrogative/emo­tive compound (کجا + شد) meaning roughly ‘how / what became (of it)’, asking incredulously. که introduces the clause ‘that …’. بنشست is past ‘sat down / ceased’. جوش نبرد is a compound noun phrase where جوش (‘surge, ardour’) qualifies نبرد (‘battle’) — ‘the battle’s ardour’ or ‘the fury of the fight’.
segments.s657-g824: مگر here functions as corrective ‘but rather / instead’; it negates the previous suggestion. چشم من is the possessor phrase ‘my eye’. تیره گون شد is a periphrastic predicate: تیره (‘dark’) + گون (forming ‘-colored/-looking’) + شد ‘became’ = ‘became dark/dim’. ز is the poetic form of از (‘from’) and گرد here means ‘dust’ — ‘my eye grew darkened by the dust.’
segments.s657-g889: This is an enumerative noun phrase listing preservatives/incense in parallel, joined by the coordinating و 'and' each time; the repeated و is a common poetic cadence. Each item is a noun (or adjective used as noun) that will be applied to the coffin in the next segment.
segments.s657-g890: سرتنگ precedes تابوت as an adjectival/qualifying phrase (poetic placement); تابوت is the direct object. کردند is the third-person plural past of کردن used causatively ('made'/'did'), and خشک is a resultative predicate ('dry', here implying sealed/airtight). Word order in poetry can be compressed: literally 'they made the coffin dry.'
segments.s657-g891: وزان is a connective prepositional phrase ('and/from that') linking this action to what came before; جایگه is 'place'. تیز modifies لشکر ('the swift army'), and براند is a third-person singular (poetic) form of راندن meaning 'drove/led/set in motion' — here 'set off'.
segments.s657-g892: به ... و به ... is a paired prepositional phrase ('on/at the road and on/at the stopping-place'); فراوان here functions adverbially ('much/long') modifying نماند, which is negative past/poetic meaning 'did not remain/linger' — so the whole clause means they did not stay long either on the road or at the encampments.
segments.s657-g893: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; شاهان is the plural subject 'the kings'; گزیدند is past tense 'they chose'; جای نبرد is the object phrase 'the place of battle' (noun + genitive/compound noun). This is a straightforward subordinate temporal clause.
segments.s657-g894: بشد is a narrative/resultative verb meaning 'it happened/it became' (here introducing what happened to the mother). مادر is the affected party; از خواب و آرام ('from sleep and calm') indicates she was roused out of rest; خورد is used in the intransitive, idiomatic sense 'befell/struck' (i.e., misfortune or disturbance befell her). Together: 'the mother was roused from sleep and peace and was stricken.'
segments.s657-g895: همیشه is the adverb 'always' and appears sentence-initially for emphasis. بره here is literal 'lamb' but functions idiomatically as a diminutive/affectionate reference to a young or gentle watchman; دیدبان is 'sentry/watchman'. داشتی is the second-person past 'you had' — 'you kept/maintained.'
segments.s657-g896: به تلخی is an adverbial phrase 'in bitterness' or 'bitterly'; همی روز is a demonstrative/intoned phrase 'this very day' (همی here intensifies); بگذاشتی is second-person past of گذاشتن used idiomatically 'you let (it/him) be/you left him' or 'you allowed' — together: 'you let him go on this bitter day.'
segments.s657-g225: A noun phrase where کلید 'key' is head and درگنج 'in the treasure' functions as a genitive/locative modifier (classical Persian often omits an explicit ezāfe). دو پادشا follows to specify whose treasure it is: 'the two kings.' Word order is head + modifying locative + possessor.
segments.s657-g226: A relative clause introduced by که 'who/that' modifying دو پادشا. بودند is past plural of بودن 'to be' (they were). بادانش here is a fused poetic form meaning 'با دانش' = 'learned/knowledgeable' (sense not listed); و links the two predicates; پارسا is an adjective 'pious'.
segments.s657-g227: Compound verb phrase: بیاورد is a past subjunctive/past-tense verb 'brought/produced', followed by وکرد 'and did/made' as a coordinating verb — a typical poetic coordination. آشکارا is an adverb 'openly/evidently' and نهان is an adjectival/nominal (here 'hidden') describing what is made manifest.
segments.s657-g228: A prepositional phrase: به پیش = 'in the presence of' (به + پیش) marking the audience; جهاندیدگان is a plural noun 'those who have seen the world' = 'worldly‑wise/experienced people'; ومهان is a coordinated noun meaning something like 'and the great/magnates' (the exact sense is a poetic title and the provided sense list does not match).
segments.s657-g229: Adverbial phrase + verb: سراسر 'throughout/entirely' modifies the giving; بر ایشان 'upon/to them' is the indirect object marked by بر; ببخشید is past tense 'he bestowed/gave' (بخشیدن), and راست here is an emphatic adverb 'truly/indeed' qualifying the action.
segments.s657-g230: Nominal phrase functioning as a second clause: همه کام 'all desires/wishes' is the object; آن هر دو فرزند 'of those two sons' (آن + هر دو + فرزند) specifies whose desires; خواست is a past tense verb (classically here used in the sense that their wishes were realized/granted).
segments.s657-g231: Sequence of discourse markers and the reporting verb: چنین گفت 'thus he said' (چنین = thus, گفت = said). زان پس = 'thereafter/from that, then'. به طلخند گو = 'to Talakhund, saying...' — به marks the addressee and گو ('say') introduces the direct speech; طلخند here is a proper name (not the common noun sense listed).
segments.s657-g232: Direct address/vocative introduced by که‌ای (classical written form of 'که ای' = 'O'); a string of epithets: نیک‌دل 'kind‑hearted', نامور 'renowned', followed by یار نو 'new companion' (یار = companion/ally, نو = new). Word order is vocative + adjectives + noun + postpositive adjective.
segments.s657-g153: This is an imperative clause in poetic word order: 'بدو' is the indirect object 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb, and 'گوی' is the imperative 'say/tell'. 'تا' begins the subordinate clause meaning 'so that / lest', and the idiom 'از پی' (literally 'from/after + following') functions as 'in pursuit of' or 'seeking'. 'تاج و تخت' are coordinated direct objects 'crown and throne.'
segments.s657-g154: 'نگیرد' is a negative present/subjunctive (jussive-like) form meaning 'should not seize / must not take' (negated by the initial ن-). 'به' here is adverbial/agentive 'by/through' marking the means, so 'به بی‌دانشی' = 'by/through ignorance'. 'کارسخت' is a nominal predicate 'a hard/arduous task' commenting on the action.
segments.s657-g155: Word order places the indirect object 'بدو' before the verb: 'مادر گفت بدو' = 'the mother said to him'. 'که' introduces reported speech/content. 'تندی مکن' is a negative imperative: the noun 'تندی' ('haste') with the prohibitive verb 'مکن' = 'do not be hasty.'
segments.s657-g156: 'براندیشه' (literally 'upon thought') is an adverbial phrase meaning 'after thinking / with reflection'. 'باید' is the impersonal/modal 'it is necessary / you must'. The following 'که' introduces the content clause and 'رانی سخن' is the object phrase 'words to the rani' (i.e., 'speak to the queen'); the explicit subject is omitted (understood: you/one).
segments.s657-g157: 'هرآنکس' is the indefinite relative 'whoever / anyone who'. 'که' is the relative pronoun introducing the clause. 'برتخت' (بـ + تخت) means 'on the throne', 'شاهی' qualifies it as 'royal / of kingship', and 'نشست' is the past/simple perfect form 'sat/settled' used idiomatically for 'occupies the throne.'
segments.s657-g158: 'میان بسته' literally 'with the middle/waist bound' (i.e., restrain one's appetite/self), where 'میان' = 'middle' and 'بسته' = 'bound/tied'. 'باید' expresses necessity 'must', and 'گشاده دو دست' 'two hands open' places the adjective 'گشاده' before the noun 'دو دست' to mean 'hands wide open' (figuratively: generous).
segments.s657-g159: 'نگه‌داشتن' is the infinitive 'to keep / to preserve'. 'جان پاک' is the direct object 'a pure soul' (noun + adjective), and 'از بدی' is the prepositional phrase 'from evil' marking the source/thing to be avoided.
segments.s657-g160: This is an infinitival clause: 'سپردن' is the infinitive 'to entrust/hand over', 'ره' is the direct object 'the way/path', and 'بخردی' (به + خردی) marks the recipient/instrument 'to wisdom / to wise counsel'. 'بدانش' functions here as a demonstrative/adverbial 'to that' or 'in this (regard)', pointing to the action just described: i.e., 'as for that, to entrust the path to wisdom.'
segments.s657-g865: سپاهست is a poetic fusion of سپاه + است meaning ‘the host is’ (a stative copula). چنان/چندان is an adverb of degree ‘so/that much’ modifying the following predicate. پر از is the idiom ‘full of’ with از marking the content, so درد و خشم are the things filling the host.
segments.s657-g866: سراسر همه is a distributive phrase ‘everywhere/all together’ (سراسر = throughout; همه = everyone). برتو is the prepositional phrase ‘upon you’ (بر + تو). دارند is the 3rd‑person plural present of داشتن ‘they have’ and here combines with چشم (literally ‘eye’) to mean ‘have their eyes (on you)’ — i.e. ‘all eyes are upon you.’
segments.s657-g867: بیارام here is an imperative/appeal meaning ‘be at peace / be calm’ (a poetic form). و is the conjunction ‘and’. ما را is the object phrase ‘us’ (را marking definiteness/emphasis) placed before the verb. تو is the emphatic subject pronoun ‘you’, and آرام ده is an imperative (آرام = calm/peace as a noun/adjective; ده = give) meaning ‘grant calm’. Word order (object before verb, pronoun before verb) is common in Persian imperatives.
segments.s657-g868: خرد را is the object ‘wisdom’ marked by را. به آرام = ‘with/into calm’ (به indicating direction/benefactive into a state of calm). دل کام is a poetic juxtaposition meaning ‘the heart’s desire/fulfilment’ (two nouns placed together as a nominal phrase), and ده is again the imperative ‘give’ — so the clause commands: grant calm to wisdom and give fulfilment to the heart.
segments.s657-g869: که چون begins a temporal clause ‘for/that when’. پادشا را is the definite object ‘the king’ (را marks the object). ببیند is the 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of دیدن ‘to see’ used in the temporal clause, and سپاه placed after the verb is the subject ‘the army’ (verb–subject order is common in poetry).
segments.s657-g870: پر از = ‘full of’ (از marking the contents). درد و گریان is a coordinate phrase ‘pain/grief and weeping’ where گریان is a verbal/adjectival participle ‘weeping, tearful’. پیاده here means ‘on foot’ (describing their mode), and به راه = ‘on the road / on the march’ (به + راه indicating direction/place). The whole phrase is a verbless descriptive clause modifying سپاه.
segments.s657-g871: بکاهدش is the verb بکاهد (from کاستن ‘to lessen’) with the enclitic ش ‘him/it’ as its object: ‘it lessens him/it’. نزد سپاه = ‘in the presence of / before the army’ (نزد = in the presence of). آبروی is the noun ‘reputation/honour’ (here the thing being diminished). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect (verb + object + prepositional phrase).
segments.s657-g872: فرومایه is an adjective ‘low‑born / base’. گستاخ گردد is a verbal phrase: گستاخ (impudent) + گردد (third‑person singular present/subjunctive of گشتن/شدن ‘become’) = ‘becomes impudent’. بروی here is a verbal form (ب‑ + روی/رفتن) meaning ‘go / vanish’ and refers back to آبرو — i.e. his reputation/face disappears; the clause states the consequence: the lowly becomes insolent and his reputation is lost.
segments.s657-g33: سران را همه is a topical/contrastive phrase: سران (the leaders) is marked by را and followed by همه ‘all’, meaning “as for the leaders, all of them…”. Then سر (head) is the possessed noun and پر از گرد بود is the predicate: پر (here “full (of)”) + از گرد (of dust) + بود (past ‘was’). The overall structure is roughly “As for the leaders, all (their) heads were full of dust.”
segments.s657-g34: ز جمهورشان introduces a causal phrase: ز here = “because of / on account of” + جمهورشان “their public/people”. دل پر از درد بود is the main statement: دل “heart”, پر “full (of)”, از “of”, درد “sorrow/pain”, بود past ‘was’. Word order is normal poetic Persian: causal phrase + subject complement + past copula.
segments.s657-g35: ز introduces a causal list: ز بخشیدن و خوردن و داد اوی = “because of his giving and feasting and his justice.” بخشیدن and خوردن are verbal nouns (infinitival nouns) joined by و; داد is a noun meaning “justice.” اوی is an archaic/poetic possessive clitic meaning “his,” attached to داد but understood to modify the whole list.
segments.s657-g36: جهان بود یک سر پر از یاد اوی is the result clause: جهان “the world” + بود (past copula) + یک سر (literally “one head,” here idiomatically indicating ‘throughout’/‘everywhere’ or simply emphasizing totality) + پر از یاد اوی “full of remembrance of him.” Note the possessive clitic اوی again = “his.”
segments.s657-g37: سپاهی و شهری همه انجمن lists the groups forming the assembly: سپاهی “the army” and شهری (here used as ‘townspeople’/‘citizens’), followed by همه انجمن “the whole assembly.” The phrase enumerates both martial and civic sides then calls them collectively an assembly.
segments.s657-g38: زن و کودک و مرد شد رای زن is a coordinated subject list (women, children, and men) followed by شد رای زن = “the opinion/decision became the woman's.” شد is the past of ‘become’, رای here = “opinion/counsel/decision,” and the final زن indicates whose opinion it was (the woman’s). In other words, the whole assembly accepted the woman’s counsel.
segments.s657-g39: که این خرد کودک نداند سپاه is a clause introduced by که “that/for”: این خرد کودک = “this (little) mind of a child” (a possessive description) and نداند سپاه = poetic word order where the verb نداند ‘does not know’ comes before its object سپاه ‘the army’. The sense is “for this child’s understanding does not comprehend the army.”
segments.s657-g40: نه داد و نه خشم و نه تخت و کلاه is a coordinated negative list introduced by repeated نه ‘neither/nor’: the child knows neither داد “justice” nor خشم “anger” nor تخت “throne” nor کلاه “cap/helmet.” This is a catalog of the things beyond the child’s comprehension.
segments.s657-g585: 'که' introduces this clause as part of the reported/commanding speech started earlier ('that...'). The phrase 'بر پای دارید' is literal preposition + noun + present 2nd-person plural verb (you keep/hold on the foot/base) and in poetic word‑order here means 'keep/hold ... firmly in place'; 'یکسر' is an adverb ('entirely/firmly') modifying the verb phrase, and 'درفش' is the object ('standard/banner'). Note the inversion typical of Persian poetry: the adverb ('یکسر') appears close to the object rather than immediately before the verb.
segments.s657-g586: This is a participial/secondary clause describing a simultaneous action: 'کشیده' is a past participle / adjective meaning 'drawn' or 'pulled' and functions like 'having drawn' or simply 'drawn'; 'همه' is the quantifier 'all'; 'تیغهای' is the plural possessive/compound for 'swords/blades'; 'بنفش' is a postpositive color adjective 'purple' modifying 'تیغهای'. Word order here is participle + quantified noun phrase, a common poetic compression meaning 'with all the purple blades drawn'.
segments.s657-g587: This short clause is compressed/poetic. 'یکی' is the numeral/indefinite 'one' (here part of 'not one / not a single one' construction spread across the next clause). 'ازیلان' is an archaic/compacted form in the manuscript (functioning like a group genitive 'of the clans/people'); 'پیش' is an adverb 'forward/in front'; 'منهید' appears here in a poetic/archaic verb form immediately before the object 'پای' ('put/step [the] foot forward'). Overall the clause orders elements in a terse, inverted way typical of epic verse: numeral + group + directional adverb + verb + object = 'let not a single one step forward.'
segments.s657-g588: 'نباید' is an impersonal negative modal meaning 'must not / ought not'; it governs the subordinate clause introduced by 'که'. After 'نباید' the verb 'جنبد' (3rd person present) is understood in the sense of a prohibitive/subjunctive 'move/shift' ('should move' → here negated). 'پیاده' is an adverb/noun meaning 'on foot / the foot-soldier' describing who should not move; 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from'; 'جای' is 'place/position'. Together: 'Must not be that the foot-soldier moves from his place.'
segments.s657-g589: 'که' again links this clause into the reported speech. 'هرکس' is the indefinite relative 'whoever/anyone who'; 'تندی' is a deverbal noun/abstract meaning 'haste/rashness' (here adverbially: 'rashly' or 'in haste'); 'کند' is the present/subjunctive 3rd-person form 'does/makes' (here 'acts rashly'); 'روز جنگ' is a temporal noun phrase 'the day of battle' placed after the verb phrase as the time specification. The whole clause sets up a general statement: 'whoever acts rashly on the day of battle...'.
segments.s657-g590: 'نباشد' here is a negative copular/subjunctive meaning 'will not be / is not to be'; it predicates the qualities that follow. 'خردمند' is the predicate adjective 'wise/prudent'; 'یا' is the coordinating 'or'; 'مرد' is 'man' (here 'a man' / 'one'); 'سنگ' is the adjective 'stone' used figuratively in a compound 'مردِ سنگ' ('man of stone' — i.e. hard/firm). The construction is predicate after a negative existential/categorical verb: 'will not be wise or a man of stone.'
segments.s657-g591: 'ببینم' is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive used optatively or deliberatively ('let me see' / 'I shall see'), commonly used to introduce an indirect question or expectation. 'که' introduces the subordinate clause ('how...'); 'طلخند' here functions as the nominative subject — in the epic it is a proper name (in the lemma list the entry is a common noun, but in context it is the name of a person), and 'با این سپاه' is an accompanying prepositional phrase 'with this army' (preposition + demonstrative + noun).
segments.s657-g592: 'چگونه' is the interrogative adverb 'how' introducing the indirect question; 'خرامد' is an archaic/poetic third-person singular form meaning 'strides/proudly advances' (a classical verb; here future/prospective sense 'will stride'); 'به' is the preposition 'to/into/toward'; 'آوردگاه' is a compound noun 'place of bringing' used poetically for 'battlefield'. Together they form the indirect question clause: 'how he will stride to the battlefield.'
segments.s657-g721: ازین = از + این (poetic/contracted 'از این') meaning 'from/away from this'. مرز آباد is a noun phrase with adjective after noun (border + prosperous/inhabited). ما is the 1st person plural pronoun (subject). بگذریم is the 1st person plural present/subjunctive (classical/hortative) of گذراندن/گذریدن, here best rendered 'we shall/let us pass' or simply 'we will pass.' Word order places the adverbial/locative phrase before the verb.
segments.s657-g722: سپه را: سپه (army/host) plus the object marker را, marking the army as definite/direct object. همه modifies سپه ('the whole army'). پیش دریا is a directional phrase 'toward/in front of the sea' (پیش + noun). بریم is 1pl present/subjunctive of بردن 'to take/lead', here 'we will lead/bring.' The verb follows its object phrase, a normal classical Persian order.
segments.s657-g723: یکی 'one/a single' (numeral/determiner) + کنده used adjectivally ('detached/uprooted'—here 'a detached unit'). سازیم is 1pl present/subjunctive of ساختن/ساختن (to make/form) = 'we will form.' گرد سپاه is a postpositional phrase 'around the host' (گرد = around; classical Persian often places such phrases after the verb cluster).
segments.s657-g724: برین = بر + این (upon/against + this) functioning as 'against these' or 'on these'; جنگجویان is the plural 'warriors'. ببندیم is 1pl present/subjunctive of بستن 'to close/tie', used here in the sense 'to block/close off'; راه is the object 'road/way'. Together: 'we will block the way against these warriors.'
segments.s657-g725: ز دریا 'from the sea' (ز is a poetic variant of از). بکنده is a participial/adjectival form 'torn/uprooted' or 'the ones plucked'; here it functions as object/participle meaning those pulled from the sea. در آب افگنیم: در آب 'into the water' (در = into/in), افگنیم is an archaic 1st person plural form related to افکندن (classical verb 'to cast/throw'); here it means 'we will cast/throw.' The clause describes removing something from the sea and throwing it into the water (poetic/staged violence).
segments.s657-g726: سراسر 'throughout/entirely' (adverb). سر 'head' (literally, but here probably a synecdoche for men/people). اندر شتاب 'in/into haste' (اندر = in; شتاب = haste). افگنیم again the archaic 1pl of افکندن 'we will cast/make/put', here idiomatically 'we will throw everyone into haste' = 'we will put all into panic or rapid movement.'
segments.s657-g727: بدان is an imperative or hortative 'know/see to it' (compound of ب- + دان). تا here introduces purpose/result 'so that/in order that'. هرآنکس که بیند شکست is a relative clause 'whoever sees/observes defeat/the rout' (هرآنکس 'whoever', که relative particle, بیند 3rd sg present 'sees', شکست = defeat/rout). The clause sets up the condition for the following command.
segments.s657-g728: ز کنده 'from the kande/kandeh' (ز = from; کنده here = 'the detached unit' or 'the uprooted ones'). نباشد is a negative jussive/subjunctive 'let there not be' / 'must not be' (classical negative desire/command). ورا 'for him/thereby' or 'to him' (archaic oblique pronoun). راه جست is a verbal phrase 'to take the road / to flee' (راه = way; جست from جستن/جهیدن, used here as 'escape/flee'). Together the clause says 'let there be no way for him to flee from the detachment' — i.e. ensure no escape.
segments.s657-g369: 'که' introduces a subordinate clause ('that/for/as'), followed by the temporal marker 'تا' ('as long as/until') and 'چون' used as 'as/when'. 'بود' is past copula ('was'/'kept being'), so together 'که تا چون بود گردش آسمان' literally means 'for as long as the rotation of the sky was/continued' — an adverbial temporal clause. 'گردش آسمان' is a genitival noun phrase 'the turning/circuit of the heavens.'
segments.s657-g370: The relative/interrogative 'کرا' (archaic 'who/whom') is the subject/object reference; 'برکشد' is a present/subjunctive/past-future-looking verb form meaning 'would lift/raise' (literary). 'زین' is the direct object 'the saddle,' followed by the attributive phrase 'دومهتر زمان' ('the age's second/second-in-rank'), so the whole clause asks who would raise (hoist) him onto that saddle. Word order is verb after subject (archaic) and the noun phrase after the verb completes the action.
segments.s657-g371: 'همه کشور آگاه شد' is a simple past/resultative clause: 'the whole country/realm became aware.' 'زین دو شاه' is a preposed (archaic) modifier identifying 'these two kings' as the cause/topic of that awareness; note that 'شد' is the past-tense/perfective 'became.' The phrase order is subject ('همه کشور') + predicate ('آگاه شد') + explanatory/genitive phrase.
segments.s657-g372: 'دمادم' is an adverb meaning 'continually/constantly.' 'بیامد' is past tense 'came.' The sequence 'زهر سو سپاه' gives direction and theme: 'سو' = 'side/toward' and 'سپاه' = 'army/host'; together the clause means 'armies came from (that) side' or more broadly 'armies came from all sides.' The word 'زهر' as listed does not fit the usual sense here, so context forces a directional reading (hence -1 above).
segments.s657-g373: 'بپوشید' is past simple third-person singular ('he put on' / 'he donned'). It takes the following noun phrase as its object: 'طلخند جوشن' — 'جوشن' (cuirass/armor) modified by 'طلخند' ('bitter smile' used adjectivally or as an accompanying expression). 'نخست' ('first/at first') is an adverb marking sequence: he first put on the armor.
segments.s657-g374: This clause describes the subsequent action. 'به خون ریختن' uses the verbal noun/infinitive 'ریختن' ('to pour/spill') with the preposition 'به' functioning adverbially ('by/with'), so 'به خون ریختن' = 'by shedding blood' or 'in the shedding of blood.' 'چنگها را' (object marked by 'را') is 'his claws/claws (metaphorical for hands/swords)'; 'بشست' is past 'he washed.' Syntactically this is an object-action clause: '(by) shedding blood, he washed the claws.'
segments.s657-g375: 'بیاورد' is past tense 'he brought.' The following 'گو' is an enclitic/particle meaning 'so-called' or 'saying/thus' (literary) and can introduce reported/parenthetical speech; 'نیز' = 'also.' 'خفتان' here is formed from 'خفتن' (to sleep) and functions as a noun/participle 'the sleepers' or an adjectival phrase 'sleeping ones'; 'وخود' ('and himself') links this phrase to the following clause. The line uses compressed, literary coordination: 'he brought — the sleepers as well — and himself...' Note: 'گو' in older Persian often marks reported or parenthetical material.
segments.s657-g376: 'همی‌داد' is an iterative past form meaning 'kept giving' or 'gave continually.' The direct object 'جان پدر را' ('the father's life/soul') is marked by 'را', and 'درود' ('greeting/blessing') is the thing given. Word order is verb ('همی‌داد') after the coordinated subject/clause, then object ('جان پدر را درود') — 'he continued to offer salutations to his father's soul.'
segments.s657-g313: پرآشوب شد places an adjectival phrase before the verb شد (became): adjective + شدن is the standard copular pattern 'became X.' کشور is the noun 'realm/country' and سندلی follows as a postnominal identifier (likely a name or epithet). Word order is adjective → verb → noun phrase, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s657-g314: بدان here is the fused prepositional/particle sequence (ب + دان) introducing a cause or reference ('because of/with regard to that'); نیکخواهی is the abstract noun 'goodwill' and آن یک دلی is a short nominal phrase 'that one‑heartedness' (آن = 'that', دلی = 'one‑hearted/heartfelt'). The coordinating و simply links the two nouns.
segments.s657-g315: خردمند گوید is a reporting phrase 'a wise one says'; note that گوید is used as a verb 'says' though the provided sense list does not include that exact gloss. که introduces the reported content (that‑clause). در یک سرای is a locative phrase 'in one house' (در = in, یک = one, سرای = house/household).
segments.s657-g316: چوفرمان is the temporal/conditional 'چو فرمان' = 'when/if the command' (here treated as a fused unit); دوگردد is a present/subjunctive/optative form meaning 'becomes two/fold' (passive/intransitive idea of splitting), and نماند (negative of مانَد) means 'does not remain.' به جای is an adverbial phrase 'in place' (= 'in its rightful place'). The whole clause is conditional/consequential: when command is divided, nothing stays in place.
segments.s657-g317: پس آگاهی آمد is an impersonal/perfective clause 'then information/news came'; آمد is simple past 'came' and به marks the recipient. طلخند here functions as the recipient (likely a personal name), and the coordinating و + گو attaches the speech verb 'said' (گو = 'said').
segments.s657-g318: که introduces the content of the speech ('that'). هر is the distributive 'each/every.' برزنی is a compound/poetic noun here ('man of the field' or a type of man) and functions as the subject; بایکی is an adverbial expression 'as one/ together,' and پیشرو is a participial/adjectival word 'going forward/leading.' The clause states that each such man advances 'as one.'
segments.s657-g319: همه شهر ویران کنند is a verbal clause with an implicit plural subject ('they' or 'people/men') and the verb کنند 'do/cause' taking ویران as its object/predicative element – literally 'they make the whole city ruined' = 'they destroy whole cities.' از هوا is an ablative phrase 'out of whim/ desire,' giving the motive.
segments.s657-g320: نباید is an impersonal modal negative 'it must not be / ought not'; که introduces the subordinate clause. دارند here is present 'have/possess' and pairs with روا 'permissible' to express 'that kings possess such permission' (شاهان = kings). The sense is: 'It ought not be that kings have this permissibility.'
segments.s657-g1025: 'سه خانه برفتی' places the numeral + noun before the past verb in an older Persian word order: 'سه خانه' (lit. 'three houses/steps') is the measure/interval and 'برفتی' is the past verb 'went/advanced' (negative prefix absent here). 'شتر همچنان' follows as the subject + adverb: 'شتر' (camel) and 'همچنان' ('still, in the same way') qualifies the continuing state of the camels.
segments.s657-g1026: Here 'برآورد' is an older/periphrastic past verb meaning roughly 'brought up/put on' or 'caused to go' (listed senses offer 'accomplish/fulfil' as the closest match). 'گه' is a temporal/adverbial particle 'sometimes/at times'. The preposition 'بر' (on/upon) governs the following nouns 'دمان و دنان' (poetic/archaic body/garment terms); 'و' simply connects them.
segments.s657-g1027: This uses the older negative/existential pattern: the past verb with negative prefix 'ن' appears before the indefinite 'کسی' to mean 'there was no one who went'. 'پیش' = 'before/in front of' and 'رخ' here is a proper name (Rokh) followed by the epithet 'کینه‌خواه' ('the vengeful/avenging one').
segments.s657-g1028: 'همی‌تاختی' is an archaic imperfect/continuous-form with the old prefix 'همی' (like modern 'می'), indicating a habitual or continuous action in the past: 'he was charging/used to charge'. The clitic/pronoun 'او' is the subject 'he', and 'همه رزمگاه' (lit. 'the whole battlefield') is the object/extent of his onrush.
segments.s657-g1029: 'همی‌راند' is an imperfect/continuous past verb 'was driving/was urging on' (the 'همی' prefix marks habitual/continuous aspect). 'هر یک' = 'each one', and 'به میدان خویش' = 'to his own field'; note 'خویش' is formed of 'خوی' + enclitic 'ش' meaning 'his own'. Word order places verb before subject in this older register.
segments.s657-g1030: This repeats the older negative-existential pattern: the infinitive/masdar-like 'برفتن' ('to depart/return') followed by the negative past 'نکردی' and then 'کسی' means 'there was no one who did not depart' (i.e. nobody refrained). The phrase 'کم و بیش' is an adverbial idiom 'more or less'.
segments.s657-g1031: 'چو' introduces a temporal clause 'when'. 'دیدی' is an archaic past form (verb stem + final 'ی') meaning 'he saw' and is followed by the indefinite subject 'کسی' ('someone'). 'شاه را' uses the object marker 'را' on 'king', and 'در نبرد' means 'in battle'.
segments.s657-g1032: 'به آواز' means 'in a voice' or 'aloud' (the preposition 'به' + noun 'آواز'); 'گفتی' is the past reporting verb (archaic form meaning 'he said/called'), and 'که' introduces the quoted address. 'شاها' is the vocative 'O king!' and 'بگرد' is an imperative 'turn!' addressed to the king.
segments.s657-g209: من is the emphatic first‑person subject 'I' placed before the verb. ازگل برین گونه is a prepositional/adjectival phrase modifying مردم: ازگل ('from clay') + برین (poetic 'best/superior', here written as بر + the suffix ین) + گونه ('kind/type'); مردم is the direct object 'people', and کنم (present subjunctive/optative form of کردن) sits at the end as the verb 'I will make/fashion'. In short: 'I will make people (مردم) of a superior kind (ازگل برین گونه).'
segments.s657-g210: مبادا introduces a negative wish/fear 'may it not be / lest'; که begins the subordinate clause. نام پدر is a genitive phrase 'the name (i.e. reputation) of (my) father'. گم is an adjectival/predicative element 'lost', here combined with the light verb کردن (کنم) to yield 'I lose' or 'I cause to be lost' in the subjunctive mood triggered by مبادا.
segments.s657-g211: یکی here means 'a certain' (introducing an unspecified person) modifying مادرش ('his mother' — مادر + the possessive clitic ش). سخت qualifies سوگند ('a severe/solemn oath'), and خورد is the past form of خوردن used idiomatically with سوگند to mean 'took (an oath)'. Word order is [indefinite determiner + noun+possessive] ... [adverb/adjective + noun] + verb.
segments.s657-g212: که introduces the content of the oath: 'that ...'. بیزارم is the speakerless/subjective present 'I am loath/averse' (here the 'I' is the oath‑taker: 'I loathe'). از marks the object/target of loathing 'from/of', and گنبد لاژورد is a compound noun phrase 'the lapis‑lazuli dome' (a luxurious or azure roof).
segments.s657-g213: اگرهرگز is the conditional adverbial 'if ever'. این آرزو is the noun phrase 'this desire/wish'. خواستم is the past/conditional form 'I wanted' or 'I had wished' and here heads a protasis ('if ever I wished...') — in classical Persian past forms often express hypothetical/conditional sense.
segments.s657-g214: ز (poetic variant of از) marks source 'from/by'. یزدان means 'God' or divine providence. وبردل ('و بر دل' contracted) is a compound meaning 'and in/onto the heart' or 'and secretly in the heart'. بیاراستم is the first‑person past/conditional of آراستن 'to adorn/arrange' — here 'I would have adorned/established it', completing the conditional: 'I would have taken it from God and set it in my heart.'
segments.s657-g215: مبر is an imperative negative 'do not take' addressed to the addressee. زین here is a poetic contraction (used like از این, 'from/of this'); the supplied sense list doesn't match that function, so it is treated as the demonstrative 'this' in context. سخن 'speech' is the noun governed by مبر. جز به نیکی گمان is a prepositional restriction 'except to/with good thought' — i.e. 'have no opinion other than a good one.'
segments.s657-g216: مشو is the negative imperative of شدن 'do not become' (addressing the same addressee). تیز here carries the sense 'hasty/quick' (poetically 'swift' or 'rash'). باگردش آسمان is an ablative/causal phrase 'because of / with the turning of the sky' (i.e. the changing motions of heaven); together: 'do not be rash because of the heavens' movement.'
segments.s657-g945: کزین پس is a time phrase meaning 'from this (time) on' (کز + این + پس). نبیند is the classical negative present/future form (ن‑ + بیند from دیدن) meaning 'will not see' or 'shall not behold'. مرا is the object 'me' split here into م + را (the enclitic object pronoun + the definite/object marker), and مهر و گاه are the nouns that function as the semantic subjects/agents 'love/favor and time/occasion' that 'will not see'—poetic word order places the verb earlier and the agents after the object.
segments.s657-g946: نه ... و نه ... is the correlative negative 'neither ... nor ...' repeated before each item. The catalogue (اسب و گرز و تخت و کلاه) lists martial and regalia items—horse, mace, throne, helmet/cap—each joined by و; the whole phrase says these things are likewise absent.
segments.s657-g947: مگر here functions as 'unless' (introducing an exception). کین سخن = 'this hostile/vengeful speech (or grievance)'—کین modifies سخن. آشکارا کنم is a first-person present/subjunctive construction 'I make (it) apparent / I reveal (it) openly'; کنَم is the 1st-person verb 'I do/make'.
segments.s657-g948: ز here means 'because of / on account of'. تندی دل‑ت is a noun + 2nd-person possessive suffix (تندی = fierceness/irritability; دل + ت = 'your heart'). پرمداراکنم is a fused form meaning 'I will be indulgent / I will show leniency'—the speaker says he will spare the addressee for that reason.
segments.s657-g949: که introduces a clause ('for/that/so that'). او را is 'him' with the object marker را. بدست کسی is a prepositional phrase 'into the hand of someone' (بدست = 'by/in the hand of'). بد modifies زمان to form 'بد زمان' = 'ill/unfavorable time'; the whole clause predicts the man will come into someone's hands at an unlucky moment.
segments.s657-g950: که again introduces the result/consequence clause. مردم is 'the people' as subject, رهایی is 'release/freedom', and نیابد is the classical negative verb form (ن‑ + یابد) meaning 'will not find/attain'. ازان ('from that') refers back to the preceding misfortune; syntactically the verb follows the object in this poetic ordering.
segments.s657-g951: که introduces this clause ('that / so that'). یابد is the verb 'may obtain/attain' (present/subjunctive sense). به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in/within the world' (گیتی = the world), and رهایی ز مرگ means 'release / deliverance from death' (ز = 'from' in poetic register).
segments.s657-g952: وگر is the conditional 'and if / but if'. جان بپوشد is 'he covers / cloaks his life' (بپوشد from پوشیدن 'to put on, to clothe'), and به پولاد ترگ means 'with/into steel rags'—پولاد = steel, ترگ = rag/shred; the image is of donning iron scraps as armor. The clause sets up a hypothetical concession.
segments.s657-g929: نه ... نه ... is a correlative negation meaning 'neither ... nor ...'. The first clause has the subject من 'I' before the verb کشتم (past 'I killed') and the object او را ('him' with the definite-object marker را). The second half repeats the subject as a possessor (یاران من = 'my companions'), with the negation applying to the whole phrase.
