segments.s657-g930: نه گردی گمان برد is elliptical: گردی ('a mote/speck of dust') modifies گمان ('suspicion'), and برد is a verb meaning 'to take/possess/arise' in this idiom (here: 'took hold' or 'arose'). زین انجمن = ز + این انجمن ('from this assembly'), a poetic contraction (زین = از این). The word order is somewhat compressed for verse: the negation governs the whole clause ('nor did even a speck of suspicion come from this meeting').
segments.s657-g931: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for/that'). خود پیش او = 'himself in front of him / in his presence' (پیش here = 'in the presence of'). دم here means 'moment' (an instant). توان + زد is an auxiliary + main verb construction meaning 'was able to strike' (توان used as ability, followed by زد 'to strike/attack'); درشت modifies زد as 'a heavy/harsh/fierce [strike]'. Word order is typical classical Persian: modifiers and auxiliaries surround the verb, which completes the clause.
segments.s657-g932: ورا is the oblique pronoun 'him' (object). گردش اختر بد is a noun phrase 'the bad/ill-turning rotation of the star' — a poetic expression for unlucky fate (گردش = turn/rotation; اختر = star). بکشت is an archaic past form meaning 'killed' (the prefix ب- appears in older/perhaps dialectal forms before the past stem). The clause asserts that this unlucky celestial turn killed him.
segments.s657-g933: بدو گفت مادر = 'the mother said to him' with inverted / compressed poetic order: indirect object (بدو 'to him') + verb گفت ('said') + subject مادر ('mother'). که‌ای here introduces the direct speech and functions like a vocative/interjection ('O ...'); بدکنش is a compound noun meaning 'evil-doer' or 'malefactor' used as direct address.
segments.s657-g934: ز is the poetic form of از 'from'. چرخ بلند = 'the high heavens/lofty sky' (چرخ used poetically for heaven/fate); آیدت is the verb آید 'comes' with the enclitic ت 'to you' (literally 'it will come to you' or 'befall you'). سرزنش is the noun 'reproach' (the thing that comes). The clause literally: 'From the high heavens a reproach will come to you.'
segments.s657-g935: برادر کشی is the noun 'brother-killing' (fratricide); here کشی is a deverbal noun meaning 'killing'. از پی = 'in pursuit of / after' (literally 'from/for the sake of following'), and تاج و تخت = 'crown and throne' (objects of the pursuit). The whole phrase sets up the motive for the fratricide ('killing your brother for crown and throne').
segments.s657-g936: نخواند here is negative of خواند (from خواندن) used in the sense 'to call/name/consider'; with the negative it means 'will not call/consider'. تو را is the object 'you' (تو with the object marker را). نیکدل نیکبخت are predicate nouns 'good‑hearted, fortunate' — what one will not be called. Word order places the verb first (poetic inversion), then the object and predicate.
segments.s657-g97: بدیشان is a fused prepositional-clitic (به + ایشان) meaning 'to them' placed before the verb. سپرد is a past-simple 3rd-person singular verb 'he entrusted/handed over'. آن دو فرزند را is the definite direct object (آن 'those' + دو 'two' + فرزند 'sons/children') marked by را; the subject is omitted and retrieved from the surrounding context.
segments.s657-g98: This noun phrase is an appositive/descriptor for the object in the previous segment. دو places the numeral before the noun (two chiefs). مهتر نژاد is a post‑nominal attributive construction where نژاد ('lineage') qualifies مهتر ('chief, distinguished man'), i.e. 'chiefs of (noble) lineage'; خردمند is an adjectival attribute 'wise' following the noun. The final را repeats the object marking because the object is split across two coordinated/descriptive parts.
segments.s657-g99: نبودند is the negative past plural of بودن ('they were not'). The phrase ز ایشان جدا is a prepositional phrase 'apart/from them' (ز = from, ایشان = them, جدا = apart). یک زمان at the end is an adverbial intensifier 'for a single moment' or 'not even one moment', giving the duration/emphasis; the classical word order places the verb before these complements.
segments.s657-g100: بدیدار is an adverbial/relational form 'at the sight/of meeting' (essentially 'on meeting/at seeing'). ایشان is the object 'them' and شادمان is the predicate adjective 'joyful/happy'. شده here is a past/resultative 'became' (an epic/poetic form equivalent to 'شدند' in nearby lines); the subject is the same 'they' carried over from the previous clause.
segments.s657-g101: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. نیرو گرفتند is a verb phrase 'they took/gained strength' with گرفتند as past 3rd-plural. The conjunction و links the coordinate clause دانا شدند, where دانا is an adjective/noun 'learned/wise' and شدند is the past plural 'they became'—so the two actions are sequential/linked: when they gained strength and became learned.
segments.s657-g102: بهر دانشی is an adverbial phrase 'with respect to knowledge' or 'for the purpose/field of knowledge' (بهر = for/in regard to, دانشی = knowledge). بر here functions as a preposition 'in / with regard to' linking to توانا شدند ('they became able/capable'); توانا شدند is a past plural result 'they became capable', so the full phrase means they became competent/capable in matters of knowledge.
segments.s657-g103: زمان تا زمان is an idiomatic reduplication meaning 'from time to time' or 'as time went on'. یک ز دیگر literally 'one from the other' (یک = one, ز دیگر = of the other) expresses reciprocal separation, and جدا is the adjective/adverb 'apart' — together indicating occasional separation 'they were apart from one another'.
segments.s657-g104: شدندی is an archaic/epic past‑plural form 'they became / they were made' (used where modern Persian would have شدند). برمادر is a dative/locative phrase 'to (their) mother' (بر here = to/upon, مادر = mother) and پارسا is a post‑nominal adjective 'pious'; together this gives 'were (sent/put) to their pious mother' or 'ended up with their pious mother.'
segments.s657-g401: به here is an instrumental/circumstantial preposition meaning 'by/at' (sense 'by/-ly'); طلخند is a noun ('bitter laugh' or sneer) used as the cause/occasion; هرچند introduces a concessive clause ('although'); جانش is جان + ـش (possessive 'his life'); بسوخت is a past tense verb (perfective 'was burned/was consumed'). The clause order is concessive phrase: [by that sneer] + [although] + [his life was burned].
segments.s657-g402: ز is a poetic preposition 'from/because of'; خشم او = 'his anger' (noun + pronoun); دو چشم خرد را بدوخت should be read with the object marker را attached to the verb phrase: 'he sewed (shut) the two small eyes.' چشم = 'eye', خرد here means 'small' (adjective), and رابدوخت is the verb-form 'را بدوخت' (sewed [them]). The verb follows the object (classical Persian order allows variation); the clause gives the action caused by his anger.
segments.s657-g403: گزین کرد is a past tense compound: گزین (to choose) + کرد (did) → 'he chose'; مردی is the object 'a man' (indefinite); سخنگوی گو is a compound attributive: سخنگو ('speaker'/'spokesman') + گو as an adjectival element '-speaking' → 'an eloquent/speaking man.' Word order places the verb before the noun phrase (permissible in classical style) and the modifier follows the noun.
segments.s657-g404: کزان = 'from which / from among' introducing a source set; مهتران here means 'his chiefs/elders' (the supplied sense list is not the lemma for this word, so choose the semantic reading in context); او = 'his'; بدی پیشرو is an appositive/relative description: بدی = 'badness, wickedness' and پیشرو = 'foremost/leading' → 'foremost in wickedness.' The whole phrase modifies the chosen man (he was selected from among the chiefs, the one foremost in evil).
segments.s657-g405: که here begins a subordinate (relative/infinitival) clause ('that' / 'so that'); رو پیش طلخند combines رو (go/turn toward) + پیش ('before/in the presence of') + طلخند (the addressee/name or 'the sneer'); و links the two verbs; او را بگوی = 'tell him' with را marking the direct object (او) and بگوی representing an imperative/subjunctive form 'say/tell' in classical style. The clause functions as the purpose/mission given to the chosen man.
segments.s657-g406: که again introduces reported content ('that'); بیداد = 'injustice, unrighteous violence'; جنگ = 'war'; برادر = 'brother'; مجوی is a negative imperative/subjunctive 'do not seek' (second-person prohibitive form in classical Persian). Together this gives the content of the admonition: 'that you/he should not seek unjust war against his brother.'
segments.s657-g407: که introduces another clause ('that' / 'know that'); هر خون = 'every/any blood' (هر = each/any, خون = blood/bloodshed); که باشد is a subordinate relative or optative use of باشد ('that may be / that is'); برین (بر + این) = 'on this' or 'upon it' (poetic fused form); ریخته = participle/past form 'shed/spilled.' The phrase therefore means 'that whatever blood is spilled upon this [land/thing]...'.
segments.s657-g408: تو باشی is a present-subjunctive/optative 'you shall be / you will be' (classical second-person form); بدان = ب + دان here acts as 'to/for that' or 'in that' (imperative/infinitive idiom 'know that' previously introduced); گیتی = 'the world/this world'; آویخته is a past-participle/adjectival 'hung/attached' used figuratively: the result clause says the guilt of spilled blood will 'hang upon' you in the world. Overall the clause issues a warning about responsibility for bloodshed.
segments.s657-g1: چنین (an adverb) is fronted to mean “thus/so,” followed by گفت, the past tense verb “said.” The subject شاهوی بیداردل follows the verb (typical VSO/order in older Persian); شاهوی is an epithet/name and بیداردل is a postpositive adjectival epithet (“wakeful‑hearted”) qualifying it.
segments.s657-g2: که‌ای here functions as an archaic vocative/attention formula introducing direct address (roughly “O …”). پیر دانای is a compound noun phrase meaning “old/elder wise one” (پیر = elder, دانای = wise). و بسیار دل strings on an intensified epithet: و = “and,” بسیار = “very/very much,” دل = “heart” (i.e. “of great heart”); the whole segment is the addressee to whom the previous verb refers.
segments.s657-g3: ایا at the start is an old/poetic address particle (here functioning like “O” / calling attention). مرد is the head noun “man” (here vocative — a hero/valiant man), followed by coordinated adjectives فرزانه (wise) و تیز (keen/swift). ویر is a terse poetic epithet attached to تیز; the whole phrase is an address to the addressee of the imperative that follows.
segments.s657-g4: ز = “from” (poetic short form) introduces the source (ز شاهوی پیر = “from the venerable Shahuy”). این سخن is the direct object “this saying/speech.” یادگیر is an archaic/poetic imperative from یاد گرفتن (“to learn”): here it commands “learn!”; the verb is placed at the end as is common in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s657-g5: که is a relative/complementizer introducing the clause. درهند is a locative phrase written together (در هند = “in India”). مردی is an indefinite noun “a man,” سرافراز is a predicative adjective meaning “honored/proud,” and بود is the past‑tense copula “was.” The clause states existence/location in the past.
segments.s657-g6: This is a continuation relative clause describing what he possessed: با = “with,” followed by coordinated complements لشکر و خیل (two near‑synonymous words for army/host) and another با introducing ساز (“with instruments/music”). The clause closes with بود (past copula) repeating the verbal predicate; the repeated با emphasizes accompaniment/possession.
segments.s657-g7: خنیده is a perfect/past participle meaning “sung” (here functioning like a passive or resultative: “was sung”). بهر جای = “for/to every place” (i.e. everywhere), جمهور نام groups “public” + “name” — جمهور qualifies the realm in which the name is known, and نام (here better read as “reputation/name”) is the theme that was sung. Word order is poetic (verb/participle before its theme).
segments.s657-g8: به مردی can be read as a predicative/comparative phrase “as a man” or “to a man”; بهر جای = “to/for every place,” گسترده گام is a compound adjectival phrase meaning “broad‑stepping” or “of wide stride” (گسترده modifies گام). The phrase characterizes the man’s action/stance — he took wide steps everywhere; the syntax reflects poetic compression.
segments.s657-g25: پدر is the subject 'the father'. چون introduces a temporal clause here ('when/as'), placed before the verb. بدید is a past‑tense, 3rd‑person singular verb 'saw'. آن جهاندار نو is the direct object phrase 'that new sovereign/sovereign newly come' that completes the verb.
segments.s657-g26: هم 'also' links this action to what preceded; اندر زمان is an adverbial phrase 'in/at that time'. نام کردند is a past plural 'they named/gave the name' (کردند is the auxiliary 'did/made'). گو here follows نام کردند and functions as the reported name or speech particle ('called/said' → taken as the name 'Go' or a reporting particle), so the clause means 'they named him "Go" (at that time)'.
segments.s657-g27: برین is a fused form (بر + این) meaning 'on/after this'; the temporal phrase-level object بسی روزگار ('many days/a long time') precedes the verb. برنیامد is a negative past form 'did not come/occur' — here idiomatically 'it did not come to pass many days' → 'not many days passed'.
segments.s657-g28: که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/when'. بیمار شد is a two‑word predicate: بیمار 'sick' + شد 'became' (past). ناگهان is the adverb 'suddenly'. شهریار is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb for poetic order.
segments.s657-g29: به + کدبانو marks the indirect object 'to the lady/queen'; اندرز کرد is 'gave advice' (اندرز 'advice' + کرد 'did/made'). و connects the two coordinated actions; the following به مرد begins the prepositional indirect object for the next verb (to/for a man).
segments.s657-g30: جهانی here functions as a nominal/qualifying word for what is handed over (the realm/world) and is followed by the adjectival phrase پر از دادگو 'full of just (people)'. را is the definite object marker attached to that object, and سپرد is the past verb 'entrusted/handed over'. The overall structure is object (the realm/world described) + را + verb 'entrusted', while the recipient was introduced earlier by به مرد.
segments.s657-g31: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/because of'. خردی means 'smallness' i.e. youth. نشایست (negative of شایست) is an impersonal predicate 'is not fitting/appropriate'. گو is a parenthetical quotative 'they say' or 'it is said'. بخت را ('fortune' + object marker) is the theme being discussed — what it would or would not do.
segments.s657-g32: This clause lists the things fortune should not bestow: تاج 'crown', و 'and', کمر 'belt/girdle' (i.e. the girded sash symbolizing rule), followed by the infinitive بستن 'to bind/gird' used here in the sense 'to confer/put on', then و 'and' plus تخت 'throne' with the object marker را. The negation implied earlier (نشایست) scopes over this list: 'fortune should not bind/give the crown, the girdle, and the throne.'
segments.s657-g57: پری چهره is a compound adjective meaning 'fairy‑faced' (پری = supernatural beauty + چهره = face/appearance). آبستن آمد is a poetic/periphrastic past: آبستن 'pregnant' + آمد 'came' → 'became pregnant'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. The short phrase as a whole says 'a fairy‑faced (woman) became pregnant (from/of) ...'.
segments.s657-g58: پسر زاد is a frozen/idiomatic collocation meaning 'a son was born' or 'son‑birth' (زاد here marks birth/origin). از + ین (= ازین) = 'from this'. نامور کدخدای = 'renowned village‑headman' (modifier follows noun in Persian compounds). Together this segment supplies the origin/parentage: 'a son born from this famed headman.'
segments.s657-g59: ورا is the enclitic/old form meaning 'to him' (object/indirect object). پادشا نام طلخند کرد is a straightforward SOV order typical of classical Persian: 'the king (پادشا) name (نام) Talakhund (طلخند) did (کرد)' → 'the king named him Talakhund.' Here طلخند functions as the proper name given; it is written like a common noun in the lexicon but used as a name in context.
segments.s657-g60: روان را is the direct object with را (definiteness/accusative marker): 'his soul/spirit.' پر از مهر = 'full of affection' (پر = full (of) + از = of + مهر = love/affection). فرزند کرد is 'made (a) child' or 'made (him) a son' with کرد as the causative/making verb. The sequence states two connected actions of the king: he filled the man's soul with affection and made him his child.
segments.s657-g61: دوساله شد = 'became two years old' (دوساله 'two‑year‑old' + شد 'became/was'). این خرد = 'this little one' (این = this; خرد here = 'small/young' in a child sense). و گو هفت سال is an idiomatic glossing/parenthetical 'and (some) say seven years' — گو (say) introduces an alternate report. The clause gives the child's age, with an alternate age reported.
segments.s657-g62: دلاور گوی بود is a description: دلاور = 'valiant/heroic'; گوی here is used in an archaic/poetic way (cf. 'گوی' = 'they say' / 'so‑called') and بود = 'was'. با فر و یال = 'with far/royal glory (فر) and mane (یال)'—a standard epic pair describing radiance and heroic appearance. Overall: 'he was a brave man, endowed with glory and a mane (i.e. splendour).'
segments.s657-g63: پس از چند گه = 'after some time/a few occasions' (پس از = after; چند گه = some time/occasions). مای بیمار شد = 'the woman became ill'; مای here is an archaic/pronounal form referring to the woman in context (the provided lexical senses do not include this personal sense, so the sense is contextually irregular). شد is the past/resultative 'became.'
segments.s657-g64: دل زن = 'the woman's heart' (دل = heart/soul + زن = woman as a noun modifier). برو here functions idiomatically as 'toward him/for him' in older usage (not the literal verb 'go'), so the phrase دل زن برو پر ز تیمار شد = 'her heart toward him became full of care/solicitude.' پر ز تیمار = 'full of concern/care' (پر = full (of) + ز = of + تیمار = care). شد completes the resultative 'became.'
segments.s657-g937: چنین داد پاسخ is a compact classical phrase: چنین (thus) modifies داد (gave) and پاسخ (answer). The verb/giving-image داد (here 'gave') plus the noun پاسخ form the verbal phrase 'gave an answer thus'. که‌ای مهربان is a vocative address: که‌ای is an older/poetic form used to call someone 'O ...' and مهربان is the epithet 'kind'.
segments.s657-g938: نشاید is an archaic/poetic impersonal/modal verb meaning 'it is not fitting / it ought not'. It introduces a subordinate clause with که + subjunctive: که برمن شوی بدگمان = 'that you become suspicious of me.' برمن ('upon me' / 'of me') marks the target of suspicion; شوی is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of شدن ('become' / 'be') and pairs with بدگمان as the predicative adjective 'suspicious'.
segments.s657-g939: بیارام is an imperative/jussive addressing the addressee: 'be calm / do not be anxious' (classical poetic usage). تا introduces a purpose clause 'so that / until' and governs the following verb (نمایم). گردش رزمگاه is the noun phrase 'the movement/turn/course of the battlefield' (گردش = turn/course, رزمگاه = battlefield) and names what will be shown.
segments.s657-g940: نمایم is a 1st-person (optative/subjunctive) verb 'I may show / let me show' used as the main verb of the purpose clause introduced by تا. تو را is the addressee marked with the object/definiteness marker را (literary pattern 'show to you'); کار شاه و سپاه is the thing shown: 'the affair(s)/deeds of the king and the army' (کار = affair/deed; شاه = king; سپاه = army). Word order places the verb before the indirect object phrase 'تو را' and the direct object phrase follows.
segments.s657-g941: که introduces a relative/temporal clause ('when/which / that'). یارست is an old-form adjective/compound meaning 'a companion/one who is a friend' (here 'had become his companion/ally'), and شد is the past verb 'became/was'. پیش او is a prepositional phrase 'before him / in his presence'. رزمجوی is the noun 'combatant/warrior' describing the person in question; the clause describes a warrior who had become his companion and stood in front of him.
segments.s657-g942: کرا is an archaic relative/interrogative pronoun here functioning like 'which/that' (classical usage). بود is the past copula 'was'. در سر خود is a fixed phrase 'in his own mind' (سر = 'head' with the 'inner thought' sense; خود emphasises 'his own'). این گفت وگوی is the noun phrase 'this conversation' (گفت‌وگو split across words) and together the segment states that 'this conversation was in his mind.'
segments.s657-g943: به دادار is a prepositional/nominal phrase 'to/for the Creator' (here functioning as the head noun). کو is an archaic relative/connector equivalent to که ('who/which') introducing the relative clause. داد و مهر are nouns meaning 'justice and affection/love' (داد here is the noun 'justice', not the verb 'gave'), and آفرید is the past verb 'created' that takes داد و مهر as its objects—so the relative clause says the Creator 'created justice and love.'
segments.s657-g944: This is a simple coordinated object list followed by the verb: شب و روز و گردان سپهر = 'night and day and the revolving sky/heavens.' گردان is an adjective 'revolving/turning' modifying سپهر ('sky/heaven'). آفرید (repeated at the end) is the past verb 'created', with the enumerated nouns as its direct objects; the verb at the end is typical of Persian clause structure.
segments.s657-g713: که here is a conjunction meaning 'for' / 'that' introducing the clause. اندر دل من is a prepositional phrase 'in my heart' (اندر + noun + possessive من). جز از داد نیست is an archaic negative-equative order meaning 'there is nothing except justice' (جز + از + noun followed by نیست 'is not').
segments.s657-g714: مباد is an optative negative ('may it not be'). آنک is a subordinating demonstrative/conjunction ('that'); it introduces the following clause. از جان تو شاد نیست is the predication 'is not joyful concerning/for your life' — note the ezâfe is omitted in poetic diction (literally 'from your life, (is) not joyful').
segments.s657-g715: برینست is an archaic fused form of بر این است ('it is upon this'), so برینست رایم = 'my opinion is this'. که introduces the content of that opinion. دادم پیام is a simple past clause 'I gave/sent a message' (دادم = 'I gave/sent', پیام = 'message').
segments.s657-g716: اگر starts a conditional 'if'. بشنود is the (classical) present/subjunctive form of شنیدن and here means 'he hears' / 'should he hear' (the protasis). مهتر خویش کام groups the headman/leader (مهتر) with a possessive phrase (خویش = 'his own', formed here from خوی + ش) and کام = 'desire' — i.e. 'the chief's own desire'. The final result clause (fulfilled desire) is left implicit.
segments.s657-g717: ور is a conditional particle 'if'. ایدون (classical form of آمدن/آمدن) here means 'when it comes/appears'. که introduces the clause about the banner (رایت). رایت is 'banner/standard' and جز از جنگ نیست = 'is nothing except war' (negation following the exception phrase).
segments.s657-g718: به خوبی و پیوندت آهنگ نیست: به indicates direction/toward ('toward goodness and your bond'); خوبی = 'goodwill/kindness'; پیوندت is پیوند + enclitic ت 'your bond/union' (possessive clitic attached); آهنگ here means 'intention/ inclination' (not 'melody'); نیست negates the whole phrase: 'there is no inclination toward your goodwill and alliance.'
segments.s657-g719: بسازم is first-person subjunctive/present used for a future or volitional meaning 'I will make/prepare'. کنون = 'now'. جنگ را uses را as the definite/accusative marker with 'this war' as object. لشکری is the noun 'an army/troops' (here the thing being made). The word order is verb–adverb–object–noun, typical of older poetic Persian.
segments.s657-g720: که introduces a relative/clarifying clause 'which/that'. باید is the modal 'must / ought to'. سپاه is 'army, host'. The following items (m / را / کشوری) are given in the text as separate tokens: را is the object/definite marker and کشوری qualifies as 'of the country' or 'for the realm'; the intermediate 'م' in the manuscript here functions archaically as a clitic element tied to possession/existence and does not correspond neatly to modern spelling (treated here as part of the possessive/elliptical construction). Together the phrase means 'which must be an army for the country / that my realm needs.'
segments.s657-g841: The clause has the object pronoun 'تو' (you) fronted with the object marker fused into the following noun phrase 'راگردش اختر' — here 'راگردش' functions as 'the turning/rotation (of)'. The verb 'بکشت' is an archaic past form (prefix ب- + root) meaning 'killed' and comes at the end as normal in Persian; the adjective 'بد' modifies 'گردش' and means 'unfavorable/ill'.
segments.s657-g842: 'وگرنه' is the conditional conjunction 'otherwise'. The prepositional cluster 'نزد بر تو' (= near/on you) precedes the nominal predicate 'بادی درشت' (a 'coarse/heavy wind'); note the adjective 'درشت' follows the noun 'بادی' in this poetic order. The whole segment expresses the counterfactual alternative to the first clause.
segments.s657-g843: The verb 'بپیچید' (peichid with prefix ب-) means 'turned/twisted' and appears first (verb–agent–object order is used for poetic emphasis). The preposition 'ز' here indicates agency or source ('by/from the teachers'), and 'آموزگاران' is the agent; 'سرت' is the affected part 'your head' (object) placed after the agent for emphasis.
segments.s657-g844: This is two coordinated clauses: 'تو رفتی' — simple past 'you went/left' with the pronoun 'تو' and past verb 'رفتی' — followed by 'ومسکین دل مادرت' where the conjunction 'و' attaches to 'مسکین' ('miserable/poor') which qualifies 'دل' (heart) and the possessive suffix 'ت' on 'مادر' marks 'your mother'. Word order is adjective + noun + possessed noun-suffix cluster.
segments.s657-g845: 'بخوبی' (well/thoroughly) and 'بسی' (much/enough) are adverbial intensifiers modifying the verb 'راندم' (I drove/gave—here used idiomatically as 'I urged/gave repeatedly'). The preposition 'با' ('with/by') combines with 'تو' to indicate the addressee: 'I repeatedly gave (you) counsel.' 'پند' is the direct object.
segments.s657-g846: The verb 'نیامد' here means 'did not avail / did not come to (help)' and negates the effectiveness of the speaker's counsel. The object marker 'را' attaches to 'تو' to mark the patient ('you') and 'پند من سودمند' ('my advice useful') is the predicate quantified as not having occurred/effected change.
segments.s657-g847: 'چو' functions as the temporal conjunction 'when'. The compound 'فرزانه گو' is a poetic formation — 'فرزانه' (wise) with 'گو' ('speaking'/'one who says') functioning to name the sage or wise-speaker. 'بد' here is an adverbial/poetic particle signalling an adverse or notable event (translated roughly as 'thereupon' or marking ill-fortune), and 'آنجا رسید' is the past-tense verb phrase 'arrived there'.
segments.s657-g848: This clause is object–verb: 'طلخند' (a name/epithet literally 'bitter-laugh') is modified by the compound 'جهان جوی' ('world-seeking' = one who seeks the world), followed by the object marker 'را' and the predicate 'مرده دید' ('saw [him] dead'). The verb 'دید' final is the past 'saw/beheld'.
segments.s657-g201: بدین لشکر من فروان کسست = 'بدین لشکرِ من' is a prepositional/possessive phrase 'in/with this army of mine' (بدین = به + این, ‘with/this’; لشکرِ من = 'my army', note the possessive structure where من follows the noun). فروان کسست is a compact poetic clause: فروان (an adjective/adverb 'numerous/plentiful') modifies کس ('person(s)') and کسست is the contracted/poetic form of کس است ('there are people' / 'there is someone'); the copula -ست is attached to کس. Word order is topic (prepositional phrase) → comment ('there are many people').
segments.s657-g202: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'کس' ('who/that'). همسال ('peer, equal') here means 'one equal to him'; او is the pronoun referring back to that person. به آسمان is a spatial/figurative phrase 'in the heavens/sky' and کرکسست is the fused poetic form کرکس است ('is a vulture') with -ست copula attached; the clause literally says 'whose peer in the sky is a vulture.'
segments.s657-g203: که starts another relative clause 'who/that'. هرگز is the negative adverb 'never'. نجویند is a poetic present-tense form 'seek' (3rd person plural); here it governs the following objects 'گاه و سپاه' — 'گاه' = 'occasion/place/station' (here 'rank/station') and سپاه = 'army/force' — so the clause says 'who never seek station or an army.'
segments.s657-g204: An asyndetic list introduced by repeated نه ... نه ... ('neither ... nor ...') giving what they do not desire: تخت 'throne' (position of rulership), افسر here 'helmet' (the insignia of command; also 'officer' but physical helmet fits poetic usage), گنج 'treasure', کلاه 'cap/hat' (ornamental headgear). The nouns are simply coordinated; the negation continues from the previous 'هرگز نجویند'.
segments.s657-g205: پدر‌گر is a conditional expression 'پدر + گر' = 'if (the) father...' (گر is the old conditional particle 'if'). به‌روز جوانی is a temporal phrase 'on the day/at the time of youth' (به‌روز = 'on the day / of the day', جوانی = 'youth'). بمرد is an archaic/past verbal form 'died' (poetic form equivalent to مرد or 'he died'; the initial ب- appears in older forms). Together: 'If the father died in the time of youth...'.
segments.s657-g206: نه ... کسی راسپرد states the result: نه تخت بزرگی کسی راسپرد = 'no one was entrusted with the great throne.' Here نه begins the negation, تخت بزرگی 'the great throne' is the object, کسی functions as 'anyone/no one' (with the negation giving the meaning 'no one'), and راسپرد is the fused poetic form of را سپرد ('handed/entrusted'); راسپرد = را + سپرد.
segments.s657-g207: دلت = دل + -ت (your heart). جفت = 'a mate/pair' (a suitable match). بینم is first-person singular present 'I see' (بین + ـم). همی is a poetic present/progressive particle ('still/indeed' marking ongoing action) and سوی گو = 'turn toward him and say' (سوی = 'toward/to', گو = imperative or hortative 'say'/'tell'). Overall: 'I see a mate for your heart; go and tell him.'
segments.s657-g208: برآنی is a poetic/imperative-inflected form meaning 'bring forward' or 'cause to come forward' (here an instruction). که introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that'. او را is the object pronoun 'him' marked by را. کنی is 2nd-person present/subjunctive 'you make/do' (here 'you make him ...'). پیشرو is a predicate noun 'vanguard/leader/one in front'. The whole clause therefore: 'bring him forward so that you make him the vanguard.'
segments.s657-g753: This is a conditional clause: 'هر' is absent, so it begins with the subject 'جهانجوی' (an ambitious, fame-seeking man). 'اگر' introduces the condition. 'کشته گردد' is a passive verbal phrase ('is killed'), where 'گردد' is the subjunctive/present-form auxiliary meaning 'become / be' used in conditional contexts. The prepositional phrase 'به نام' follows and means 'with a name' i.e. 'leaving a name/fame'.
segments.s657-g754: This is the comparative clause: 'به از' functions as 'better than'; 'زنده دشمن' is a noun phrase 'an enemy who is alive'; 'بدو' is the clitic prepositional pronoun 'to him/unto him' (i.e. concerning the slain man); 'شادکام' is a predicative adjective 'rejoicing/fortunate'. Together: 'better than an enemy, alive, being joyful toward him.'
segments.s657-g755: A relative clause introduced by 'هر آنکس که' ('whoever/anyone who'). 'درجنگ' is the locative prepositional phrase 'in battle'; 'تندی' is a noun used adverbially meaning 'haste/hastefulness'; 'کند' is the present/habitual verb 'does' (here meaning 'hastens' or 'acts quickly').
segments.s657-g756: 'همی ... کند' is a classical habitual/continuous marker meaning 'continues to / does habitually'. 'از پی' is a fixed idiom 'in pursuit of / after' and takes the noun 'سودمندی' ('advantage, benefit') as its object; 'کند' again is the verb 'does' (i.e. 'seeks').
segments.s657-g757: 'بیابید' here is an optative/imperative-form verb 'you shall find / find' (classic subjunctive/imperative morphology). 'چندان' quantifies as 'so many/much'; 'ز من خواسته' is a past-participle relative phrase 'who have sought me' (with poetic 'ز' = 'of/from' introducing the agent of the desire).
segments.s657-g758: A nominative list: 'پرستنده' ('worshipper/devotee') and 'اسب آراسته' ('a horse/steed adorned'). The adjective 'آراسته' follows and modifies 'اسب' in typical Persian order; the two items form examples of those who 'have sought' the speaker.
segments.s657-g759: 'ز کشمیر تا پیش دریای چین' is a geographic range: 'ز' = 'from', 'تا' = 'as far as', and 'پیش' = 'before/in front of' used here to mean 'up to the shores of'; 'دریای چین' = 'the Sea of China'. This marks the extent of the following praise.
segments.s657-g760: 'به هر شهر' = 'in/at every city' (locative). 'برماکنند آفرین' is a verbal phrase meaning 'they bestow/utter praise' — 'برماکنند' is the verb 'they put/raise (upon us)' and 'آفرین' is the acclamation/praise they utter. The object 'ما' is implicit in 'برماکنند'.
segments.s657-g993: یکی is the numeral 'one' (here marking a single throne). تخت is the direct noun 'throne' (object). کردند is the past tense plural 'they made/they set up' (کرد+ند). از چارسوی is a prepositional/locative phrase: از 'from/of' + چارسوی 'the four sides' indicating placement ('at/on the four sides'). Word order is somewhat poetic: numeral + object + verb, then a locative phrase.
segments.s657-g994: دومرد is a noun phrase 'two men' (the beneficiaries). The adjectives گرانمایه and نیکخوی follow the noun (post‑nominal modifiers, typical in Persian) and are linked by و 'and'. These two adjectives describe the men: گرانمایه 'esteemed/precious' and نیکخوی 'good‑natured / well‑bred'.
segments.s657-g995: همانند آن is a simile phrase: همانند 'like' + آن 'that/that one' ('in the same way as that'). کنده is a noun/participle 'stump/uprooted (thing)' used as a concrete noun here, and رزمگاه is another noun 'battlefield'; they are joined by the conjunction و. The whole segment lists two items likened to a previous example.
segments.s657-g996: This clause contains the verbal phrase: آورده (past participle/verb) 'brought' with an adverbial/locative frame. بــروی here functions in the construction 'به روی' (on/upon / toward) and اندر 'in/front of' combine so that روی سپاه means 'before/upon the face of the army' (سپاه = 'host/army'). Grammatical subject is implied ('they'); the objects (the stump and the battlefield) are what was brought.
segments.s657-g997: بران is a locative adverbial 'on/thereupon' (pointing back to what was mentioned). تخت is the noun 'throne'. صدخانه کرده is a compound participial phrase: صدخانه (literally 'hundred‑chamber') used as a descriptive element and کرده (past participle of کردن) marks 'having made/formed' — together this describes a richly made/decorated throne. نگار here is a noun meaning an ornament/picture/decoration associated with the throne; syntactically the participle phrase modifies or describes that ornament/throne.
segments.s657-g998: صفی کرد is a verbal sequence meaning 'made a rank/array' (صفی is related to صف 'rank/row' and here acts as the object/descriptor of کردن). کرد is the past tense 'he/they made/did'. او is the third‑person pronoun 'he' (subject here). لشکر کارزار is the object phrase: لشکر 'army/host' + کارزار 'battle/campaign' (here 'army for battle'). The clause means 'he arranged/marshaled the army for battle.' Note: صفی is a nominal/derived form used as what was made (a rank/line).
segments.s657-g999: پس آنگه is a doubled temporal/continuative marker ('then' / 'after that', common in epic style). دولشکر is a compound noun 'two hosts/two armies'. The prefixed prepositions ز (written here attached: زساج و زعاج) mark origin/material or affiliation ('of/from saj' and 'of/from aj'); they coordinate two types of hosts with و connecting them. The phrase lists the two forces coming next in the narrative.
segments.s657-g1000: دو شاه is 'two kings' (dual/paired). سرافراز is a post‑nominal adjective 'high‑minded / lofty / triumphant' modifying شاه. با is the preposition 'with', introducing accompanying items. پیل 'elephant' and تاج 'crown' are coordinated (پیل وتاج) as possessions or accouterments of the kings. The whole noun phrase describes two eminent kings accompanied by elephant and crown.
segments.s657-g633: This is a negative imperative phrase built around مکن (“do not”): دست پیش مکن is an idiom meaning “do not make the first move / do not strike first.” به جنگ introduces the arena (‘in/into battle’) and برادر names the victim (‘your brother’). Word order is emphatic/poetic — the object and circumstantial phrase appear before the negative imperative.
segments.s657-g634: نگه‌دار here functions as an imperative (نگهدار ‘hold / restrain / keep back’). ز + آواز من is an ablative/causal phrase meaning ‘by / at my voice’ (i.e. at my command), and جای خویش = ‘(to) your own place’ (خویش = one’s own). The short pronoun ش is the possessive clitic attached to خوی (classical poetic spelling).
segments.s657-g635: همی + گفت expresses repeated or continuous speech (“kept saying”). این بدان گفت is a compacted phrase (ب = be- / be + آن → to that one) meaning ‘this one said to that one’; و آن هم بدین mirrors the construction in reverse (‘and that one likewise to this’). The short prepositional b- attaches to pronouns in poetic diction, producing the mirror structure.
segments.s657-g636: چودریای is a poetic fusion of چو + دریای (‘like a sea’), modifying خون (‘a sea of blood’). شد is the past-tense copula/verb ‘became,’ and سراسر زمین is the postverbal subject/extent ‘throughout the earth.’ The clause means literally ‘the earth became like a sea of blood.’
segments.s657-g637: یلانی is a plural noun ‘heroes/brave ones.’ The relative clause introduced by که + بودند (‘who were’) qualifies them; خنجر گزار is a compound: خنجر ‘dagger’ + گزار (here agentive/qualifying) meaning ‘dagger‑bearing / dagger‑wielding.’ The clause thus describes which heroes are meant.
segments.s657-g638: بگشتند is a simple past 3rd‑person plural ‘they turned / they returned.’ پیرامن کارزار is a circumstantial phrase ‘around/about the battlefield’ (پیرامن = around; کارزار = battlefield). The verb governs the following locative phrase.
segments.s657-g639: ز زخم دوشاه is an ablative phrase ‘from the wounds of the two kings’ (ز = from; زخم = wound; دوشاه = ‘two‑kings’, a compound indicating both kings). آن دو پرخاشجوی is an appositive or identifying phrase ‘those two quarrelsome/aggressive ones,’ inserted to name the sufferers/actors.
segments.s657-g640: همی here has a narrative/iterative sense (‘then / repeatedly / so’) and introduces the result: خون و مغز اندر آمد به جوی literally ‘blood and brain came into a stream’ — آمد used intransitively ‘flowed / came,’ اندر + آمد = ‘entered / flowed into,’ and به جوی = ‘into a stream.’ The word order places the flowing substance before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s657-g377: بدان تندی is an adverbial phrase meaning 'with that speed/so swiftly' (بدان = 'to/with that', تندی = 'speed/haste'). ازجای برخاستند is a past-tense verb clause: برخاستند is 3rd person plural past 'they rose/they sprang up' and ازجای ('from the place') is the adverbial source; the order is adverbial phrase → source → verb (typical classical Persian flexibility).
segments.s657-g378: همی is an emphatic/continuative particle here (literary) adding immediacy ('then/just/quickly'). پشت پیلان is a noun phrase 'the backs of the elephants' (پشت = 'back', پیلان = plural 'elephants') placed before the verb as object/affected locus. بیاراستند is 3rd plural past 'they adorned/arrayed', so the whole clause means they arranged/ornamented the elephants' backs.
segments.s657-g379: نهادند is the 3rd person plural past of نهادن 'to place/set' ('they placed'). برکوهه is a locative adverbial 'on the hill' (بر + کوهه), followed by پیل زین 'elephant saddle' (piled nouns in classical order); the verb precedes or follows noun phrases flexibly, here the past verb نهادند governs the following locative and object.
segments.s657-g380: توگفتی ('you said') is a parenthetical/prefatory clause in past tense. همی + جوید is a classical/perfective-present-stative pattern: همی gives an ongoing/emphatic sense and جوید is 3rd singular present/habitual 'seeks/searches'; زمین at the end is the subject ('the earth')—so literally 'you said; the earth keeps seeking a way.'
segments.s657-g381: همه دشت 'the whole plain' is the subject. پر + [نِسَبَتِی phrase] means 'full of ...' so پر زنگ وهندی renders 'full of Indian bells/the ringing of Indians' (زنگ = bell/ringing, وهندی = 'Indian'). درای is a participial/possessive formation here ('possessing' or 'having') that reinforces the sense 'was filled with'.
segments.s657-g382: همه گوش 'every ear' (or 'all ears') is the noun phrase; پر ناله کرنای is another 'full of ...' construction where ناله ('moan/lament') is governed by کرنای ('of the horn/trumpet'). Word order places the modifier phrase after the head noun, giving a compact poetic clause 'every ear was full of the trumpet's moaning.'
segments.s657-g383: به لشکر گه آمد is an adverbial phrase plus past verb: به لشکر ('to/into the army/camp') and گه ('time/place; here forming 'camp/occasion') with آمد 'came'. دوشاه جوان following the verb is the subject phrase 'two young kings' (دوشاه = 'two kings', جوان = 'young'), typical classical inversion where verb can precede subject.
segments.s657-g384: همه is 'all/everyone'. بهر بیشی is a purpose/benefit phrase 'for greater/more' (بهر = 'for', بیشی = 'greater/more'—here 'for greater deeds/advantage' in context). نهاده is the perfect/periphrastic participle 'having placed/arranged' and روان is a predicative adjective/adverb meaning 'moving/underway/ready'—together giving 'all, having prepared for greater things, set forth.'
segments.s657-g641: برین گونه is a frozen phrase meaning “in this manner / thus”: بر + ین (here ین is the old/orthographic form of این = “this”) + نمونه 'گونه' = “manner/way”. تا introduces a temporal clause ‘until’; خور ز گنبد بگشت = “the sun turned from the dome”, where بگشت is an archaic past form (prothetic ب- + گشت) meaning ‘turned/was turned’. Word order is S-V with the temporal marker تا fronting the clause.
segments.s657-g642: وز here is a written contraction equal to و ز (and/from); the phrase وز اندازه آویزش اندرگذشت literally reads “and beyond (its) measure its āvīz (pendant/suspension) passed.” اندازه = “measure/extent”; آویزش is a noun (pendant, that which hangs / a suspension) and اندرگذشت is a past verb meaning “passed away / perished.” The clause therefore says that that hanging/lasting thing went beyond measure and ended.
segments.s657-g643: خروش (subject) + آمد (past) = “a roar came/rose”; از دشت is a simple prepositional phrase “from the plain.” The second noun phrase آواز گو uses the adjectival/participial گو (from گفتن) to mean “a voice that spoke / a calling voice” – i.e. “a voice cried.” The conjunction و links the two nominal elements.
segments.s657-g644: که‌ای here functions as an exclamatory vocative (classical Persian کِه + ای) introducing direct address: “O ...!” جنگسازان is literal “war-makers” used as an epithet for warriors; گردان نو = “a troop / battalion that is new/fresh.” Word order is vocative followed by the addressees joined by و.
segments.s657-g645: هرآنکس که = “whoever / anyone who”; خواهد is the present/future third-person of خواستن meaning “wishes / wants.” زما here is a possessive/oblique form (‘of me / my’) placed before زینهار (an admonition/ warning), so زما زینهار = “my warning” (classical word order). The whole segment is the protasis: “Whoever wants [it], (let him) take my warning.”
segments.s657-g646: مدارید is a hortatory/imperative-form verb (plural) from مدارا کردن meaning “show forbearance / be lenient.” ازو = “toward/from him”; کینه = “hatred, grudge.” در here marks relation “toward/in regard to” and کارزدار is the agent/ steward (the person in charge). The clause is an injunction: “Be forbearing toward him; do not harbour a grudge against the steward.”
segments.s657-g647: بدان is the imperative ‘know/understand’ formed from ب + دان (the prefix ب- + دان ‘to know’); تا introduces purpose/result ‘so that’; برادر is the addressee/subject ‘the brother’ and بترسد is the present/subjunctive/future sense ‘may fear / will be frightened of’; ز جنگ = ‘of/from the battle.’ The clause expresses purpose/result: “Know this so that the brother may fear the fight.”
segments.s657-g648: چوتنها = چو تنها (“when/if alone”); بماند is the present/subjunctive or future sense ‘he remains / he is left’; نسازد here is a negative/future sense of ساختن ‘to make/do’ used with an object omitted but understood as ‘to make a pause’ (i.e. ‘he will not make / he will not cause’); درنگ = “delay / pause.” Together: “If he remains alone, he will not make a pause” → “he will not linger.”
segments.s657-g785: هوا شد is the basic subject-predicate: هوا (the air/atmosphere) + شد (became). ز introduces a causal/source phrase governing the following noun phrase; گرد here is a noun meaning ‘dust’ (not the verb 'to go about') and combines with سپاه as گردِ سپاه ‘the army’s dust’ (poetic word order). آبنوس is a postpositive adjective modifying سپاه = ‘the ebony / very dark host.’
segments.s657-g786: ز begins another causal phrase ‘because of/from’; نالیدن is used as a verbal noun (gerund) ‘the wailing/laments’ governing بوق (the horn) and, linked by و, آوای کوس (‘the sound/voice of the drum’). The conjunction و attaches the following noun phrase; the whole segment explains the cause of the air becoming dark.
segments.s657-g787: تو گفتی is reported-speech past: ‘you said.’ که introduces the content clause. دریا بجوشد uses بجوشد (subjunctive/optative form of جوشیدن) to report an expected/seeming event (‘would boil’ or ‘would seethe’); همی is an archaic/emphatic particle often following the verb to indicate ongoing or emphatic action.
segments.s657-g788: نهنگ is the subject ‘the whale’; اندرو is a fused locative 'اندرِ او' = ‘in it / in the sea’ (poetic contraction), placed after the subject. خون is the object ‘blood’ placed before the verb in poetic order; خروشد (خروشیدن) is an optative/energetic verb form ‘would roar/utter a roar’ and همی again adds continuous/emphatic coloring.
segments.s657-g789: ز governs the whole coordinated noun phrase that follows: زخمِ تبرزین و گوپال و تیغ = ‘from the wounds made by the tabarzin (battle-axe), the gupāl, and the sword.’ زخم here is used metonymically as ‘wounds inflicted by’ the named weapons; the conjunction و links the listed weapons. (گوپال here is used as a weapon-name in context; the provided dictionary senses do not list a weapon sense.)
segments.s657-g790: ز دریا برآمد is inverted, literal: ‘from the sea there rose/issued forth…’ برآمد is past ‘arose / emerged.’ یکی is an indefinite numeral ‘one / a certain’ modifying the following noun phrase; تیره is an adjective ‘dark/black,’ and میغ is a poetic word for ‘blade’ — so the phrase gives ‘one dark blade’ as the thing that emerged.
segments.s657-g791: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning ‘when.’ بر چرخ خورشید is poetic ordering: بر (on/upon) + چرخ (the sky/wheel) + خورشید (the sun) — ‘the sun upon the sky.’ دامن کشید is an idiomatic poetic image ‘drew its mantle/hem’ (کشید is the past of کشیدن ‘to draw/spread’ used metaphorically); together: ‘when the sun drew its mantle over the sky.’
segments.s657-g792: چنان شد = ‘it came to be so / it became such.’ که introduces the result clause. کس نیز کس را ندید is a compact negative result: کس (anyone/person) + نیز (even/also, here emphatic) + [کس را] object marked by را + ندید (negative past form ‘did not see’): literally ‘no one saw any other’ → ‘no one could see another.’ The negative is contained in ندید (ن‑ + دید).
segments.s657-g537: بگفت (past) + آنچ + بشنید forms a clause 'he said what he had heard' where آنچ = 'that which' (relative) precedes the reported content; بشنید is past-tense 'he heard'. The conjunction و links to بفزود, which is the past of افزود (archaic/poetic), meaning 'he added', and نیز reinforces 'also/too'.
segments.s657-g538: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing a list of items. شاهی here refers to 'kingship/royalty' or gifts/attributes of royal rank; the items are coordinated by و: 'and treasure and dinar and thing' (چیز = other things). The repeated ز before گنج is a stylistic reiteration of the preposition.
segments.s657-g539: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is past 'he heard'. طلخند is the person (name) functioning as subject, followed by the object گفتار اوی 'his speech' (اوی = his/her, possessive). The clause literally: 'When Talxand heard his speech...'.
segments.s657-g540: This nominal list (خردمندی و رای و دیدار اوی) supplies qualities perceived: 'wisdom, counsel, and appearance/meeting (of) him' with اوی marking possession. Classical Persian often omits an explicit verb here (elliptical 'he found/perceived').
segments.s657-g541: ازان (= از آن) functions as a discourse connector 'from that / since'. کآسمان is the fused clause marker + آسمان ('that the sky'); را is the definite/object marker used here for emphasis with آسمان; دگر 'another/different' modifies راز 'secret'; بود is past 'was'. Literally: 'From that that the sky had another secret.'
segments.s657-g542: بگفت introduces reported speech 'he said'. برادر can be read as the noun 'the brother' (either subject of the following clause or a reported referent); نیامد is negative past 'did not come'; فراز is an adverb/noun 'to the summit/above'. Together: 'he said (that) the brother did not come up.'
segments.s657-g543: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase 'he gave such an answer'. The conjunction که introduces the reported content. گو is an imperative 'say', and رابگوی (را + بگوی) is an exhortation meaning 'tell it' or 'go tell (him)' with را as the object marker attached to the verb.
segments.s657-g544: که = 'that'; هرگز = 'never'. مبادی جزا is a genitival phrase: مبادی (plural 'entrances/approaches/gateways') of جزا ('punishment/recompense'), i.e. 'the gates/sources of punishment'. ز = 'from'; چاره جوی is a compound 'searching/looking for a remedy' (جوی here 'seeking'). The clause is elliptical: 'that the entrances of punishment are never sought from a remedy.'
segments.s657-g1009: ابر here is metaphorical 'cloud' meaning a mass or host; دست شاه is a poetic nominal group 'the king's hand/force' (dast = power, retinue). از دو رویه دو پیل expresses distribution: 'from / at two sides, two elephants' — دو رویه = 'two sides/flanks' (poetic); پیل is 'elephant'. Word order omits an explicit ezāfe (ابرِ دستِ شاه) as is common in verse.
segments.s657-g1010: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' linking the following description to the elephants; پیلان is the plural 'elephants'; شده is a participle 'become/made' (passive/resultive) describing their state; گرد here functions adjectivally (poetic) as 'rounded/rounded-in-form'; همرنگ + نیل is a compound 'the same color as indigo' with نیل = indigo. The whole phrase qualifies the elephants' appearance.
segments.s657-g1011: دو اشتر is 'two Ashtar' (اشتر is given here as a proper name in the gloss); بر پیل کرده is a verbal phrase 'made/placed on the elephant' with کرده as the past participle/causative marker following the preposition بر+پیل; به پای is a locative 'at the foot/base of' (literal or figurative). The sequence places these two named figures on/near the elephant.
segments.s657-g1012: نشانده is a past participle 'seated/placed' (he had seated/placed); برایشان = 'for them' (برای + شان = for their benefit/for them); دو پاکیزه رای is a noun phrase 'two clean/pure counsels/advisers' — پاکیزه 'clean, upright' modifying رای, here used as 'plan/counsel' or 'counsellor' in context. Word order is typical Persian: participle + indirect object + object phrase.
segments.s657-g1013: به زیر is the prepositional phrase 'beneath/under'; شتر = 'camel' is the object of that phrase. در here functions poetically to introduce what is 'in/under' (literally 'in/among'): 'در دو اسب و دو مرد' = 'two horses and two men (were) there'. The clause lists the entities positioned under the camel.
segments.s657-g1014: که is a relative pronoun 'who/which/that' linking to 'دو مرد'; پرخاش جویند is a present-tense verbal phrase 'they seek aggression' (جویند = they seek/pursue, so together 'they seek to attack / provoke'); روز نبرد is a temporal/adverbial phrase 'on the day of battle'. Together the clause qualifies the two men as aggressive on battle-day.
segments.s657-g1015: مبارز is the noun 'combatant/fighter'. دو رخ is a descriptive phrase 'two-faced' or 'two-faced/with two faces' (poetic epithet); بر دو روی دوصف stacks prepositional and nominal modifiers: بر ('on/upon') + دو روی ('two sides/faces') + دوصف (two rows/ranks). The sequence lists their disposition: opposing on two sides in two ranks. Word order reflects compressed poetic coordination.
segments.s657-g1016: ز is the preposition 'from'; خون جگر is a genitive 'blood of the jigar' where جگر is used metaphorically as 'heart/seat of feeling' (blood of the heart); بر لب آورده is a verbal phrase 'brought to the lip' (آورده past participle 'brought/has brought'); کف is 'foam' — i.e. foam at the mouth. The phrase describes that foam (mixed with heart's blood) had risen to their lips.
segments.s657-g289: وزان پس is an adverbial phrase meaning 'from that time / after that' (poetic order). فرستیم is a 1st-person-plural past tense 'we sent' with an implied subject 'we/they'. یک‌یک precedes the noun as a distributive quantifier 'each / one by one', and پیام is the direct object 'a message'.
segments.s657-g290: مگر here introduces the following clause expressing purpose/hope (classical/poetic usage) rather than its common senses; شهریاران is the plural subject 'the kings', and بیابند is a 3rd-person-plural subjunctive/optative form of یافتن 'to attain/find' meaning 'might attain'. کام is the object 'wish / fulfillment' — together بیابند کام = 'may have their wishes fulfilled'.
segments.s657-g291: برفتند is past plural 'they departed / went away'. ز + ایوان ژکان is a prepositional phrase 'from the iwan of Zhakan' (ژکان is a proper/obscure name here). The conjunction و links to دژم, a predicative adjective 'sullen/grim' that describes the same subject (elliptical copula omitted in poetry).
segments.s657-g292: This is a parallel adjective phrase: 'لبان پر ز باد' = 'lips full of wind' and 'روان پر ز غم' = 'souls/full spirits full of grief'. پر followed by ز + noun is the idiom 'full of X'. The two noun + predicate units are joined by و in coordinated parallelism.
segments.s657-g293: بگفتند is the reporting verb 'they said'. کین here is an archaic/dialectal demonstrative 'this/that' (classical usage) rather than the common meaning 'vengeance' — it introduces the noun کار 'this affair'. با رنج گشت is 'became with hardship' i.e. 'turned into hardship' where گشت is past 'became/turned'.
segments.s657-g294: ز دست = 'from the hand/of the control of' (idiom meaning 'out of someone's power'). جهاندیده ('worldly‑wise') specifies whose hand; اندر گذشت is a compound poetic phrasing 'passed away / slipped out (of control)', with گذشت the past verb 'passed'.
segments.s657-g295: برادر is a vocative 'O brother' placed at the front. ندیدیم is the past negative 'we did not see', and هرگز reinforces the negation 'ever/never'. دو شاه is the object phrase 'two kings' — the clause says 'we never saw (such) two kings'.
segments.s657-g296: This continues the enumeration: دو دستور 'two (duos of) دستور' with بدخواه as an adjective 'ill‑wishing / hostile' modifying دستور; در پیشگاه is the locative 'in the presence (of the court/king)'. The repetition of دو before each noun is a poetic device for emphasis/parallelism.
segments.s657-g233: شنیدم که introduces a subordinate clause: شنیدم is the 1st-person past ('I heard'). که is the conjunction 'that' starting the reported content. جمهور چندی places the subject 'the people/public' with چندی ('for a while') as a temporal modifier. ز مای is a comparative construction: ز here marks the standard ('than') and مای is the 1st-person plural clitic/noun 'we/us'.
segments.s657-g234: سرافرازتر is a comparative adjective ('more lofty/proud') that completes the comparison begun by ز مای in the previous segment. بد here functions as an adverb 'badly/ill' modifying the following prepositional phrase. به سال is an adverbial phrase ('with regard to the year/age') and و برای links this clause forward (برای begins a reason/purpose phrase that continues into the next line).
segments.s657-g235: پدرت is the noun 'father' with the 2nd-person possessive suffix -ت ('your father'). آن گرانمایه نیکخوی is an appositive description: آن ('that/the') + گرانمایه ('esteemed/precious') + نیکخوی ('kind‑hearted, well‑bred') describe the father.
segments.s657-g236: نکرد is the simple past negative 'did not do'; ایچ is an emphatic negative adverb ('not at all' / 'nothing'), so together 'did nothing at all'. ازان links this negated action to the following object (literally 'of that' / 'regarding that'), پیش is 'before/previously' and تخت آرزوی is a genitive-like compound 'the throne of desire' (i.e. the coveted throne) — so the clause means he never sought that throne.
segments.s657-g237: نه is a sentential negator here modifying ننگ ('shame'). آمدش is the past verbal phrase 'came to him' with the clitic -ش 'to him'. هرگز ('ever/never') is a negative adverb reinforcing the negation. از کهتری uses از to mark source/cause ('from/in consequence of') and کهتری is a nominalized adjective 'inferiority/being lesser' — altogether 'no shame ever came to him from being inferior.'
segments.s657-g238: نجست is the past form (here with the sense 'sought/strived for'). ایچ is the emphatic negative 'at all' (so 'did not at all seek'). بر introduces the standard/target of the seeking ('over/against'). مهتران is plural of 'greater/elders/chiefs' (here 'the great/greater ones'), and مهتری is the abstract 'preeminence' — 'he did not at all seek preeminence over the great.'
segments.s657-g239: نگر is the imperative 'look' or 'consider'. تا here introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'in order that'). پسندد is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive 'may approve/will deem fit' (used after تا to indicate the outcome the speaker seeks), and چنین دادگر is the noun phrase 'such a just man/ruler' that is the subject of پسندد.
segments.s657-g240: که introduces the content clause explaining what approval is sought. من is the 1st-person subject 'I'. پیش کهتر is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of inferiors' (پیش = before, کهتر = inferior). ببندم is the 1st-person singular present-subjunctive of بستن used here as 'that I should gird/tie' (i.e. 'gird my loins' = submit), and کمر is the object 'waist/loins' of that verb.
segments.s657-g465: This is a compact past-clause: 'غمی شد' (became sorrow) is the predicate in past tense, with 'دل گوچو' (Gocho's heart) as the subject/affected noun. 'پاسخ شنید' is a trailing temporal clause ('when he heard the answer'); the implied subject of شنید is گوچو. Word order is poetic (predicate then temporal clause) but the meaning is 'Gocho's heart became sorrowful when he heard the reply.'
segments.s657-g466: 'که' introduces an explanatory/reason clause ('for/because'). The phrase 'طلخند را رای پاسخ ندید' is literal: 'he did not see (ندید) a رای (plan/counsel) as a پاسخ (reply) for/against طلخند.' In poetic syntax the object marked by 'را' (طلخند را) is placed before رای and پاسخ; رای functions as 'counsel/plan' and the whole string expresses 'he found no counsel to answer (or deal with) طلخند.'
segments.s657-g467: 'پر اندیشه فرزانه' is an adjectival phrase meaning 'the sage full of thought' (پر = full of; placed before اندیشه). 'را' is the definite/object marker attaching to the following object phrase, and 'پیش خواند' is a compound: پیش (forward/in the presence) + خواند (called/summoned, past). Together: 'He called forward the thoughtful sage.'
segments.s657-g468: 'ز' (= از) marks source/relation ('from' or 'in answer to'). 'پاسخ فراوان' = 'an ample/plentiful answer.' 'سخنها براند' uses براند (from راند) poetically as 'drove forth' = 'uttered' or 'set forth' many words; thus the clause means 'from that ample answer he put forth many words' = 'he spoke at length.'
segments.s657-g469: 'بدو گفت' is a short clause meaning 'he said to him' (بدو = to him; گفت here functions as 'said'). 'کای' is the vocative 'O' addressing someone directly. 'مرد فرهنگ جوی' is a noun phrase: مرد (man) modified by فرهنگ جوی ('seeker of learning' — فرهنگ = learning/culture, جوی = seeker/searcher). The whole string is a direct address: 'He said to him, “O man seeking wisdom/learning,”'.
segments.s657-g470: 'یکی' here is an indefinite numeral/particle meaning 'one' or 'some' (i.e. 'a single/one'). 'چاره کار' is a genitival/construct phrase 'the remedy/solution of the affair' (چاره = remedy, کار = affair). 'با من بگوی' is an older/poetic way to say 'tell me' (با functioning like a dative 'to'; بگوی is an subjunctive/imperative form used here as 'say/tell'). So the line requests 'Tell me one solution to the matter.'
segments.s657-g471: 'همه دشت' is the subject 'the entire plain.' 'خونست' is a poetic predication meaning 'is blood/bloodied' (i.e. covered with blood). The coordinate 'و' joins the next phrase 'بی تن سرست' where 'بی' = 'without', 'تن' = 'body', and 'سرست' states 'are heads' (poetic inversion of 'سرها بی‌تن‌اند' — 'heads are without bodies'). The line describes battlefield carnage: the plain is blood and heads lie separated from bodies.
segments.s657-g472: 'روان را' (souls, with را marking the theme) is the thing undergoing passage. 'گذر بر' here means 'the passing/transference to' and 'جهانداورست' names the destination/agent — 'the World's Arbiter' (an epithet for the divine judge). Grammatically the clause states that the souls' passage is onto/into the care/judgment of the world's arbiter; 'ست' supplies the copula 'is' in this compact poetic form.
segments.s657-g41: همه (all/every) precedes the noun پادشاهی (kingship) to quantify it; شود is the present/optative 3rd‑singular of شدن used here as 'becomes' or 'will be', and پرگزند is an adjective formed with the prefix پر‑ ('full of') + گزند ('harm'), so the clause literally says 'all kingship becomes full of harm.'
segments.s657-g42: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'; شهریاری is a noun meaning 'sovereignty/royal rule'; نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive third‑person of بودن ('not be' / 'there be not'), here meaning 'there is not'; بلند is an adjective 'lofty' or 'exalted' modifying شهریاری, so the clause means 'if there is no lofty sovereignty.'
segments.s657-g43: به دنبر is a locative phrase ('to/at the rear') with به + دنبر; برادر بد is the noun 'bad brother' (adjective after noun); آن شاه را uses را as the definite/object marker attached to 'that king' — in this poetic line the placing verb is deferred, so this segment supplies the object and its locative ('[they placed] that king's bad brother at the rear').
segments.s657-g44: خردمند و شایسته is a coordinated adjective phrase ('wise and worthy') modifying گاه ('place/post/seat'); the final را again marks that phrase as the definite object of an implied verb (the verb 'placed' appears elsewhere), so this cluster reads '[they placed] the wise and worthy one in the seat.'
segments.s657-g45: کجا is the interrogative 'where'; نام is the subject here meaning 'name' in the sense of 'reputation/fame'; آن نامور is a determiner + adjective 'that renowned'; مای is an archaic/ambiguous word (here functioning as a referent tied to 'that renowned one'); بود is the past copula 'was', so the clause asks 'Where was the fame/reputation of that renowned one?'.
segments.s657-g46: به دنبر is again a locative 'at the rear'; نشسته is a past participle/adjectival verb 'seated'; دلارای is a substantive/adjective meaning 'beloved' or 'charming' and is the logical subject of بود, and بود is the past copula 'was', so this clause states 'the beloved was seated at the rear.'
segments.s657-g47: جهاندیدگان is a plural subject 'worldly‑wise people/experienced men'; یک به یک ('one by one') is an adverbial expression placed after it; شاه‌جوی is a compound attributive noun 'seeker of kingship' describing those men, so this nominal group reads 'the worldly‑wise, one by one, (as) king‑seekers.'
segments.s657-g48: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; سندل 'sandal' is the noun following it; به دنبر is a directional/locative phrase 'to/at the rear'; نهادند is the past 3rd‑person plural of نهادن 'they placed/laid (down)'; روی here means 'face' and functions as the object — together the clause describes that they 'laid their faces down, from the sandal to the rear' (i.e., a gesture of prostration), with the verb in the past plural tying the motion together.
segments.s657-g921: که introduces a relative/clausal clause ('who/that'). سوزد is a poetic/present-tense form meaning 'burns' or 'was burned' (here best read as 'burned/sets on fire'); تن خویش is 'his own body' with خوی + possessive ش (the suffix ش = 'his'). به آیین هند is an adverbial phrase 'in the manner/rite of the Indians' (به + آیین = 'in the rite/custom of' + هند = 'India/Indian').
segments.s657-g922: ازان = 'from that' or 'thereupon'; سوگ here denotes the mourning/rite of mourning. پیداکند is a classical compound verb 'finds/comes upon' but in context means 'learned/accepted/adopted' (he came to possess). دین هند = 'the religion/faith of India'. The whole segment links as the result/consequence of the previous clause.
segments.s657-g923: چو = 'when' (temporal). از مادر آگاهی آمد = 'word/news/information came from the mother' (آگاهی = information; آمد = 'came' in simple past). بگو here is parenthetical 'بگو(یند)' = 'it is said' (a narrative formula), not an imperative.
segments.s657-g924: برانگیخت is past tense 'he roused/rose' (transitive or reflexive); آن باره = 'on that occasion' (آن = 'that', باره = 'occasion/instance'); تیزرو is an epithet 'swift‑running/fast one' and functions as the subject following the verb (classical Persian often permits V–S order).
segments.s657-g925: بیامد = 'he came' (simple past). ورا = 'to her' (ورا = به او/آن را). تنگ در بر گرفت is an idiomatic sequence: تنگ = 'tight/close', در بر گرفتن = 'to take into one's arms' (here گرفت is the past verb 'took'). Word order shows two coordinated actions: he came, and (then) took her close in his embrace.
segments.s657-g926: پر از خون modifies مژه = 'eyelashes/eyes full of blood' (پر از = 'full of'). خواهش = 'plea/request' and اندر گرفت = 'took into / accepted' (اندر = 'into/within'); together the clause means he accepted/gave in to the plea while his eyelashes were full of blood (emotion/tears/blood). The subject is understood from the previous clause.
segments.s657-g927: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'; گفت here introduces direct speech). کای مهربان is a vocative: 'O kind one' (کای = archaic vocative 'O'). گوش دار is an imperative formula 'give ear / pay attention' (literally 'have ear'), here addressed to the addressee.
segments.s657-g928: که introduces the content clause 'that...'; ما بیگناهیم = 'we are innocent' (بیگناهیم = predicate with first‑person plural). زین کارزار = 'of/from this battle/conflict' (زین = archaic 'this/of this', کارزار = 'battle/conflict'); the clause gives their plea/exculpation.
segments.s657-g857: سپاس از جهان آفرینت یکیست is a nominal predication: 'سپاس' (thanks) is the theme, followed by the prepositional marker 'از' linking it to 'جهان آفرینت' ('the world's Creator'). 'آفرینت' here functions as the creator/creative stem tied to 'جهان' (a genitive construction), and 'یکیست' is a copular predicate meaning 'there is one' / 'it is one' (literal 'is one').
segments.s657-g858: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause explaining that one thing. طلخند is the subject ('the bitter laugh/wry smile'), بر دست تو is a prepositional phrase marking the agent/instrument ('by/at your hand'), and کشته نیست is a negated perfect/passive construction: past participle + نیست = 'has not been killed' / 'is not slain'.
segments.s657-g859: همه بودنی is a nominal phrase 'all that is / all existents' serving as the object. گفته بودم is the past perfect (past participle گفته + auxiliary بودم) meaning 'I had said / I had told.' به شاه is the indirect object 'to the king.' Note the participial construction: گفته (past participle) + بودم = 'I had told.'
segments.s657-g860: ز introduces the list in an ablative/pertaining sense ('concerning / about' in poetic usage). The following coordinated proper names (کیوان، بهرام، خورشید، ماه) are items of that list, joined by و ('and'). Word order is a simple enumerative series after ز.
segments.s657-g861: که begins a subordinate clause. چندان ('to such an extent') is an adverbial degree modifier of the verb پیچید (past 'twisted/turned'). The phrase برزم likely refers to 'in/over my battle or host' (object/affected domain), and این جوان is the agent/subject appearing after the verb because of poetic word order (verb before subject).
segments.s657-g862: که again introduces the continuation: برخویشتن ('upon himself / to his own self') is a reflexive prepositional phrase; بر سر ('upon the head') marks the locus of the action; آرد is a third-person present/habitual or narrative present of آوردن 'brings' (here best rendered 'would/shall bring'); زمان ('Time') is the logical subject placed at the end for emphasis (poetic order).
segments.s657-g863: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now.' کار طلخند is a genitive construction 'the affair/work of the bitter smile' serving as the topic/subject. چون is the simile marker 'like/as,' and بادگشت ('wind-change' / 'reversal of wind') is used predicatively to describe that change (i.e., 'turned like a shifting wind').
segments.s657-g864: بنادانی and تیزی are two coordinated nouns ('foundations' and 'sharpness/swiftness') joined by و. اندر is a preposition meaning 'in/within' and گذشت is the past verb 'passed' (narrative past), so the clause says these things 'passed away' or 'went by within/among.'
segments.s657-g881: ازان پس چو is a temporal phrase: ازان 'from/thereupon' + پس 'after', then چو 'when' introduces a subordinate temporal clause. دانندگان را uses دانندگان (plural agent 'the knowers/wise men') with را as the definite/direct-object marker (here marking the people summoned). بخواند is a past-subjunctive/classical narrative form meaning 'he called/summoned' (here causative/active 'had them called' or simply 'called').
segments.s657-g882: به مژگان بسی خون دل is a prepositional/nominal phrase giving what was scattered: به here functions as 'upon/to' (indicating location 'on his lashes'), مژگان 'eyelashes' is the locus, بسی 'very/much' (classical adverb), خون دل 'heart-blood' is a compound object (blood of the heart = bitter tears). برفشاند is a simple past/classical form meaning 'he scattered/spattered (upon)', with بر- prefix indicating motion onto ('upon').
segments.s657-g883: ز پند آنچ is a genitive/source phrase: ز 'from' + پند 'advice/counsel' + آنچ 'that which/what'. طلخند را is the direct object marked by را; here طلخند is a proper name (not the common noun meaning 'bitter smile'), and داده بود is the pluperfect ('had given') formed by the past participle داده + auxiliary بود 'was/had been', indicating an action completed earlier.
segments.s657-g884: بدیشان بگفت is a simple clause: بدیشان 'to them' (dative/adverbial), بگفت 'he said/told' (classical past form). آنچ زو هم شنود strings a relative content clause: آنچ 'that which/what', زو 'from him', هم 'also' (emphatic), شنود here is a classical form meaning 'he had heard' (used in narration to report past reception), so the clause means 'what he had also heard from him'.
segments.s657-g885: یکی here is an indefinite 'one'/'someone' (introducing an unspecified agent). تخت تابوت is a compound noun ('a flat bed/coffin') with تخت functioning as the flat platform/bed. کردش is کرد + clitic ش (sh = 'him/it') so 'he/they made it/him' — classical past tense verb + object clitic. ز عاج 'of ivory' is a material genitive introduced by the poetic preposition ز.
segments.s657-g886: This coordinate list uses repeated ز 'of' to enumerate materials: ز زر 'of gold' و ز پیروزه 'and of turquoise' و خوب ساج 'and (of) fine/beautiful saj'. خوب modifies ساج ('beautiful/good saj'), with the conjunctions linking the series of precious materials used for the coffin.
segments.s657-g887: بپوشید رویش is a verb + object phrase: بپوشید (past of پوشیدن) here means 'covered' (sense 'to cover/conceal'), and رویش = روی + -ش (face/surface + his/its clitic) 'its face'. به چینی پرند is an instrumental/descr. phrase: به چینی 'with/after the Chinese/Chinese-made' and پرند 'a bird' (likely describing a bird-shaped or bird-decorated covering); the exact material/image is poetic, but the structure is prepositional/qualifying.
segments.s657-g888: شد آن نامور نامبردار هند is a nominative/predicative clause: شد 'became/was' (verb of becoming), آن 'that/the' points to the preceding coffined person, نامور 'renowned' and نامبردار 'name-bearing/celebrated' are stacked epithets, and هند 'India' is the domain/attribution ('of India' or 'the Indian'). Together they render 'he became that famed, name-bearing one of India.'
segments.s657-g185: "مرا خواست" is object + verb (me + he sought/called) — classical word order where the object precedes the verb; "مرا" is the object marker + pronoun. "انباز" is an archaic/adverbial element here (literary) indicating a subsequent action or repetition (roughly 'then' / 'again'). "گشتیم" is 1st-person plural past of گشتن 'we turned/returned'. "وجفت" is the conjunction و + جفت; here و links the action and جفت functions adverbially ('together/paired'), so the whole chunk means 'he called me; then we turned back together.'
segments.s657-g186: بدان is the classical compound be + an (roughly 'to/unto that' or 'toward that') heading a purposive/result clause. تا introduces purpose/result ('so that' / 'in order that') and governs the following predicate. نماند is present/subjunctive-like negated verb 'not remain' (used here with تا to express a desired/resultant non-occurrence). سخن is the noun 'word/speech' (object of نماند). درنهفت is a fused form meaning 'be hidden' (literary/passive), completing the idea: 'so that no word remains hidden.'
segments.s657-g187: اگر begins a conditional clause ('if'). زانک (زان + ک) is a literary connective meaning 'for that reason / since' and gives the ground for the condition. مهتر برادر تویی has inverted order common in older/poetic Persian: مهتر (elder/chief) modifies برادر (brother), and تویی is the 2nd‑person copula 'you are' — together: 'you are the elder brother.'
segments.s657-g188: به here marks the domain of comparison ('in' or 'with respect to'): به هوش و خرد = 'in intelligence and wisdom'. و‑خرد is the conjunction ו attached to خرد ('and wisdom'). نیز is an adverb 'also/likewise' emphasizing the following predicate. برتر is the adjective 'superior', and تویی is again the copula 'you are' — 'you are also superior in intellect and wisdom.'
segments.s657-g189: همان کن is an imperative phrase: همان 'that/same' + کن 'do!' meaning 'do that.' که introduces the subordinate clause describing what is at stake. جان را uses the object marker را with جان 'life/heart' (here idiomatically 'the self' or 'courage to endure'). نداری is 2nd‑person present negative 'you do not have' / 'you lack'. به رنج is a prepositional phrase 'through / by hardship' modifying نداری: 'you lack (the) life/heart in hardship' (i.e. you will not or do not have the disposition to endure suffering).
segments.s657-g190: ز (poetic form of از) + بهر form a purposive phrase 'for the sake/for the sake of'. سرافرازی is 'honor/glory', followed by و تاج وگنج 'and crown and treasure' (و attaches to the following noun as usual). The whole segment states the motive: 'for the sake of glory and crown and treasure.'
segments.s657-g191: یکی ازشما = 'one of you' (object phrase). گرکنم is a fused conditional expression based on گر + کنم meaning 'if I were to do' (here 'if I were to pick'). من is the emphatic subject 'I'. گزین is the verb 'choose/select' (infinitive/stem form used with the conditional construction) — the whole order is a poetic/inverted way of saying 'If I choose one of you.'
segments.s657-g192: دل دیگری گردَد is 'another's heart becomes' (گردد is subjunctive/present of گشتن 'become/turn'). از من is the prepositional phrase 'toward me / against me'. بکین is a poetic form meaning 'take vengeance / bear resentment / act hostilely' (here used intransitively of the other's feeling or action). Together: 'another's heart would turn against me (and bear a grudge).'
segments.s657-g697: درشتی (a noun) + نه + زیباست: here نه negates the copula phrase زیباست (“is becoming/beautiful”), so the clause means “rudeness is not fitting.” با + شهریار places the reference/person for whom something is (un)suitable: literally “with a king” = “for a king/with a lord.” Word order is typical Persian: subject/nominal (درشتی) → negated predication (نه زیباست) → prepositional phrase (با شهریار).
segments.s657-g698: This is a coordinated clause: پدرنامور بود = “(your) father was renowned” (بود here is past tense). و links that past statement to the present predicate تو نامدار = “and you are famed” (the second clause omits an explicit copula, typical in Persian). The sequence contrasts ancestor’s fame with the addressee’s present standing.
segments.s657-g699: مرا این درستست is a dative-type construction: مرا (to me / for me) + این درستست (“this is correct/right”) — meaning “I find this right.” کز (= که + ز) introduces the content clause “that …” and پند من is the source/object of that clause (“my advice/ counsel”). The particle order (مرا + این + درستست) emphasizes the speaker’s judgment.
segments.s657-g700: تو دوری نجویی literally puts the noun دوری (“separation”) before the verb-like form نجویی: here نجویی is negative present/subjunctive formed with the prefix ن- (ن + جویی) meaning “do not seek” / “should not seek.” ز پیوند من = “from my tie/bond.” The order (تو + دوری + نجویی + ز …) is Persian: topic (تو) → object/goal (دوری) → verb (نجویی) → complement (ز پیوند من).
segments.s657-g701: ولیکن is a conjunction “but/yet.” مرا (to me) + ز آنک هست آرزوی: ز آنک هست (“because of that there is / for that reason there is”) introduces آرزوی (“a wish”) — together: “but I, because of that, have a wish.” The clause shows cause (ز آنک) and possession/existence of a desire (آرزوی) for the speaker.
segments.s657-g702: که introduces the content of the wish: a subjunctive clause. تو نامور باشی uses the present subjunctive باشی after آرزوی to mean “that you be renowned” (wish/optative). و links the second predicate نامجوی, here a noun meaning “one who seeks a name” (i.e. strives for fame). The subjunctive is standard after verbs/nouns of wishing.
segments.s657-g703: بگویم here is a first-person verb form meaning “I will say” / “let me say” (a classical Persian optative/future sense). همه آنچ اندر دلست = “all that which is in the heart”: آنچ = “that which,” اندر = “in/within,” دلست = “is of the heart / feeling.” The phrase announces the speaker will express everything internal.
segments.s657-g704: سخنها = “the words/talks” (plural) and که introduces a relative clause. جانم برو مایلست is a poetic phrasing: جانم = “my soul,” برو here functions adverbially/locative (“toward it/there”) and مایلست = “is inclined” or “leans.” Together: “the words toward which my soul is inclined.” Word order places the target noun (سخنها) before the relative clause describing them.
