segments.s657-g793: توگفتی is the simple past, 2nd singular: “you said.” The following clause هوا تیغ بارد همی is a compact, poetic word order: هوا (the air/atmosphere) + تیغ (sword(s)) + بارد (an archaic/poetic verbal/adjectival form here implying ‘fall(s)/rain(s)’) + همی (the classical continuous/habitual marker). In context the phrase should be read as a reported statement: “you said that the air was raining swords.”
segments.s657-g794: This segment is a highly compressed, poetic clause. بخاک اندرون (into the ground/within the soil) is a locative phrase; لاله is the (metaphorical) noun ‘tulip’ (a common poetic image for red blood/flowers of the field); کارد names the instrument ‘knife/dagger’ and stands here in an agentive/nominal position where a verb is omitted (poetic ellipsis) so that کارد همی implies ‘the knife kept [doing so]/kept plunging’; همی again marks the continuous/habitual sense. Read together: “into the earth, the knife kept (making) tulips” (i.e. planting red wounds/flowers of blood).
segments.s657-g795: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/owing to'; افگنده is a participle/adjective 'thrown/scattered' modifying گیتی 'the world'; بران گونه is an archaic demonstrative + گونه 'in that manner/way'; and گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned'. Together: 'from/when the world was thrown, it became in that way.'
segments.s657-g796: که introduces a result/relative clause 'that…'. کرکس is the subject 'the vulture'. نیارست is an archaic/unusual form in the text (here functioning negatively or as a defective past of coming/attaining — the dataset senses supplied do not match this usage), and برسرگذشت (literally 'came upon the head') functions as the predicate 'befell/transpired (upon)'. The clause therefore means 'so that even the vulture did not/could not come upon what befell.'
segments.s657-g797: گروهی here is partitive 'some' or 'a number (of them)'. بکنده is a participial/adjectival form from کندن (‘torn out, pulled out’) describing victims whose درون (innards/inside) is modified by پر ز خون 'full of blood' (پر 'full (of)' + ز 'of' + خون 'blood'). The whole phrase describes people ‘some whose entrails were torn out and full of blood.’
segments.s657-g798: دگر is 'the other/another (group)'; سر بریده is a compound adjectival phrase 'head‑severed' (بریده = cut off); فگنده is a past participle 'thrown/scattered' and نگون is an adjective meaning 'overthrown/downfallen' — together they form the predicate describing those others as 'thrown down with severed heads.' Note the copula/verb is omitted (typical in epic poetry), and participles and adjectives carry the action.
segments.s657-g799: ز دریا is the source 'from the sea.' همی‌خاست is an archaic/imperfect verbal form (here meaning 'arose/was rising') describing موج 'wave(s)'; از باد links the agent/instrument 'by the wind' (literally 'waves from the wind'). Word order is inverted for poetic effect: verb (همی‌خاست) appears near the start and the agent phrase follows.
segments.s657-g800: سپاه اندر آمد is straightforward: 'a host/army came in/arrived.' آمد is the past of آمدن 'came.' همی again is the classical continuous/habitual marker (here giving an ongoing, dramatic sense), and فوج فوج is a reduplicated noun meaning 'troop after troop / in successive ranks' (an intensifying plural).
segments.s657-g249: برین is the fused prepositional phrase بر + این = “on/onto this.” برنهادند is a past (3rd plural) verb meaning “they raised/mounted” (برنهاد- + -ند); here it describes a completed action. هر دو جوان = “both young men” (هر + دو = “each/both of the two,” followed by جوان “young man”).
segments.s657-g250: کزان = کز + آن, an adverbial meaning roughly “therefrom/thereupon,” marking consequence. پس ز is a poetic/comparative sequence (پس = “after/behind/thereupon” + ز = “from”), here functioning like “beyond/above” the following items; وز is the fused form of و + ز (conjunction + preposition) linking گردان and پهلوان so both are being compared. The phrase thus says they stood above the troop and the champion.
segments.s657-g251: ز is the poetic preposition “from.” دانا و پاکان = “the wise and the pure” (coordinated by و). سخن is the noun “speech/words.” بشنویم is the 1st‑person‑plural present-subjunctive/hortative meaning “let us hear” or “may we hear,” used here to make a polite proposal.
segments.s657-g252: بران سان = بر + آن (thereupon) + سان (so/like) = “in that manner.” که introduces a purpose/result clause (“so that/that”). باشد is a 3rd‑person singular subjunctive/optative “may it be/let it be.” بدان is composed of بـ (to/into) + an archaic pronoun (= ‘to that’), and بگرویم is 1st‑person‑pl. subjunctive “we may adopt/attach ourselves to,” so the clause means “so that it may be, to that we may adhere.”
segments.s657-g253: کز ایشان = کز (from) + ایشان (them) = “from them.” همی here is the archaic verbal particle often marking the verbal aspect or adding emphasis in narrative; آموختیم is past tense (1st‑person plural) “we learned,” and دانش is the object “knowledge.” The clause = “From them we learned knowledge.”
segments.s657-g254: به is the preposition “to/into/for.” فرهنگ دلها = “the culture (or cultivated lore) of the hearts” (فرهنگ + دلها in a genitive relationship). برافروختیم is past (1st‑person plural) formed with the intensifying prefix بَر‑ and means “we kindled/ignited” (here metaphorically: we stirred up or inspired).
segments.s657-g255: بیامد is a simple past verb “came” (3rd singular in classical narrative). دو فرزانه رهنمای = “two wise (men), guides/advisers”: فرزانه = “wise/sage,” and رهنمای is an appositive noun “guide/adviser.” Word order places the verb before its noun phrase, common in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s657-g256: میانشان = میان + شان = “among/between them.” همی‌رفت is an imperfective/habitual past form “was going/went about” used idiomatically for things that circulated or moved back and forth. هر گونه رای = “every kind/sort of counsel” (هر + گونه = “every kind,” رای here = “plan/counsel”), so the whole clause means that all manner of counsel circulated among them.
segments.s657-g705: تو را سر بپیچد is a construction where تو را (you + object marker) marks the person affected and سر (literally “head”) is the grammatical subject that undergoes the action; بپیچد is a third‑person present/future form (prefix ب‑ + پیچد) meaning “turn(s)” or “will turn.” ز here is the preposition “from/through/because of,” and دستورِ بد (‘dastur bad’) is a genitive/noun phrase meaning “bad rule/administration.” The overall phrasing means “(Because of) bad rule, your head will be turned (i.e. you will be thrown off).”
segments.s657-g706: This is a coordinated list introduced by زآسانی (here functioning like a frozen prepositional/adverbial element meaning roughly “(from) ease/comfort” or “with ease”), followed by و (and) رای (counsel/opinion), وراه خرد (and the way/path of wisdom). Grammatically the whole phrase is the complement of ز (i.e. what one is turned away from).
segments.s657-g707: مگوی is the negative imperative (do not say/tell). ای برادر is a vocative addressing someone (“O brother”). سخن is the direct object “speech/words.” جز introduces an exclusion (“except”), and بداد here functions as the noun being excepted (in the lexical list given it is ‘oppression’); so the clause means “Do not speak, O brother, any word except (that which concerns) oppression.”
segments.s657-g708: که begins a causal/explanatory clause (“for/because/that”). گیتی سراسر is the noun + adverbial phrase “the world altogether/throughout.” فسونست is a fused older form (فسون + است) meaning “is enchantment/witchcraft” (a predicate). The final و باد contains باد as an optative/benedictive particle (“may it be so”/“would that”), so the whole clause reads “for the world is all enchantment — may it be so.”
segments.s657-g709: سوی راستی is a prepositional/directional phrase “toward truth/rightness.” یاز is an imperative “open” (classical/poetic form). تا introduces a purpose/result clause (“so that”), and هرچ هست is “whatever exists” (هرچ = whatever; هست = is/exists). Together this gives an instruction followed by its purpose: “Turn to righteousness; open [up] so that whatever exists …”
segments.s657-g710: ز introduces a partitive/listing sense “of/from,” so ز گنج و ز مردان خسروپرست enumerates what the clause is concerned with: “of treasure and of men who worship the king.” مردان خسروپرست is a noun + modifier (men who are sycophants/devotees of the king). Grammatically this phrase completes هرچ هست: “whatever there is of treasure and of king‑worshipping men.”
segments.s657-g711: فرستم is first‑person present/future (“I send / I will send”). همه سر به سر is an idiomatic adverbial meaning “all, one by one” or “one after another.” پیش تو is a locative/ablative phrase “before you / in your presence.” Word order places the actor/agentive verb first and the adverbial phrases after it.
segments.s657-g712: ببیند is a third‑person present/subjunctive form of دیدن used here with a purposive/result sense (“that he/they may see”). روان بداندیش functions as the subject phrase placed after the verb (poetic V‑S order): روان = “spirit/soul” and بداندیش = “ill‑wishing/malicious” used substantively (“the ill‑willed/ill‑minded”). تو at the end is the object of دیدن (“see you”). So the clause reads “so that the ill‑minded spirit(s) may see you.”
segments.s657-g129: زگفت is a fused prepositional/nominal unit (ز = from/at + گفت = saying) meaning 'at that saying'. بدآموز qualifies that saying as 'ill‑instructive' or corrupting. جوشان شدند is a past plural verb phrase 'they became boiling/seething' (جوشان = seething/angry; شدند = they became), so the whole segment means 'at that corrupting remark they seethed'.
segments.s657-g130: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'toward/near'. مادرخروشان is a poetic compound 'mother (source) of the uproar/roaring' ('مادر' + 'خروشان'), and شدند is again past plural 'they became/turned' used here to indicate movement/turning toward that figure. Together: 'they, roaring, came close to the mother'.
segments.s657-g131: بگفتند is the reporting verb 'they said'. کزماکه is a fused relative/partitive 'که ز ما که' = 'which/ who among us' (classical poetic ordering), and زیباترست is the predicate 'is more beautiful' (زیباتر + است contracted). The clause introduces reported speech: 'they said that who among us is more beautiful...'.
segments.s657-g132: که introduces the relative continuation 'who/that ...'. برنیک و بد here should be read as 'بر نیک و بد' = 'with respect to good and bad' (poetic compounding), وبد = 'and bad'. برشکیباترست is a fused comparative predicate 'بر + شکیبا + تر + است' meaning 'is more patient' (i.e. more enduring). So the clause says 'who is more patient in good and bad'.
segments.s657-g133: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. پاسخ آورد is a light-verb construction 'brought/produced an answer' (آورد used to present the act of giving the reply). فرزانه زن is the agent 'the wise woman' (adjective + noun); note verb-before-subject order is common in poetic narrative.
segments.s657-g134: که introduces the content of her answer. باموبدی is 'با موبدی' = 'with a mobed' (accompanied by/alongside the Zoroastrian priest). یکدل = 'one‑hearted/with one heart'. ورای زن in the manuscript is actually the poetic fusion of 'و رای زن' = 'and one counsel/opinion with the woman' (i.e. 'and of one counsel'); here the phrase means they were united in heart and counsel. (This segment shows classical poetic fusion and occasional orthographic merging of 'و رای'.)
segments.s657-g135: شمارابباید is a fused colloquial/classical construction where شمارا (older/poetic order of 'شما را' or 'the count') combines with باید ('must/should') to express necessity — here functioning as an impersonal/second‑person instruction 'you must'. نشستن is the infinitive 'to sit (down)' used as the complement of باید, and نخست is the adverb 'first/initially'. So: 'you must first sit down.'
segments.s657-g136: بآرام is an adverb 'calmly/gently'. و = 'and'. با کام = 'with (a) satisfied/desiring heart' (کام here = satisfaction/desire). فرجام is 'the outcome/end', and جست is the past of جستن used here as 'sought' or poetically as an imperative-ish counsel 'seek'. Put together: 'calmly and with a contented heart seek the outcome.'
segments.s657-g489: This is a prepositional phrase headed by the poetic preposition ز (ز = 'from/of'), followed by the adjective گردنده ('revolving') modifying the quantified noun هفت اختر ('seven stars'). اندر سپهر is a locative phrase 'in the sky/heavens' placed after the noun. Word order is typical poetic Persian: adjective before the noun phrase, then the locative.
segments.s657-g490: یکی را is an object NP with را as the definite/object marker; ندیدم is 1st-person past 'I did not see'. بدو is the archaic/informal short form of به او 'to him'. رای و مهر is a coordinated noun phrase 'counsel and favour/affection'. The clause omits a transitive verb like 'داد' ('gave') for poetic succinctness; read as 'I saw no one who would give him counsel and favour.'
segments.s657-g491: تبه گردد او هم = 'he too will be ruined' (تبه here conveys ruin/misfortune). گردد is the present/subjunctive/optative form of 'become' used as a future/predictive 'will be' in poetry; او هم 'he also'. بدین is the poetic contraction of به + این 'to/at this', so بدین دشت جنگ = 'in this plain of battle'.
segments.s657-g492: نباید + گرفتن: an impersonal modal construction meaning 'one should not (do something)'; here گرفتن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to take (on)'. خود 'oneself' adds emphasis/reflective sense; این کار تنگ literally 'this narrow/tight affair' = 'this difficult task' (tنگ used figuratively). The order (modal + infinitive + object) is normal in Persian.
segments.s657-g493: مگر introduces an exception 'except/unless'; مهر شاهی = 'love/ambition for kingship' (مهر 'affection/desire' + شاهی 'kingship'); و تخت و کلاه is a paired apposition meaning 'and (the) throne and (the) crown' (کلاه here = crown in poetic use). The list after مگر names what would make the army judge them harshly.
segments.s657-g494: بدان here functions as 'for that/thereby' (poetic compound from به + آن) introducing the consequence; تات is the ethnic name 'the Tats' (direct object of the evaluative verb); بد دل ('bad‑hearted') is a descriptive compound adjective. نخواند is the past negative of خواندن used in the sense 'to call/name' (third sense), so (سپاه) نخواند = 'the army did(n't) call (them) ...' — Persian allows flexible order so سپاه at the end is the agent 'the army'. Overall: 'the army would not call those Tats ill‑hearted except for the foregoing (love of kingship)'.
segments.s657-g495: دگر هرچ خواهد = 'as for the rest / whatever else he wants' (دگر 'other/again', هرچ 'whatever', خواهد = 'he wants' present/future of خواستن). ز اسب و ز گنج is a double prepositional source 'from/of horses and of treasure' listing what is desired.
segments.s657-g496: بده is the imperative 'give' directed to someone; تا introduces purpose/result 'so that', followed by نباشد (subjunctive negative 'may not be') and روانش 'his soul/spirit' with به رنج 'in suffering' as the state to be avoided. The construction تا + subjunctive expresses intended result or purpose.
segments.s657-g953: چنان = an adverb meaning 'so/thus' setting the manner. شمع رخشان is a noun + adjective ('candle, radiant') with the adjective following the noun. فرو is an adverbial prefix/adverb 'down/ward', and پژمرد is a past tense verb 'withered/blew out' describing a completed action; together فرو پژمرد = 'fell/withered down'.
segments.s657-g954: به گیتی is a prepositional phrase 'in/onto the world' marking location/scope. کسی here, with a following negated verb, functions as 'no one' (negative indefinite). یک‌نفس is an adverbial measure 'a single breath/moment'. نشمرد is the past negative of شمردن 'did not count/ reckon' (the object is omitted but understood). Word order places locative + subject + adverbial + negative verb.
segments.s657-g955: وگر = 'if' (conditional). چون نمایم = subordinate clause 'if/when I show (it) thus' with نمایم a first‑person present/subjunctive form 'I show/manifest'. نگردی is the second‑person negative form of گشتن (here 'become') meaning 'you will not become' or 'you do not become', and رام is the predicative adjective 'tame'; تو is the emphatic pronoun 'you'. The negative particle is attached to the verb (ن‑).
segments.s657-g956: به دادار دارنده is a prepositional phrase 'to/for the Creator, the Possessor' (two epithets in apposition). کوراست is a present copula+adjective 'is blind' describing the following noun کام 'desire', which appears at the end as the grammatical subject (common in older/poetic Persian: predicate before subject). The line thus reads roughly 'to the Creator and Possessor, desire is blind.'
segments.s657-g957: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that'). پیشت is 'before you / in your presence' (prepositional/locative). به آتش 'with/in fire' marks the instrument/means. بر + خویش را: بر is 'upon/on', خویش (here written as خوی + ش) is the reflexive possessive 'one's own' (ش = his/her/its) and را is the definite object marker; together بر خویش را indicates the object 'upon his/her/one's own (self)'. Word order places the locative/instrument before the object and verb.
segments.s657-g958: بسوزم is a first‑person (subjunctive/optative) form of سوختن 'may I burn / I should burn' used to express volition or hypothetical action. ز بهر = a fixed poetic expression 'for the sake of' (ز = 'from/because of', بهر = 'for/for the purpose of'). بداندیش را is the object 'the ill‑wisher/malicious one' marked by را. Together the clause means 'I (would) burn (myself) for the sake of the malicious one.'
segments.s657-g959: چو = 'when'. بشنید is a past tense verb 'she heard' (third person past). مادر is the subject 'the mother'. سخنهای is the plural or genitive form 'words/speeches' (here 'the words'), and گو is an attributive form from گفتن 'speaking/said' (i.e., 'spoken words' or 'the speaker's words'); word order is temporal clause → verb → subject → object phrase.
segments.s657-g960: دریغ آمدش is an impersonal phrasing 'pity/sorrow came to her' (آمدش = 'came to her'). برز و بالای are nouns/adjectives meaning 'height/exaltation and loftiness' used here to describe her carriage or spirit, and گو again qualifies that phrase (an archaic adjectival/participial use). The whole phrase means something like 'pity seized her exalted/lofty aspect' — an idiomatic, poetic way to say she was moved with pity.
segments.s657-g145: به marks the indirect object ('to his mother'); گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (the speaker 'he' is omitted). چنين is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying گفت. پرمایه گو is an epithet placed after the reported clause in vocative position: پرمایه (literally 'substantive/weighty') describes the speech, and گو here is the productive suffix/participle meaning '-speaking' or 'one who speaks', so together 'O richly-worded / eloquent one'.
segments.s657-g146: کزین پرسش is a causal phrase 'because of/from this question' (کزین = 'from/for this' modifying پرسش). اندر زمانه is a prepositional phrase 'into/among the age/the world'. مرو is the negative imperative 'do not go' (archaic/poetic form identical to modern مرو). The whole segment gives the command: 'do not go into the world over this question.'
segments.s657-g147: اگر begins a conditional clause 'if'. کشور ازمن نگیرد literally 'the realm from me does not take' — نگیرد is here a present/subjunctive form meaning 'does not receive/take' in the protasis of the conditional, and ازمن marks source ('from me'). فروغ is the object 'splendor/radiance' that the realm might or might not receive.
segments.s657-g148: به کژی uses به instrumentally/adverbially ('by/with crookedness') — here کژی is the abstract noun 'crookedness/wrongness'. مکن is the prohibitive imperative 'do not (do)'. هیچ before a noun negates it ('no/any'), so هیچ رای دروغ = 'no false counsel' (رای 'counsel/plan', دروغ 'falsehood'). The clause commands: 'do not, by crookedness, give any false counsel.'
segments.s657-g149: به + proper name places the recipient before the verb: 'to Talakhund'. بسپار is the imperative 'entrust/leave (to)'. گنج و سپاه are the direct objects 'treasure and army' (وسپاه is the joined form 'و'+'سپاه'). The word order is typical Persian: preposition + recipient, imperative verb, then objects.
segments.s657-g150: This clause is elliptical: من او را is the topicalized object 'him' (را marks the definite object). The predicate 'I consider/regard (him) ...' is left implicit, followed by the descriptive phrase یکی کهترم نیکخواه 'a someone inferior and well-wishing'. یکی functions as 'someone/a certain one', کهترم describes relative smallness/inferiority (here 'inferior to me' / 'younger/lesser'), and نیکخواه is a compound adjective 'well-wisher/benign'.
segments.s657-g151: وگر is a conditional 'if' (archaic/poetic). به سال و خرد is an instrumental/causal phrase 'by/with years and wisdom' (سال = age/period; وخرد = 'and wisdom', here خرد = wisdom). مهترم is a predicate meaning 'I am the elder/chief' with the first-person suffix -م ('I am elder'). The clause expresses a condition based on the speaker's seniority in age and wisdom.
segments.s657-g152: هم is 'also/likewise'. از پشت جمهور is a prepositional phrase 'from behind the people/assembly' (از پشت = 'from behind', جمهور = 'the public/assembly'). کنداورم is a first-person verbal form used here in an archaic/poetic sense meaning roughly 'I will fetch/remove/drag out' (the precise lexical history is old and the base lemma appears as a noun in some dictionaries, so the form is idiomatic/poetic). Grammatically it is the main verb of the apodosis ('I will ... him').
segments.s657-g113: چو (چوتنها here fused form) is the archaic subordinating 'when'; تنها شدی is a past-tense predicate 'you became alone' (چو + past = temporal clause). سوی مادر یکی is a prepositional phrase placed after the verb in poetical order: سوی 'toward/to', مادر 'mother', یکی 'one / a certain one' (i.e. 'one of them' or 'a certain mother').
segments.s657-g114: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the following verb phrase. هم‌سخن راندی is a compound verb phrase: هم‌سخن (a conversational partner / speech-companion) functions as the object/phrase with راندی (past of راندن) meaning here 'addressed / spoke (to)'; اندکی is an adverb 'a little' that limits the verb. Word order is typical Persian (adverb/phrase + verb) but slightly compressed in verse.
segments.s657-g115: که introduces a content clause 'that/which'. از ما دو فرزند is a genitive/possessive expression 'two children of us' = 'our two children'. کشور here means 'the realm/country' and functions as the domain being allocated; کراست is a predicate element in this line (an older/poetic form whose precise lexical senses are ambiguous here), so the whole segment states which of the two children is associated with the country/realm.
segments.s657-g116: به شاهی is a prepositional phrase 'for/to kingship' (به + شاهی = 'for the kingship' or 'to rule'). و این تخت و افسرکراست continues the allocation: این تخت 'this throne' and افسر (crown/headgear) with the verb/predicate element کراست (here attached as افسرکراست) — the line assigns the throne and crown. The predicate element is poetic/archaic and treated as indicating possession/assignment.
segments.s657-g117: بدو is the dative 'to him/to her/to it' (here 'to her' = the mother). مام is a dialectal or poetic form for 'mother'. گفتی is the past form 'you said' (verb); که introduces the reported content. تخت آن تست is a nominal clause 'that throne is his' with آن 'that' and تست (archaic copula) marking predication.
segments.s657-g118: This segment lists three nouns joined by و: هنرمندی 'skill/artistry', رای 'counsel/judgement', و بخت 'and fortune/fate'. آن تست repeats the demonstrative + (archaic) copula 'that is his'. Grammatically the three nouns form a compound predicate identifying the qualities that make the possession/claim.
segments.s657-g119: به indicates the indirect object 'to' (to/for). دیگر پسرهم is 'the other son' with پسرهم (poetic fusion) meaning 'also the son / the son likewise'. ازینسان سخن 'such speech / in this manner speech' is a noun phrase describing the content of what was said; it functions as the object of the verb that follows.
segments.s657-g120: همی‌راندی is an archaic/past-progressive form 'you were driving/uttering' used here idiomatically for 'you kept saying' or 'you continued to speak'. تا is the subordinating 'until'. سخن شد کهن is 'the speech/talk became old' (کهن = old/longstanding) — i.e. the conversation grew lengthy or became established; شد is the past 'became'.
segments.s657-g777: بیاراست here functions as a preposed verbal form/participle meaning “(having) arrayed/ornamented” and links to the subject that is implicit in the following main verb. با marks the instrument or accompaniment. میمنه (the right side/flank) is the locative/position being described; میسره is an inserted modifier of that flank (its exact lexical sense is uncertain or specialized here). The whole chunk describes how the flank was dressed/arranged before the main action.
segments.s657-g778: کشیدند is past tense, 3rd-person plural (they drew/moved). نزدیک دریا is an adverbial phrase (“near the sea”) — نزدیک is an adjective/adverb and دریا its object. بنه here is a noun complement (likely meaning an encampment/anchorage in context); word order in poetry allows the verb to come before the adverbial phrase.
segments.s657-g779: دو شاه is the numeral + noun ‘two kings’ with گرانمایه as an epithet (‘noble/esteemed’). پر is the adjective ‘full (of)’ and takes the following nouns as the contents: درد و کین = ‘pain/sorrow and hatred/vengeance.’ The chain is a noun phrase describing the emotional state of the kings.
segments.s657-g780: نهادند is past tense, 3rd-person plural of نهادن ‘to place/set’ (they placed). برپشت is a locative compound ‘on the back (of)’, پیلان is the plural ‘elephants’, and دو زین is the direct object ‘two saddles’. The verb governs the locative phrase and the object; Persian word order here (verb after locative/object) is typical of Ferdowsi’s poetic flexibility.
segments.s657-g781: به قلب اندرون is a compound locative phrase ‘into/within the inner heart’ (به + noun + اندرون). ساخته is a verbal adjective/participle ‘made/construct(ed)’ and modifies جای (‘place’), while خوی + ش together form the possessive ‘his own’ (خوی as root with the clitic ش). The phrase therefore means ‘having made a place in the inner heart (for) his own…’.
segments.s657-g782: شده is the resultative/passive-like perfect ‘has become’ or ‘was made’, linking the previous made-place to the following predicate. هر یکی ‘each one’ is the subject, and لشکر آرای خویش is a genitive phrase ‘the arranger/arrayer of his own army’ (آرای here = ornament/array). The construction means ‘each one became the (one who) arrays his own army.’
segments.s657-g783: زمین قار شد uses قار (an onomatopoeic/ideophonic word indicating a harsh sound or outcry) + شد ‘became/was’ to mean ‘the earth resounded’ or ‘the ground cried out’. The clause is paralleled by آسمان شد بنفش ‘the sky became purple’; each noun takes شد to form two parallel predicates, a common rhetorical device in epic poetry.
segments.s657-g784: ز بس here introduces the cause/quantity ‘from/because of the multitude (of)’, followed by نیزه و پرنیانی درفش ‘spears and the silken banner(s)’. پرنیانی is an adjective ‘silken’ modifying درفش ‘banner’. The whole segment gives the reason why earth ‘cried’ and sky ‘turned purple’.
segments.s657-g337: Poetic word order: برادر (subject/topic) + ورا (dative 'to him') + همچو جان بود و تن gives a simile in which the brother is likened to both 'soul' and 'body'. بود is the past copula ('was'); the phrase literally reads 'Brother to him was like soul and body.' The conjunction و links جان and تن as two coordinated terms of the simile.
segments.s657-g338: بشاهی is the prepositional/infinitival notion 'to (the) kingship' (be‑+شاهی), ورا is the object 'him/to him', خواندند is a past 3rd‑person plural verb 'they called/summoned/proclaimed', and انجمن 'assembly' is the agent or setting; together: 'the assembly proclaimed/called him to the kingship.'
segments.s657-g339: اگر + بودمی forms a counterfactual conditional ('if I had been' / 'if I were'); من is the emphasized subject and سزاوار is a predicate adjective meaning 'worthy/deserving'; گاه here means 'post/place/occasion' (time/position). The whole segment: 'If I had been worthy of the post...'.
segments.s657-g340: نکردی is the past negative (you did not / would not have), understood here with an implied permissive or causative sense ('you did not let / you would not have allowed'). به + اندرون = 'into/into the inner', مای is here read as the collective/possessive 'our' (from 'we/us') modifying اندرون, کس 'anyone', and نگاه (a noun used verbally) 'to look' — so: 'you would not have allowed anyone to look into our inner quarters.' The clause is elliptical: the verb 'allow' is implicit in the sense of نکردی.
segments.s657-g341: بر آیین شاهان گیتی is a prepositional phrase 'according to the custom of the world's kings' (بر = 'according to/on'), and رویم is a 1st‑person plural verb meaning 'we go/behave/follow' in an older/formal register — together: 'we follow the customs of the world's kings.' The noun phrases precede the verb in typical Persian poetic word order.
segments.s657-g342: ز is the poetic preposition 'from', فرزانگان 'the sages/learned men', نیک و بد 'good and bad' is a paired contrast, and بشنویم is 1st‑person plural 'we hear/listen (to)' (present/optative sense). The clause means 'we hear (learn) [things] from sages, the good and the bad.'
segments.s657-g343: من ازتو is a comparative 'I compared with you', به سال و خرد is an ablative/instrumental idiom 'by years and wisdom' (i.e., 'in age and wisdom'), and مهترم is the predicate 'I am senior/greater'. The structure is a direct comparative claim: 'By age and wisdom I am senior to you.'
segments.s657-g344: توگویی is 2nd‑person present 'you say/you might say', که introduces the subordinate clause 'that...', من کهترم 'I am lesser/younger' (the claim attributed to the addressee), and بهترم (with the enclitic/copula م) asserts 'I am better.' The line sets up an objection ('you may say I am inferior') and immediately rebuts it ('I am better').
segments.s657-g657: همه (all/every) modifies the following noun phrase خوب کاری, here functioning as 'every good deed'. خوب ('good') is an adjectival modifier placed before کاری. کاری in classical/poetic usage stands for 'a deed/action' (noun); ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' marking source or agent (so 'deeds from/by the God‑knower'). یزدان‌شناس is the source/agent in the genitive construction.
segments.s657-g658: وزو is the religious term 'wudu/ablution'. دار here functions as an older/poetic imperative/verb meaning 'keep/maintain' (i.e. 'hold/observe the ablution'); تا + باشی marks duration ('as long as you are/while you live') with باشی in the subjunctive (2nd person singular) after تا. سپاس is a nominal/exclamatory word 'thanks/praise' used as an exhortation 'give thanks' or 'be grateful'.
segments.s657-g659: که introduces the clause (here causal/temporal 'since/that'). زنده is a predicative adjective 'alive' placed before the verb for emphasis. برفتی is the 2nd‑person past tense 'you went/left' (subject is incorporated in the verbal ending). توازپیش is a fused prepositional/locative expression ('تو از پیش' or 'from before/at the front with you') indicating 'from the front/before the battle'. جنگ is the noun 'battle/war' indicating the scene.
segments.s657-g660: نه negates the following expressions: هنگام رایست = 'a time for counsel/deliberation' (هنگام 'time; at the time (of)'); رایست is a poetic/archaic nominal form referring to deliberation/counsel (here used as the thing that is not appropriate). و links the next phrase: روز درنگ = 'a day for delay' with روز (day) placed before درنگ (pause/delay) to mean 'a day of delay'. The negation scopes over both coordinated phrases.
segments.s657-g661: چو (archaic for 'when') + شنید (he heard) forms a temporal clause 'when he heard'. طلخند is a compound meaning a bitter or wry laugh/smile; it is the object of شنید. آواز اوی is a possessive/genitive phrase 'the voice of him/his cry' (اوی is an older enclitic form 'his/he'). Note the poetic word order where the temporal marker and verb precede the object phrase.
segments.s657-g662: شد is the past/copular verb 'became'. از introduces the cause/source 'from' (here 'from shame'). ننگ is 'shame/disgrace' and پیچان is an adjective 'twisting/writhing', so از ننگ پیچان describes a face 'twisted from shame'. و links the second predicate: پر آب روی = 'face full of water' (پر = 'full (of)', آب = 'water' used figuratively for tears, روی = 'face'), i.e. 'tears on his face'.
segments.s657-g663: به + مرغ is a simile 'like a bird'. آمد is the simple past 'he/it came'. از دشت marks the origin 'from the plain'. آوردگاه is the destination 'the gathering‑place/the battlefield' and follows as a goal. Word order places the simile and motion verb up front, then source and destination.
segments.s657-g664: فراز is an adverbial 'on high/from above'. آمدندش is a 3rd‑person plural past 'they came' with the enclitic -ش = 'to him' (they came to/at him). زهر سو is an adverbial phrase meaning roughly 'from all sides/toward him' (سو = 'toward/side'; زهر here is idiomatic/poetic in combination), and سپاه is the subject 'the host/army' which comes at the end. The verb+enclitic shows motion toward the person described.
segments.s657-g689: به پاسخ تو is a fronted prepositional phrase meaning 'in answer to you' (به + noun + possessive تو). با او درشتی مگوی is the main clause: درشتی (a noun 'rudeness') is used adverbially and مگوی is the negative imperative/prohibitive ('do not say' / 'do not be'); با او marks the person 'to/with him'. The poetic ending -ی on مگوی is an archaic/metrical form of the 2nd-person negative.
segments.s657-g690: بپیوند is a 2nd-person singular imperative meaning 'join' / 'make peace' (hortative 'be reconciled'). و simply links the two imperatives. آزرم here functions as a common noun 'honor/favor' (an older or rare word) and او را is the possessor/object construction ('his ...'); بجوی is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'seek' (seek X). Word order places the object phrase (آزرم او را) before the verb, a common poetic/orderly inversion.
segments.s657-g691: اگر introduces a conditional protasis ('if'). جنگ سازد uses سازد (3rd-person present/subjunctive 'he makes/sets up') to mean 'wages war'. بسازیم جنگ is the apodosis with بسازیم (1st-person plural subjunctive/hortative 'let us make/prepare'); the repeated جنگ echoes and emphasizes the action. The verb forms here express a prospective or hortatory sense rather than simple present.
segments.s657-g692: که introduces an explanatory/casual clause ('for/because'). او با شتابست is a predicative phrase where با + noun (شتاب) + copula -ست yields 'he is hasty'; this is an older compressed form (شتابست). و ما با درنگ parallels it: ما with درنگ ('delay/slowness') describes the speaker's side. The contrast is explicit: his haste vs. our deliberation.
segments.s657-g693: سپهبد is the subject ('the spahbed'). فرستاده را is the direct object marked by را. پیش خواند is a compound verb meaning 'called/summoned in (someone's) presence' — پیش ('in the presence of') combines with خواند (here 'called/summoned') to form the action; خواند is past 3rd-person.
segments.s657-g694: به خوبی فراوان is an adverbial phrase 'with much goodness / courteously and abundantly' modifying the verb. سخنها (plural 'words/speeches') is the object and براند (3rd-person past) is used metaphorically 'he put forth/uttered' (r. راندن 'to drive/drive forth'); the verb here conveys 'uttered many words' in a poetic register.
segments.s657-g695: بدوگفت is a compact past tense phrase 'he said to him' (بـ + او + گفت). رو here is an imperative interjection 'go' (classical usage) or a directional adverb telling the messenger to go; با برادر بگوی is an instruction 'tell/say to your brother' where با + noun marks the addressee and بگوی is the imperative 'say/tell' (poetic/brief form). Word order places the injunction before the content clause introduced by که.
segments.s657-g696: که introduces the content of what to tell him ('that'). چندین is an indefinite numeral/adverb 'many'/'several' intensifying the following nouns. درشتی و تندی are coordinated nouns 'rudeness and harshness' (تندی = fierceness/harshness here). مجوی is the 2nd-person negative imperative 'do not seek' (prohibitive), addressing the brother.
segments.s657-g833: بیامد is a simple past third‑person verb 'he came'. چوطلخند here appears as the direct object/name (classical Persian sometimes preserves proper names with no additional marking). را is the definite/object marker attaching to that name, and مرده دید is a past‑tense verb phrase 'saw (him) dead' where دید is the main verb and مرده is a predicate adjective/noun describing the object.
segments.s657-g834: دل لشکر is a genitival/possessive phrase 'the heart of the army' (دل = heart + لشکر = army). از marks cause/reason ('from/because of'), درد is the cause 'grief/pain', پژمرده is a predicative adjective 'withered/ashen', and دید is the past verb 'he saw' — together: 'he saw the army's heart withered from grief.'
segments.s657-g835: سراپای او is a fixed phrase 'his whole body / from head to foot' (سراپا head‑to‑toe + او 'him'). سر به سر is an adverbial intensifier 'all over / completely', and بنگرید is the past form of the verb 'to look/gaze' (classical third‑person past), so the clause literally: 'he looked him all over from head to foot.'
segments.s657-g836: به جایی here means 'in any place / at any spot'; the token برو in this classical/poetic context functions idiomatically (not a literal imperative 'go') as part of a negative locution linked to ندید ('did not see'), i.e. 'nowhere.' پوست خسته is a nominal phrase 'weary/tired skin', and ندید is past 'he did not see' — together: 'he saw nowhere even a patch of weary skin.'
segments.s657-g837: خروشان is an adverb/participle 'roaring/tumultuous' modifying the action; همه گوشت بازو is the object 'all the flesh of the arm' (همه 'all' + گوشت 'flesh' + بازو 'arm'). بکند is the past verb (classical form) indicating the action 'they tore off / removed' (a causative/active past). Word order is typical poetic Persian: circumstantial adverbial + object + verb.
segments.s657-g838: نشست here is past 'they sat down' (or 'he/they settled'), از marks cause or relation 'because of / at', برش functions as a brief locative/relational noun 'at his side / in regard to him' in this context, and سوگوار و نژند are coordinate adjectives 'mourning and dejected/base' describing their state. The clause describes the actors' reaction: they sat down mournful and abased.
segments.s657-g839: همی‌گفت is an iterative past 'kept saying' (habitual past). زار‌ای is an exclamatory lament ('alas, woe!'), and نبرده جوان is a terse classical phrase meaning 'the youth was not spared/taken alive' or broadly 'the youth has been lost' — here the speaker addresses or laments the young man. The clause is elliptical and emotive, typical of elegiac speech.
segments.s657-g840: برفتی is an archaic second‑person past 'you went / you departed' (used here in direct address). پر از is the adjectival phrase 'full of' governed by از 'of', درد is 'pain/grief', و links, خسته is 'weary', and روان here is used poetically for 'soul/spirit' — 'you departed full of pain and weary in spirit.'
segments.s657-g1001: پیاده (noun) appears first and functions as the direct object 'infantry/foot soldiers'; بدید is the simple past 'saw' with an omitted subject (understood from context). اندرو is a fused adverbial phrase (اندر + او) meaning 'there/in it' and follows the verb for poetic word order. با سوار is a prepositional phrase 'with horsemen' (سوار = rider/horseman) appended to show accompaniment.
segments.s657-g1002: همه here means 'all' or 'together' and scopes over the action; کرده is the perfective/past participle of كردن 'to make/do' meaning 'had made' or 'arranged'. آرایش کارزار is a noun phrase 'the array/arraying of battle' (آرایش = array/arrangement, کارزار = battle), the direct object of کرده. Poetic compression omits a copula or subject explicitness.
segments.s657-g1003: ز is the poetic preposition 'by/from' introducing an instrumental/causal list; اسبان 'horses' and پیلان 'elephants' are coordinated by و. دستور شاه is a genitival phrase 'the king's command/order' appended to the list. The whole phrase names the means/forces or causes (horses, elephants, the king's order).
segments.s657-g1004: مبارز is a noun 'fighter/warrior' being further specified by the relative clause introduced by که ('who/that'). اسب افگند is the verb phrase 'threw/cast (his) horse' (اسب = horse; افگند = past of افکندن 'to throw'), and بر سپاه is the directional/prepositional complement 'upon the army/host'. The relative clause follows the head noun in typical Persian order.
segments.s657-g1005: همه کرده is again 'they all made/did' with کرده as the perfective verb. پیکر here means 'body' or 'mass' and denotes the troops' physical formation; به آیین جنگ is an adverbial phrase 'according to the manner/ritual of war' (آیین = manner/custom, جنگ = war) marking the standard or method according to which they formed.
segments.s657-g1006: This is a balanced descriptive phrase: یکی ... یکی ... ('one ... another ...'). تیز و جنبّان are adjectives used nominally/adverbially meaning 'sharp/fast and moving' (تیز = swift, وجنبان = moving/active) describing one party, while با درنگ 'with delay/hesitation' describes the other. The verb is omitted for brevity—the pattern contrasts modes of movement.
segments.s657-g1007: بیاراسته is an adjectival (literally 'unadorned' or 'plainly dressed') placed before شاه to modify it: 'the unadorned/plain king'. قلب سپاه is a possessive/genitival phrase 'the heart (core) of the army' apposed to the king. The copula is omitted (typical in Persian poetry), so the sequence presents apposition/identification.
segments.s657-g1008: ز here functions agentively/instrumentally 'by/from'; یک دست is 'one hand' (دست as literal 'hand'), and فرزانه نیک‌خواه are two adjectives in sequence modifying the (implied) agent—'wise' and 'well-wishing/benevolent'. The phrase means something like 'by the single hand of a wise, well-wishing (man)', with ز introducing the agent or means.
segments.s657-g1033: This is an adverbial + verb + locative phrase. ازان پس (an archaic compound meaning “thereupon/after that”) sets the time; ببستند is perfect/past tense, 3rd-person plural (“they closed/blocked”); بر شاه‌راه is a prepositional phrase (بر + شاه‌راه) giving the place affected. Word order places the time-marker before the verb in a poetic inversion.
segments.s657-g1034: A coordinated list of nouns (items placed across the road) conjoined by the coordinating particle و. There is no new verb here; this noun series completes the action of ببستند by specifying what they put/placed on the highway. The list is simple enumerative syntax common in epic Persian.
segments.s657-g1035: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction: noun نگه (“gaze”) + the past of کردن (کرد) functioning as ‘looked’ or ‘fixed his gaze.’ شاه is the (implied) subject; the verb precedes the subject here (poetic inversion). اندران چارسوی is an adverbial phrase: اندران (“among them/therein”) + چارسوی (“the four directions/everywhere”), specifying where/around he looked.
segments.s657-g1036: This clause continues the observation: سپه is the object being perceived; دید is the simple past ‘saw’; افگنده is a participle meaning ‘cast/imbued’ and heads a small noun phrase افگنده چین در بروی describing the army (i.e. ‘with folds/wrinkles cast on their faces’). در is the locative preposition and بروی (poetic contraction for بر روی) marks the surface ‘on (their) face(s).’
segments.s657-g1037: ز repeated here is an ablative/instrumental marker (poetic flavor) listing instruments/causes: ز اسب ‘by/with horses’ and ز کنده ‘by/with uprooted trunks’. The sequence بر و بسته راه mixes a locative/position marker (بر ‘on’) with بسته (‘closed/blocked’) modifying راه (‘the way/road’): overall this unit lists what blocks the road. The clause is elliptical and coordinated, typical of poetic enumeration.
segments.s657-g1038: A string of directional adverbs (چپ و راست و پیش و پس = left and right and front and back) followed by اندر سپاه, a locative phrase meaning ‘within the army/host.’ The directions specify the positions relative to the host; اندر is the locative preposition ‘in/among.’
segments.s657-g1039: شد is the past/perfect of شدن ‘became’; it governs the result-state that follows. از رنج و ز تشنگی is a compound ablative giving causes (’from suffering and from thirst’), where وز here functions in a contracted/poetic sense linking the two causes (equivalent to و + ز). شاه مات places the subject (شاه) and the predicate adjective مات (‘stunned/overwhelmed’): literally ‘the king became stunned from suffering and thirst.’
segments.s657-g1040: چنین یافت is a short clause meaning ‘thus he found/received/experienced’; یافت is past of یافتن used here in the sense ‘to receive/meet with.’ از چرخ گردان is a genitival/ablative phrase ‘from the turning wheel’ — a standard poetic image for fate/the wheel of fortune, where گردان is adjectival ‘turning/revolving.’ برات is the object here meaning ‘a safe-conduct/respite’ (a formal protection or relief). Overall: ‘thus he obtained from the turning wheel a safe-conduct.’
segments.s657-g737: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense verb meaning 'he ordered/said' (archaic literary form). تا introduces a subordinate clause of result/purpose ('so that' / 'that'), and پیش او is the prepositional phrase 'before him / in his presence'. خواندند is a past plural verb meaning 'they called/summoned' (here transitive: they caused someone to come before him); Persian allows the object to be omitted when context makes it clear.
segments.s657-g738: سزاوار functions as an adjective 'fit/appropriate' modifying جای ('place/seat'). هر is the universal quantifier 'every/each' and جای is 'place/seat'. بنشاندند is a past plural transitive verb 'they seated' (they caused [them] to sit); the implied object ('them' — the summoned people) is omitted because it's recoverable from context.
segments.s657-g739: همه here is adverbial/augmentative ('entirely/wholly') modifying the clause. پاسخ‌گو is a noun 'the one who answers/respondent' and functions as the subject. بدیشان is the indirect object 'to them', and بگفت is an older past-tense form 'he said / he told' (ب‑ prefix is common in literary Persian). Word order is subject – indirect object – verb.
segments.s657-g740: همه رازها is a quantified direct object 'all the secrets'. برگشاد is a verbal/adjectival past form meaning 'were opened / were revealed' (passive/intransitive sense). از نهفت is a prepositional phrase 'from concealment / that were hidden' (نهفت = 'hidden'); the whole segment reports that every previously hidden secret was disclosed.
segments.s657-g741: به لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'to the army' (به = 'to/for'), and چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' introducing reported speech. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (note: the provided lemma senses were nominal, so no exact match — the verb here is the 3rd-person past). کین جنگ نو is a noun phrase 'this vengeful/new war' (کین often has a poetic sense of enmity/vengeance; نو = 'new'). The phrase names the subject of the reported question.
segments.s657-g742: به دریا here means 'against/to the sea' (به = 'towards/into'), and که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'which/that'. اندیشه کردست is a perfect/archaic verbal phrase: اندیشه 'intention/plan' and کردست (کرده است) 'has done / has formed' — together 'has thought up/has intended'. گو at the end is an exhortative/quotative particle meaning 'say' / 'tell (us)' in this context, calling the army to speak.
segments.s657-g743: چه بینید is an interrogative 'what do you see/observe?' (چه = 'what'; بینید here addresses the addressees — 2nd-person plural present/imperative sense used interrogatively). واین را uses را as the definite-object marker ('and this [object]'), and چه رای آوریم is a parallel question 'what counsel/plan shall we bring/adopt?' — آوریم is first-person plural present/subjunctive ('shall we bring / should we adopt'). The two clauses are parallel interrogatives addressed to the assembly.
segments.s657-g744: که introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that / in order that'). اندیشه او is 'his intention/plan' (اندیشه = intent; او = 'his'). به جای آوریم is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'to put into place / to carry out' (به جای = 'in place / to fulfil' + آوریم = 'we bring about'); آوریم is again first-person plural subjunctive/optative expressing purpose.
segments.s657-g297: ببودند is an archaic past-plural verb meaning 'they were.' The temporal phrase یک شب ('one night') follows the verb as a time adverbial. پرآژنگ is a compound adjective 'full of ornament / resplendent' placed before the noun چهر ('face, appearance') — a poetic word order (adjective + noun) that here yields 'faces full of splendour.'
segments.s657-g298: بدانگه = 'at that moment / thereupon' introduces the subordinating clause; که is the relative/connector 'when/that.' برزد is an archaic verb form (here meaning something like 'rose/appeared/was uplifted') used with سر as its subject. سر is used in the sense 'summit/top' and از marks origin 'from'; کوه مهر = 'Mount Mehr' (مهر used as a place/name here). The clause therefore means 'at that moment when the summit rose from Mount Mehr.'
segments.s657-g299: برفتند is the past plural 'they went / departed.' The phrase یک سر بزرگان شهر functions as a nominal phrase meaning 'the foremost/head of the city's great ones' — سر here carries the sense 'chief/head' and بزرگان شهر = 'the grandees of the city.' Together they indicate the leading nobles set out.
segments.s657-g300: هرآنکس که is a relative construction 'whoever / any one who'; شان بود = 'had the rank/status' (شان = 'status, dignity', بود = past 'was'); زان کار بهر is a short prepositional phrase 'for that affair' (زان = 'from/for that', کار = 'work/affair', بهر = 'for/toward'). The whole segment reads 'whoever had the rank for that task.'
segments.s657-g301: پر + آواز شد is an idiomatic combination: پر here means 'full (of)' and آواز 'voice,' so پر آواز شد = 'became full of voices / resounded.' سندلی means 'seat' or 'chair' (here the council/seat). چار سوی literally 'four directions' = 'from every side,' with چار = 'four' and سوی = 'toward/side'; the noun order is poetic but conveys 'the seat became full of voices on all sides.'
segments.s657-g302: سخن رفت is an idiom meaning 'words passed / words were spoken' (رفت here with سخن conveys the happening of speech). هرگونه = 'of every kind / in any manner.' بی‌آرزوی is an adjective 'without desire / disinterested,' modifying the manner of the speech: 'speech of every kind, without self‑interest.'
segments.s657-g303: The phrase یکی را ز گردان uses را as a topical/definite marker: 'one (of them) from the troop.' بگو بود رای is a terse poetic phrasing meaning 'there was the counsel/opinion (that was said)' — بگو here functions as 'said/voiced,' بود is the past auxiliary 'was,' and رای = 'opinion/counsel.' So the segment says 'one of the troop voiced an opinion.'
segments.s657-g304: Another instance of یکی introduces a (second) opinion or speaker: 'one (of them) ...' سوی marks direction/target 'toward' (here indicating to whom or toward what the suggestion was aimed). طلخند is a word that can mean 'bitter/wry smile' but in context it reads like a name/target; بد rهنمای is a noun phrase 'a bad/evil guide' (بد modifying رهنمای). The verb is omitted (typical in poetry) and the segment gives the content of the counsel: '(one) toward Talakhund, (appoint) a bad guide.'
segments.s657-g913: جهاندار طلخند functions here as a compound personal name (a title or name made of two elements). بر زین is a prepositional phrase meaning "on the saddle" (بر = on/upon, زین = saddle) modifying the action that follows. بمرد is an archaic/perfect/optative-form of مَردَن used here to indicate that he died (classical usage often strings the b- prefix with past stems).
segments.s657-g914: سرگاه شاهی is a noun phrase meaning "the royal seat/seat of kingship" (سرگاه = seat/portico, شاهی = royal). بگو is ambiguous in isolation (normally 'say'), but in this syntax it functions as the recipient/agent element of the following verb; در is the preposition/adverbial "into/in", and سپرد is the past of سپردن "to entrust/hand over" — together meaning he handed over or committed the royal seat.
segments.s657-g915: همی is a classical particle marking continuous or habitual action (here giving an ongoing/intoned past action). جامه زد چاک literally "struck/tore the garment [open]" (زد = struck/struck at, چاک = a rent/gash), و connects the clauses, رخ را بکند = "he tore/rent his face" with را marking رخ as the definite object and بکند (an older form of کردن) meaning 'he did/caused' — overall describing self-mutilation/tearing of clothes and face.
segments.s657-g916: به introduces direction/goal here (towards/into). گنجور is the treasure-house/treasury (a place), گنج is 'treasure', آتش 'fire', اندر 'inside/within', and فگند (فَگَند) is a past form meaning "threw/cast". Together the clause says he cast the treasure into the fire inside the treasury.
segments.s657-g917: The word order is inverted: به ایوان او شد دمان مادرش literally "to his iwan came the hem of his mother." به + ایوان او marks the location (to/at his portico), شد here = 'came to be' or 'came', and دمان مادرش = 'his mother's hem/skirt' (مادرش = his mother, with the possessive ش). The clause presents the mother's garment/hem arriving at the portal.
segments.s657-g918: به خون اندرون غرقه گشته سرش is a participial description: به خون = 'in/with blood', اندرون = 'within/inside', غرقه گشته (غرقه + گشته) = 'having become drenched/immersed' (گشته as perfect participle), and سرش = 'her/his head'. Grammatically, the phrase modifies or describes the mother: her head was soaked in blood inside (i.e. her head within the house was drenched in blood).
segments.s657-g919: همه کاخ وتاج بزرگی is a coordinated NP: همه = all/the whole, کاخ = palace(s), و تاج = and the crown, بزرگی qualifying the crown (great). بسوخت is the past passive/active perfect meaning 'were burned' or 'burned up' (بسوخت = was burned). Together: the entire palace and the great crown burned.
segments.s657-g920: ازان پس is a temporal connector 'from that/thereafter', بلند آتشی = 'a tall/high fire' (بلند qualifying آتشی), and برفروخت here is a classical verb meaning 'kindled/started (a fire)' (برفروخت often used poetically for lighting or inflaming). The clause says that after that he lit a great flame.
segments.s657-g905: This is a subordinate clause introduced by که meaning 'that/when'. لشکر (subject) + گذر کرد (verb phrase) = 'the host passed'. زین روی کوه is a locative phrase 'on/at this face/side of the mountain' — زین is a poetic contraction of زِ این ('from/this'), روی = 'face/side', so the whole order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: 'that the host passed this side of the mountain.'
segments.s657-g906: گو وهرک here functions as a proper name or fixed designation (the phrase is presented as the name of a troop). بودند is the past plural copula 'were', and با او گروه literally 'a group with him' — the word order places the named group up front and then states that 'with him was the troop Go‑Vahrek.'
segments.s657-g907: The triple negation pattern نه ... نه ... sets up a 'neither ... nor ...' listing. طلخند پیدا = 'a grimace/sneer was visible' (طلخند = wry smile/grimace, پیدا = visible); then نه پیل و درفش = 'nor an elephant and a standard' (پیل = elephant, درفش = banner/standard).
segments.s657-g908: The final coordinated negative phrase: نه آن نامداران زرینه کفش = 'nor those renowned ones of golden shoes.' آن is a demonstrative 'that/those', نامداران = 'renowned ones', زرینه modifies کفش ('golden shoes'); the adjective/modifier follows the noun in this poetic noun phrase.
segments.s657-g909: ز (= 'from') + مژگان ('eyelashes') gives the source; فروریخت is a past verb 'fell/collapsed' used here with a liquid subject: خون مادرش ('his mother's blood') — poetic inversion places the verb phrase before the noun phrase, yielding 'from the eyelashes fell his mother's blood.'
segments.s657-g910: فراوان is an adverb 'abundantly' or 'copiously' modifying the action that follows. به دیوار بر زد سرش = 'he struck his head against the wall' — به دیوار indicates direction/target ('to/against the wall'), بر زد is a compound/phrased verb meaning 'struck against', and سرش is the possessed noun 'his head'. Word order places the adverbial and prepositional phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s657-g911: ازان پس = 'after that' (temporal adverbial). چوآمد = 'when it came/as soon as it arrived', and به مام آگهی = 'the news/information reached the nurse' — مام here is a dialectal/older term for the wet‑nurse or nurse in charge, and آگهی = 'news/information'. The clause as a whole sets a temporal frame: 'After that, when the news reached his nurse...'.
segments.s657-g912: که introduces the reported content 'that ...'. تیره شد = past 'became dark/bleak' (tireh = dark, شد = became). آن فر شاهنشهی is a nominal phrase 'that royal glory of kingship/empire' (فر = royal glory, شاهنشهی = of the realm/imperial); together they report that the royal radiance was eclipsed or darkened.
segments.s657-g961: بدو (to him) is the prepositional pronoun placed before the verb; گفت is the past verb 'said' and مادر is the subject that follows the verb (typical V–S order). که introduces reported speech (an object/clause) and بنمای is an imperative/optative form 'show' governing راه 'the way/method' as its object.
segments.s657-g962: The second که begins the subordinate clause giving the manner. چون here means 'how' (introducing manner). مرد is the human/agent 'a man'; بر is the preposition 'on/upon'; پیل is 'elephant' and طلخند is a descriptive noun 'bitter/wry smile' which is followed by شاه in genitive: 'the king's (scornful) smile'. The clause is elliptic (the verb is implied from the previous imperative 'show').
segments.s657-g963: مگر here introduces a wish/conditional meaning 'if only' or 'unless' ('if not'); بر من 'to me' marks the experiencer; این آشکارا شود is a clause with آشکارا 'clear/openly' and شود (subjunctive/optative 'become / may be') expressing a wished-for or potential state.
segments.s657-g964: پر آتش is a compound modifier 'full of fire' (a poetic phrase meaning 'fiery' or passionate) modifying دل 'heart'. The enclitic م is the possessive '-my' attached to دل (here tokenized as a separate item), and پرمدارا is an adjectival predicate 'very lenient / magnanimous'. شود again is the subjunctive/optative 'would become' or 'may become', giving the wished-for change in the speaker's heart.
segments.s657-g965: This clause is elliptical/poetic: پر از در شد is an idiomatic/periphrastic motion 'burst forth from the door' (پر here is adverbial/poetic), with از + در marking motion out of the doorway and شد the past intransitive 'became/went' used to indicate the action. گو bounces as a short discourse marker (related to 'said') and بایوان خویش is a possessive phrase 'his bāyvān / hall' (بایوان is an archaic/uncertain form and خویش is 'his own', split here into خوی + ش).
segments.s657-g966: جهاندیده فرزانه is the object phrase 'the worldly-wise, discerning sage'; را is the definite/object marker attached to that noun phrase; خواند is the past tense verb here meaning 'he called / summoned' (sense 2) and پیش 'before / in the presence of' indicates the direction/location of the summons.
segments.s657-g967: بگفت is the past 'he said / told'. آنچ is a relative/indefinite 'that which / what' introducing the content of his speech. با مادرش 'with his mother' uses با 'with' plus مادر + possessive ش 'his', and رفته بود is the past perfect/periphrastic form 'had gone' (past participle + بود) describing the prior action he is reporting.
segments.s657-g968: ز مادر here functions like 'concerning / about the mother' (ز is the poetic variant of از). که introduces the relative clause describing that mother: برآتش آشفته بود — this is a compact clause meaning 'she was distressed / upset because of the fire'; the internal wording is slightly compressed/archaising (برآتش appears as a fused form referring to 'on/about the fire'). آشفته بود is the past 'was disturbed/distraught'.
segments.s657-g625: 'ز' here introduces the cause/trigger (poetic 'because of / from'). 'رخشنده پیکان' places the adjective before the noun (رخشنده modifies پیکان = 'gleaming arrows'). 'و پر عقاب' connects a second noun phrase with و: پر (feather/plume) governed by عقاب (eagle). The whole phrase names what causes the following action.
segments.s657-g626: همی is a classical/perfective-continuous particle here indicating ongoing or habitual action ('kept / repeatedly'). دامن اندر کشید is a verbal phrase: دامن (hem/robe) + اندر (inward) + کشید (past tense 'drew/pulled'). Note the subject آفتاب follows the verb (poetic inversion): 'drew in the robe, the sun' = 'the sun drew its robe in.'
segments.s657-g627: زمین شد is 'the earth became' (شد = past of شدن 'become'). به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner/like' (به + کردار 'in the semblance/mode of'). دریای خون is a genitive construction 'sea of blood' that completes the simile.
segments.s657-g628: در is the preposition 'in'. ودشت here functions as a poetic noun phrase referring to 'the plains/fields' (written fused in the manuscript). بد is an adverb/adjective giving a negative/strong quality ('badly' / 'severely'); زیرخون is a compound meaning 'under blood' i.e. 'blood-soaked/besmeared', and اندرون 'inside/within'. Together they describe the plains and fields being deep within covered by blood.
segments.s657-g629: دو پیل ژیان places the numeral + noun + modifier: دو (two) + پیل (elephants) + ژیان (literally 'living'/'lively' here used as an epithet 'mighty/living' for the elephants). شاهزاده دو شاه lists persons associated with the action (a prince and two kings) in apposition; classical Persian often strings titles/nouns like this for dramatic effect.
segments.s657-g630: براندند is a past plural verb 'they drove away / expelled'. هر دو 'both' specifies that two entities are meant. ز is the preposition 'from'. قلب سپاه is a genitive phrase 'the heart/core of the army' (قلب = core, سپاه = host/army). The subject is left implicit or picked up from the previous noun phrase; the verb precedes its explicit objects/adjuncts as is common in poetry.
segments.s657-g631: برآمد خروشی 'a shout/roar arose' — برآمد (arose/issued forth) + خروشی (a roar/clamor). ز introduces the source 'from' (ز طلخند). طلخند is used here as a proper name or a martial nickname in the narrative (though the lemma list also gives 'bitter laugh' as a lexical meaning); وگو is the poetic/simplified past of گفتن 'said' (literally 'and said' or 'he said').
segments.s657-g632: که introduces reported speech ('and he said:'). از is 'from / because of' and باد here means 'blast / wind' (figurative: the gust caused by a blow). ژوپین is the padded jacket/gambeson (armor); من is the possessive 'my / of me'. دور شو is an imperative: دور (away) + شو (imperative of شدن 'become' used here as 'go / stand away'). The whole is an imperative warning: 'Stand away from the blast of my gambeson.'
segments.s657-g193: مریزید is a negative imperative (2nd person plural) meaning 'do not spill/ shed' and is placed before its object (خون). از پی is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning 'in pursuit of' or 'for the sake of' and takes the goals تاج و گنج as its complement; the verb prohibits the action for those aims.
segments.s657-g194: که here introduces the reason/explanation ('for/because'); نماند is the past/future-oriented negative of ماندن ('will not remain / be left'). سرای سپنج appears as a nominal phrase (سرای = 'house/abode' + سپنج a name or noun), so the clause gives the reason why one shouldn't shed blood.
segments.s657-g195: ز مادر 'from his mother' (poetic prepositional order) followed by چو + past verb بشنید: چو here is the temporal 'when' and بشنید is 3rd-person past 'he heard.' طلخند پند is a compound—طلخند ('bitter laugh' / wry smile) modifies پند ('advice, admonition'), together specifying what he heard.
segments.s657-g196: نیامدش is an impersonal/experiential construction: نیامد + the enclitic -ش 'to him/her', here functioning idiomatically as 'did not seem / did not come (to him)' or 'was not fitting/useful to him.' گفتار او 'her speech/words' is the subject of this evaluative predicate, and سودمند is the predicate adjective 'useful.'
segments.s657-g197: به مادر 'to his mother' marks the indirect object/recipient; چنین گفت is a set phrase 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so'). کز introduces the content/reason clause ('that/because'), and مهتری here refers to preeminence/seniority — the basis for what he is about to claim.
segments.s657-g198: همی indicates habitual/continuous aspect ('always/continually'). از پی is an idiomatic prepositional phrase ('on account of / by virtue of' or loosely 'because of'); گو کنی is a verb phrase where گو (say) combines with the light verb کنی ('you do') to mean 'you make/express by saying' or 'you decide by saying', and داوری is the noun 'judgment' that is the action being done.
segments.s657-g199: به سال is an adverbial phrase 'in years / with respect to age' (به used adverbially), ار is the conditional 'if', برادر is subject 'a brother', ز من 'than me' marks the comparative object, and مهترست is the predicate 'is greater/older' (archaic/poetic third-person copula-stem attached to مهتر).
segments.s657-g200: نه negates the whole clause. هرکس که 'whoever/anyone who' is a relative noun phrase; او مهتر 'he is older/elder' is the subordinate descriptive clause, and او بهترست is the main predication 'he is better' (بهترست = 'is better'), so the sentence denies that age automatically implies superiority.
